content: Let $m_1$ and $m_2$ denote the sums of the middle row and middle column, respectively, of a $3\times 3$ square array of numbers. Let $c_1$ and $c_2$ denote the sums of the main diagonal and secondary diagonal, respectively. Let $S$ denote the sum of all nine elements, and let $x$ denote the center element. Then $m_1+m_2+c_1+c_2=S+3x$.

Suppose that the sum of each row and each column of a $3\times 3$ square equals $r$. In the notation of Entry 2(i), the center element is $x=\dfrac{c_1+c_2-r}{2}$. If the square is magic, then $x=r/3$ and $r$ is a multiple of $3$.

In a $3\times 3$ magic square with common row, column, and diagonal sum $r$, the three elements in the middle row, the middle column, and each diagonal are in arithmetic progression (with middle term $r/3$).

Construct $3\times 3$ magic squares with (i) $r=15$ and all entries odd, and (ii) $r=27$ and all entries odd. Solutions: $\begin{smallmatrix}6&1&8\\7&5&3\\2&9&4\end{smallmatrix}$ and $\begin{smallmatrix}15&1&11\\7&17&3\\13&5&9\end{smallmatrix}$.

Construct $3\times 3$ magic squares with (i) $r=36$ and all entries even, and (ii) $r=63$ with all entries divisible by $3$. Solutions: $\begin{smallmatrix}14&4&18\\12&12&12\\16&20&10\end{smallmatrix}$ and $\begin{smallmatrix}24&9&30\\21&21&21\\27&33&3\end{smallmatrix}$.
Construct a $3\times 3$ square with all row and column sums equal to $19$ but with only one diagonal sum equal to $19$. Solution: $\begin{smallmatrix}10&2&7\\4&6&9\\5&11&3\end{smallmatrix}$.

Construct a $3\times 3$ square with all row and column sums equal to $31$ but with only one diagonal sum equal to $31$ (Ramanujan also asks that all entries be odd). Solution: $\begin{smallmatrix}14&5&12\\7&11&13\\10&15&6\end{smallmatrix}$.
Construct a $3\times 3$ square with all row and column sums equal to $20$ and diagonal sums $16$ and $19$. Solution: $\begin{smallmatrix}10&2&8\\4&5&11\\6&13&1\end{smallmatrix}$.
Construct a square with diagonal sums $15$ and $19$, column sums $16$, $17$, $12$, and row sums $6$, $21$, $18$. Solution: $\begin{smallmatrix}1&2&3\\8&9&4\\7&6&5\end{smallmatrix}$.
Construct $3\times 4$ rectangles whose average row sum and average column sum equal (i) $8$, and (ii) $15$ with all entries odd.
In a $4\times 4$ square, the sum of the middle four elements equals $\tfrac12\bigl(\text{(sum of diagonals)}+\text{(sum of middle rows)}+\text{(sum of middle columns)}-\text{(total sum)}\bigr)$.

Construct a $4\times 4$ magic square by letting $S_1=\{A,B,C,D\}$ and $S_2=\{P,Q,R,S\}$ and placing the nine sums $A+P$, $B+Q$, etc., in a $4\times 4$ array so that each letter from $S_1$ and $S_2$ appears exactly once in each row, column, and diagonal.
Construct $4\times 4$ magic squares with common sum $34$ (Ramanujan gives three examples with sums $34$, $34$, and $35$).
Construct two $4\times 4$ magic squares with common sum $66$.
Construct two $3\times 3$ magic squares with common sum $60$.
Construct $5\times 5$ magic squares with common sums $65$ and $66$.
Construct $7\times 7$ magic squares with common sums $170$ and $175$.

For each positive integer $n$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{n+k}=\frac{1}{2n+1}+\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{(2k)^3-2k}=\varphi(2,n)$, where $\displaystyle\varphi(a,n)=1+2\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{(ak)^3-ak}$ and $\varphi(a)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\varphi(a,n)$.

Let $k$ and $n$ be nonnegative integers and define $A_k=3^k(n+\tfrac13)-\tfrac13$, with $A_{-1}=0$. If $r$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{j=n+1}^{A_r}\frac{1}{j}=r+2\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}(r-k)\sum_{j=A_{k-1}+1}^{A_k}\frac{1}{(3j)^3-3j}$.
Let $H=\sum_{k=1}^{1000}\dfrac{1}{k}$. Ramanujan asserts that $H$ equals $7$ very nearly.
Let $n>0$ and let $r$ be a natural number. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}\arctan\frac{2}{(n+2k+1)^2}=\arctan\frac{2r}{n^2+2nr+1}$.

Let $f$ be an entire function with zeros $z_1,z_2,\ldots$ such that $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}1/|z_k|$ converges. Then $f(z)=e^{a+bz}z^m\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}(1-z/z_k)\,e^{z/z_k}$, a special case of the Hadamard factorization theorem.

Let $x$ and $a$ be complex with $a$ not an integral multiple of $\pi$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{\sin(x+a)}{\sin a}=e^{x\cot a}\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\left(1-\frac{x}{k\pi-a}\right)\left(1+\frac{x}{k\pi+a}\right)$.

Let $x$ and $a$ be complex with $a$ not an odd multiple of $\pi/2$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{\cos(x+a)}{\cos a}=\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\left(1-\frac{x}{(k-\tfrac12)\pi-a}\right)\left(1+\frac{x}{(k-\tfrac12)\pi+a}\right)$.

Let $x$ and $a$ be complex with $a$ not an integral multiple of $\pi$. Then $\displaystyle 1+\frac{x}{a}+\frac{\sin x}{\sin a}=e^{x\cot a}\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\left(1-\frac{x}{2k\pi-a}\right)\left(1+\frac{x}{2k\pi+a}\right)$\times\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\left(1-\frac{x}{(2k-1)\pi+a}\right)\left(1+\frac{x}{(2k-1)\pi-a}\right)$.
Let $x$ and $a$ be real. Then $\displaystyle\arctan\frac{x}{a}+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\left(\arctan\frac{x}{k\pi-a}-\arctan\frac{x}{k\pi+a}\right)=\arctan(\tanh x\cot a)$.
Let $x$ and $a$ be real. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^k\arctan\frac{x}{k\pi+a}=\arctan(\sinh x\csc a)$.

Let $P_k$ be defined by $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}A_k z^k=1/\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}P_k z^k$ with $P_0=1$. If $\lim_{n\to\infty}P_n/P_{n+1}=L$ exists and all roots of $\sum A_k z^k=0$ other than $z_0$ of smallest modulus satisfy $|z|>|L|$, then $z_0=L$ is the root of smallest modulus.
For $x+x^2=1$, Ramanujan gives convergents to the root of smallest modulus $(\sqrt5-1)/2$.
For $x+x^2+x^3=1$, Ramanujan gives convergents to the real root of smallest modulus.
For $x+x^3=1$, Ramanujan gives convergents to the real root of smallest modulus.
For $2x+x^2+x^3=1$, Ramanujan gives convergents to the real root of smallest modulus.
For $e^{-x}=x$, Ramanujan gives convergents to the positive root.

Let $f(z)$ be analytic on $|z|<R_1$ with $R_1>1$, and let $g(z)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty Q_k z^k$ be analytic on $|z|<R_2$ with $R_2>0$. Define $P_k$, $0\le k<\infty$, by $\sum_{k=0}^\infty P_k z^k=e^{zg(z)}$ for $|z|<R_2$. Suppose $\sum_{j=0}^\infty Q_j\sum_{k=0}^\infty f^{(j+k)}(0)/(j!k!)$ converges and this repeated summation may be replaced by a diagonal summation $j+k=n$, $0\le n<\infty$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty P_n f^{(n)}(0)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty Q_n f^{(n)}(1)$.

Suppose the hypotheses of Entry 1 hold for $f(z)=(1+xz)^n$ with $|x|<1$ and $n$ arbitrary. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty P_k\frac{x^k}{\Gamma(n-k+1)}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty Q_k\frac{x^k(1+x)^{n-k}}{\Gamma(n-k+1)}$.

For all complex $x$ and $z$, define $\displaystyle\varphi(z)=e^x\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k-1}x^k}{z(z+1)\cdots(z+k-1)}$, where $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{x^k}{(z+k-1)(k-1)!}$ defines the auxiliary function $q_1(z)$.
Let $f$ satisfy the hypotheses of Entry 1. Then for all $z$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{f^{(k)}(0)}{(z+k)k!}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k f^{(k)}(1)}{z(z+1)\cdots(z+k)}$.

For each complex $x$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{k}\right)\frac{x^k}{k!}=e^x\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k-1}x^k}{(z+k)(k-1)!}$ (obtained from Entry 2 by $x\to -x$, $z\to z+1$, differentiating, and setting $z=0$).

Let $\varphi$ and $f_n$ be defined by (3.1) and (3.3), respectively. As $z\to\infty$ in the region $R_e=\{z:-\pi+\delta\le\arg z\le\pi-\delta\}$, $\delta>0$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^n\frac{(-1)^k f_k(x)}{z^{k+1}}=\sum_{k=1}^{n+1}\frac{(-1)^{k-1}x^k}{(z+1)(z+2)\cdots(z+k)}+O(z^{-n-2})$.
Let $a$ and $x$ be arbitrary complex numbers. Then $\displaystyle\Gamma(e^a-1)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{a^n}{n!}\,f_{n-1}(x)$, where $f_{-1}(x)=1$ and $f_n$ is defined by (3.3).
For each nonnegative integer $n$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^n\binom{n}{k}f_k(x)=f_{n+1}(x)$, where $f_n$ is defined by (3.3).
Let $n$ be a nonnegative integer and $r$ a positive integer with $r\le n+1$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}\frac{1}{k!}\binom{n}{k}r^{n-k}=\frac{r^n}{(r-1)!}$.
Let $r$ and $n$ be nonnegative integers with $r\le n$. Then $\displaystyle r!\,\varphi_{r+1}(n)=\sum_{k=0}^r(-1)^k\binom{r+1}{k}(r+1-k)^n$, where $\varphi_k(n)$ are the coefficients in (6.1).
Let $n$ and $r$ be integers with $1\le r\le n+1$. Then $\varphi_r(n+1)=r\,\varphi_r(n)+\varphi_{r-1}(n)$, where $\varphi_0(n)=0$.

Let $\varphi_1(n),\ldots,\varphi_{n+1}(n)$ be defined by (6.1). Let $\{a_k\}$, $1\le k<\infty$, be any sequence of complex numbers such that $\displaystyle\psi(z)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k-1}a_k}{(z+1)(z+2)\cdots(z+k)}$ has abscissa of convergence $A<\infty$. Define $F(j)=\sum_{k=1}^j a_k\varphi_k(j-1)$. Let $R_E$ be as in (3.2) if $A=-\infty$, and if $A$ is finite let $R_E=\{z:-\tfrac12\pi+\beta\le\arg(z-A)\le\tfrac12\pi-\beta\}$, $\beta>0$. Then as $z\to\infty$ in $R_E$, $\displaystyle\psi(z)\sim\sum_{k=0}^n\frac{(-1)^k F(k)}{z^{k+1}}$.
Let $r$ and $n$ be integers with $0\le r\le n$. Then $r!\,\varphi_{r+1}(n)$ is the coefficient of $x^n/n!$ in the Maclaurin series of $e^x(e^x-1)^r$.
Let $n$ be a positive integer. Then $\displaystyle f_{n-1}(x)=\sum_{k=0}^n\binom{n}{k}f_{k-1}(x)$.

Let $n\ge -1$ be an integer. Then $\displaystyle\int_0^x f_n(t)\,dt=\sum_{k=0}^n\frac{n!}{(k+1)!}\,B_{n+1-k}\,\varphi_k(x)$, where $B_m$ are Bernoulli numbers in Berndt's convention (11). (Ramanujan's original statement used $n$ in place of $n+1$ on the right except in the subscripts.)

For each nonnegative integer $n$, define $A_n$ by $\displaystyle e^{A_n}=\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{k^n}{(k-1)!}=e^{f_n(1)}$. Then $A_0=1$, $A_1=2$, $A_2=5$, $A_3=15$, $A_4=52$, $A_5=203$, $A_6=877$, $A_7=4140$, $A_8=21147$ (Bell numbers $B(n)=A_{n-1}$, $n\ge 1$).
For each nonnegative integer $n$, define $C_n=\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{(-1)^k k^n}{e\,k!}$. Then $C_0=-1$, $C_1=0$, $C_2=C_3=1$, $C_4=-2$, $C_5=C_6=-9$, $C_7=50$, $C_8=267$.

As $z\to\infty$ in $R_e$ (defined by (3.2)), $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^n\frac{(-1)^k F_k(x)}{z^{k+1}}=\sum_{k=1}^{n+1}\frac{(-1)^{k-1}x^k}{(z+a+b)(z+a+2b)\cdots(z+a+kb)}+O(z^{-n-2})$, where $F_n(a,b;x)$ is defined by (9.1).
Suppose $r$ and $n$ are integers with $0<r\le n+1$. Then $\displaystyle r!\sum_{k=0}^r\varphi_{r-k}(n)=\frac{(a+br)^n}{(r-1)!}$.
Let $r$ and $n$ be as in Entry 9(iv). Then $\displaystyle(r-1)!\,\varphi_r(n)=\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}(-1)^k\binom{r-1}{k}\{a+(r-k)b\}^n$.
Let $n$ and $r$ be integers with $1\le r\le n+1$. Then $\varphi_r(n+1)=(a+br)\varphi_r(n)+b\,\varphi_{r-1}(n)$, where $\varphi_0(n)=0$.
Let $r$ and $n$ be integers with $0\le r\le n$. Then $r!\,\varphi_{r+1}(n)$ is the coefficient of $x^n/n!$ in the Maclaurin series of $e^{(a+b)x}(e^{bx}-1)^r$.
$\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k(2k+1)^6}{k!\,k}=\frac{F_4(-1,2;-1)}{e}=-\frac{41}{e}$ (in Ramanujan's second notebook either side should be multiplied by $-1$).
$\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k(2k+1)^4}{k!\,k}=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k}{k!\,k}-8$ (Berndt notes: in the second notebook replace $-4$ by $-8$ on the right).

Let $\varphi(x)$ have at most polynomial growth as real $x\to\infty$. Suppose there exist $A\ge 1$ and $G(x)$ of at most polynomial growth such that for each $m\ge 0$ and all large $x$, $\varphi^{(m)}(x)$ exists and $|\varphi^{(m)}(x)|\le G(x)(A/x)^m m!$. Define $\displaystyle\varphi_\infty(x)=\varphi(x)+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{x^k}{k!}\varphi^{(k)}(k)$ (the prime on the sum omits terms where $\varphi^{(k)}$ is undefined). Then for any fixed $M>0$, as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\varphi_\infty(x)=\varphi(x)+\sum_{k=2}^M\sum_{n=k}^{2k-2}\frac{b_{kn}x^{n-k+1}}{n!}\varphi^{(n)}(x)+O(G(x)x^{-M})$, where $b_{kk}=1$, $b_{kn}=0$ for $n<k$ or $n>2k-2$, and $b_{k+1,n+1}=nb_{k,n-1}+(n-k+1)b_{kn}$ for $k\le n\le 2k-1$.

Ramanujan asserts that $\displaystyle\log\sum_{k=0}^{100}\frac{(\log k)!}{k!}\approx 100\log\Bigl(\frac{\varphi(110)+\varphi(90)}{2}\Bigr)$ using $\varphi(100!)\approx\varphi(100)$ and a midpoint approximation.
Let $\psi(x)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty x^{1/k}$. As $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{x^k\psi(k)}{k!}=e^x\Bigl(\log x+\gamma+o(1/x)\Bigr)$, where $\gamma$ is Euler's constant.

Suppose $f(x)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty A_n x^n/n$ is analytic for $|x|<R$. Define $P_n$, $0\le n<\infty$, by $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty P_n x^n=\exp\{f(x)\}$ for $|x|<R$. Then for $n\ge 1$, $\displaystyle nP_n=\sum_{k=1}^n A_k P_{n-k}$.

Let $\{a_k\}$, $1\le k<\infty$, satisfy $\sum_{k=1}^\infty|a_k|<\infty$. Let $S_n=\sum_{k=1}^\infty a_k^n$ for positive integer $n$. For $n\ge 1$, let $P_n$ be the sum of all products of $n$ distinct terms from $\{a_k\}$, with $P_0=1$. Then $\displaystyle nP_n=\sum_{k=1}^n(-1)^{k-1}S_k P_{n-k}$ for $n\ge 1$.
For integral $r$ and complex $n$, with $F_r(n)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty(n+k)^{r+k}a^k/k!$ convergent as in (12.1), $\displaystyle F_{r+1}(n)=nF_r(n)+\frac{1}{a}F_{r+1}(n+1)$.

Let $p$ and $q$ be constants. The polynomials $f_0(x)=1$, $f_1(x)=x$, and $\displaystyle f_n(x)=\frac{1}{n!}\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\prod_{j=1}^{k-1}(x+np-jq)$ for $n\ge 2$ satisfy $\displaystyle f_n(x+y)=\sum_{k=0}^n f_k(x)f_{n-k}(y)$ (Gould polynomials).
Let $f(n)=nF_{-1}(n)$, where $F_{-1}$ is defined by (12.1). If $a$ is real with $|a|\ge e$, there exists a positive real $x$ with $x=a\log x$ such that $x^n=f(n)$ for every real $n$.
Let $z$ be complex and $w$ complex with $|e^w-1|\ge|w|$. Then $\displaystyle e^z=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{z(z+kw)^{k-1}e^{-kw}}{k!}$.
Let $\varphi$ be defined by (14.2) with $c_k(n)$ as in (14.1). If $x$ is a root of $aqx^p-x^q+1=0$, then $\varphi(n)=x^n$ for every real $n$.

Let $n$ be real with $|a|\le 1$. Then $\displaystyle(a+\sqrt{a^2+1})^n=1+na+\sum_{k=2}^\infty b_k(n)a^k$, where $b_k(n)=n^2(n^2-2^2)\cdots(n^2-(k-2)^2)$ if $k$ is even and $b_k(n)=n(n^2-1^2)\cdots(n^2-(k-2)^2)$ if $k$ is odd.

Define $u$, $0<u<1$, by $u-\log u=1+x^2/2$ where $x$ is real. Then $\displaystyle e^{-k(1+x^2/2)/u}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(k+1)^{k-1}}{k!}e^{-k(1+x^2/2)}$. For sufficiently small $x>0$, $\displaystyle u=\sum_{k=0}^\infty b_k x^k$ with $b_0=1$, $b_1=-1$, $b_2=1/3$, $b_3=-1/36$, $b_4=-1/270$, and higher $b_k$ from $\tfrac12 x^2=\sum_{i=2}^\infty(1-u)^i/i$.

Let $m$ be real and $0<n\le 2$. Then $\displaystyle 2^m=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{m\,\Gamma(m+kn)}{\Gamma(m+kn-k+1)^2\,n^k k!}$. The series converges for $0<n<\infty$ but equals $2^m$ only for $0<n\le 2$.

Let $a>0$ and $m,n,p$ be real and nonzero. Define $x>0$ by $(\log x)^m=ax^n$. Then for $|m/n|a^{-1/m}\ge e$, $\displaystyle x^p=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{m\Gamma(mp/n+k)}{\Gamma(mp/n+k-1+1)\,(ma^{-1/m}/n)^k k!}$.

Let $a$ be real with $|a|\le e$ and $x$ defined by $x=ae^{\pm x}$. Then $\displaystyle e^{-x}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(k+1)^{k-1}(-a)^k}{k!}$ (with $+$ on the exponent for $x=ae^x$ and $-$ for $x=ae^{-x}$).
Let $a>0$ with $|\log a|\le e$ and $x>0$ defined by $x\pm x=a$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{1}{x}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(k+1)^{k-1}(\pm\log a)^k}{k!}$.

Let $a$ be real and $x$ defined by $e^x\pm x=a$. If $a\le -1$, $\displaystyle e^x=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(k+1)^{k-1}(-a)^k}{k!}$; if $a\ge 1$, $\displaystyle e^x=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(k+1)^{k-1}e^{-ak}}{k!}$.
Let $x>0$ and $v=x^x$. For $|\log x|\le 1/e$, $\displaystyle v=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(k+1)^{k-1}(\log x)^k}{k!}$.
If $r$ is an integer and $n$ any complex number, with $\varphi_r(n)$ defined by $x^r\varphi_r(n)=F_r(n)$ and $x=a\log x$, then $n\varphi_r(n)=\varphi_{r+1}(n)-(\log x)\varphi_{r+1}(n+1)$.

Let $r$ and $t$ be integers with $1\le t\le r+2$. Then $\psi_t(r+1,n)=(n-1)\psi_t(r,n-1)+\psi_{t-1}(r+1,n)-\psi_{t-1}(r+1,n-1)$, where $\psi_t(r,n)=0$ unless $1\le t\le r+1$, and $\varphi_r(n)=\sum_{k=1}^{r+1}\psi_k(r,n)/(1-\log x)^{r+k}$.

Let $x$ be complex. If $-p<n<1$ where $p\approx 0.27846$ is the unique real root of $ye^{y+1}=1$, then $\displaystyle e^x=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(x/n)^k(x/n+k)^{k-1}}{k!}$. If $n>1$ and $0<m<1$ with $e^m/m=e^n/n$, then $\displaystyle 1-\frac{1}{m}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(yn+kn)^k}{e^{kn}k!}$.

For $r\ge 2$, with $A_k$ the coefficients in $x-a=\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k-1}A_k(h/a)^k/k!$ from $x^x=aa^h$ (so $A_1=n=1/(1+\log a)$), $\displaystyle\frac{A_r}{(r-1)!}=n(r-2)A_{r-1}+n\sum_{k=1}^{r-1}\frac{k}{k!}A_k A_{r-k}$.
Fix $a$, $0<a<e$. For real $h$, define $x>0$ by $x^x=a^{1/a}e^h$. Then for small $|h|$, $\displaystyle\frac{a}{x}=1-\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{A_k(ah)^k}{k!}$.

Let $F_0(x)=x$ and $F_{r+1}(x)=e^{F_r(x)}-1$ for each integer $r$ (1.1). For each integer $r$, differentiating $F_{-1}(x)=\log(1+F_r(x))$ yields $\varphi'_{r+1}(x)=[1+F_{r+1}(x)]\varphi'_{-r-1}(x)$, where $F_r(x)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\varphi_k(r)x^k=\sum_{k=0}^\infty f_k(x)r^k$ as formal power series (2.1).
If $r$ is a positive integer, then $F'_r(x)=\prod_{k=1}^r\{1+F_k(x)\}$.
Let $r$ be any integer and let $n$ be a positive integer. Then $n\{\varphi_n(r)-\varphi_n(r-1)\}=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(n-k)\varphi_k(r)\varphi_{n-k}(r-1)$ (2.2).
For each real number $x$, $f'_0(x)=x+\sum_{n=1}^\infty B_n f_n(x)$, where $B_n$ are Bernoulli numbers and $f_n$ are defined by (1.2).
For each positive integer $n$, $f'_{n+1}(x)=f_1(x)f'_n(x)$, where $f_n$ is defined by (1.2) and (4.3).

If $n$ and $r$ are positive integers, then (i) $\displaystyle n\psi_n(n)=\sum_{k=1}^n(n+k-1)\psi_k(n-1)\psi_{n-k+1}(1)$, and (ii) $\displaystyle\varphi_n(2r)=\sum_{k=1}^n(-1)^{k-1}r^{n-k}\psi_k(n-k)$, where $\displaystyle f_n(x)=\frac{x}{n!}\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k-1}\psi_k(n)x^k$ (4.3).

We have $\psi_1(1)=1$, and for $n\ge 2$, $\displaystyle(n+1)\psi_n(1)=n\psi_{n-1}(1)+\sum_{k=1}^N B_{2k}2^{1-2k}\psi_{n-2k}(2k)$, where $N$ denotes $(n-1)/2$ or $(n-2)/2$ according as $n$ is odd or even.
Let $y=x/(1-rx)$ and $z=1-rx$. Then $F_{2r}(x)=y+\frac{x^2}{6}\log z+\frac{x^3}{72}\{\log^2 z+(1-\log z)^2-z\}+\cdots$ (7.1).

Let $H_n=\sum_{k=1}^n 1/k$. If $x$ is a positive integer, then (i) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x H_k=(x+1)H_x-x$; (ii) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x H_k^2=(x+1)H_x^2-(2x+1)H_x+2x$; and (iii) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x H_k^3=(x+1)H_x^3-\tfrac12(2x+1)H_x^2+\tfrac13(2x+1)H_x-6x+\tfrac12\pi^2$.

Let $\varphi(z)$ be entire. Suppose there exists a sequence $r_n$, $1\le n<\infty$, of positive numbers tending to $\infty$ such that $\displaystyle I_n=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{|z|=r_n}\frac{\varphi(z)\,dz}{z\cos(nz/2)}=o(1)$ as $r_n\to\infty$. Then $\displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{k=-N}^N\frac{(-1)^k\varphi(2k+1)}{2k+1}=\tfrac12\varphi(0)$.

Let $\varphi(z)$ be entire. Suppose there is a sequence of positive numbers $r_n$, $1\le n<\infty$, tending to $\infty$ such that $\displaystyle I_n=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{|z|=r_n}\frac{\varphi(z)\,dz}{\sin(nz)}=o(1)$ as $n\to\infty$. Then $\displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{\substack{k=-N\\ k\neq 0}}^N(-1)^{k-1}\varphi(k)=\varphi(0)$.

Let $\varphi(z)$ be entire. Suppose there exists a sequence of positive numbers $r_n$, $1\le n<\infty$, tending to $\infty$ such that $\displaystyle I_n=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{|z|=r_n}\frac{\varphi(z)\,dz}{z\sin(nz)}=o(1)$ as $n\to\infty$. Then $\displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{\substack{k=-N\\ k\neq 0}}^N\frac{(-1)^{k-1}\varphi(k)}{k}=\varphi'(0)$.

Let $\varphi(z)=\psi(z)/\Gamma(z+1)$ satisfy the hypotheses of Example 3, where $\psi$ is entire. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k}{k\cdot k!}\psi(k)\approx\gamma\psi(0)+\psi'(0)$ (9.1), where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant.
As $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k-1}x^k}{k!\,k}\sim\log x+\gamma$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant.
Let $n$ be positive. Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k-1}x^{nk}}{k(k!)^n}\sim n\log x+n\gamma$. (Berndt notes Ramanujan's claim is false for $n>2$.)

Let $n$ be complex and let $\varphi$ be entire. Suppose there exists a sequence $r_j$, $1\le j<\infty$, of positive numbers tending to $\infty$ such that $\displaystyle I_j=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{|z|=r_j}\frac{n\varphi(z)\cot(nz)\cot\{n(z-n)\}\,dz}{\Gamma(z+1)\Gamma(n-z+1)}=o(1)$ as $r_j\to\infty$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\binom{n}{k}\varphi(k)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\binom{n}{k}\varphi(n-k)$, provided each series converges.

Let the hypotheses of Entry 10 be satisfied with $n$ replaced by $r$ and $\varphi(z)$ replaced by $x^{-z}\varphi(z)$, where $x>0$. If $\displaystyle f(x)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\binom{r}{k}x^{r-k}\varphi(k)$ (10.1) represents a function in a neighborhood of $\infty$, then $f(0)=\varphi(r)$.
Let the hypotheses of the Corollary above be satisfied with $r=-1$ and $\varphi(z)$ replaced by $\varphi(z+1)$. Then $f(0)=\varphi(0)$.

For $x>0$, let $\displaystyle f(x)=e^x\int_x^\infty\frac{1-e^{-t}}{t}\,dt$. Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle f(x)\sim e^x\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k-1}\psi(k)x^k}{k!\,k}$, where $\displaystyle\psi(k)=\sum_{n\le k}\frac{1}{n}$ and $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant. Also, $f(0^+)=\infty$.

For $\operatorname{Re} n>-1$ and $x\ge 0$, let $\displaystyle f(x)=\int_0^\infty e^{-t}(x+t)^n\,dt$. Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle f(x)\sim\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{\Gamma(n+1)\,x^{n-k}}{\Gamma(n-k+1)\,k!}$, and $f(0)=\Gamma(n+1)$.

For $x\ge 0$, let $\displaystyle f(x)=\int_x^\infty\frac{\sin(t-x)}{t}\,dt$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{\pi}{2}\cos x+(\gamma+\log x)\sin x-\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k\psi(2k+1)x^{2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}=f(x)\sim\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k(2k)!}{(2k+1)x^{2k+1}}$ as $x\to\infty$, where $\psi$ is defined in the third version of Example 2. Furthermore, $f(0)=\pi/2$.
If $m>-1$ and $n$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2}\cos^m x\cos(nx)\,dx=\frac{\pi\,\Gamma(m+1)}{2^{m+1}\Gamma\bigl(\frac{m+n+2}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\bigl(\frac{m-n+2}{2}\bigr)}$.
If $x$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\prod_{n=1}^\infty\left(1+\frac{x^6}{n^6\pi^6}\right)=\frac{\sinh(2\pi x)-2\sinh(\pi x)\cos(\pi x\sqrt{3})}{4\pi^4 x^4}$.

For each positive integer $k$, let $\displaystyle G_k=\sum_{0\le 2n+1\le k}\frac{1}{2n+1}$. Then for all complex $x$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k+1}x^k}{k!\,k}=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{G_k x^k}{k!}$ (15.1).

Assume that for $|h|$ sufficiently small, $f$ can be expanded as $f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n h^n \varphi^{(n)}(x)$, where each $a_n$ is independent of $\varphi$. If $f(x+h)-f(x)=h\varphi'(x)$, then $a_n=B_n/n!$ for $0\le n<\infty$, where $B_n$ denotes the $n$th Bernoulli number.
Under the expansion hypothesis of Entry 1(i), if $f(x+h)+f(x)=h\varphi'(x)$, then $a_n=(1-2^n)B_n/n!$ for $0\le n<\infty$.

For a polynomial $\varphi$, define for each nonnegative integer $n$ $F_{2n+1}(x)=\varphi(x)+\sum_{k=1}^n(-1)^k\binom{n}{k}^2\bigl\{\varphi(x+k)+\varphi(x-k)\bigr\}$. If $f(x+1)-f(x-1)=2\varphi'(x)$, then there exists a polynomial solution $f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty F_{2n+1}(x)/(2n+1)$.
With $F_{2n+1}$ as in Entry 2(i), if $f(x+1)+f(x-1)=2\varphi(x)$ for a polynomial $\varphi$, then there exists a polynomial solution $f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty F_{2n+1}(x)/(2n+1)$.

Define the Eulerian polynomials $\psi_n(p)$, $0\le n<\infty$, $p\ne -1$, by $\displaystyle\frac{e^x+p}{p+1}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\psi_n(p)x^n}{n!(p+1)^{n+1}}$ for $|x|<|\log(-p)|$. If $f(x+h)+pf(x)=\varphi(x)$ with $p\ne -1$, then in the notation of Entry 1(i), $a_n=(-1)^n\psi_n(p)/\{n!(p+1)^{n+1}\}$ for $0\le n<\infty$.

Let $1\le r\le n$, and let $F_k(n)$ denote the Eulerian numbers defined by $\displaystyle\psi_n(p)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}F_{k+1}(n)(-p)^k$. Then (i) $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{r-1}\binom{n+j}{j}F_{r-j}(n)=r^n$; (ii) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n+1}k^n=\sum_{j=0}^{r-1}\binom{n+1}{j}F_{r-j}(n)$; (iii) $\displaystyle F_r(n)=\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}(-1)^k\binom{r}{k}(r-k)^n$.

If $n\ge 0$ and $0<x<1-e^{-2\pi}$, then $\displaystyle\frac{x^{n+1}}{1-x}\,\psi_n(x-1)=\frac{n!}{(1-x)^{n+1}}\left(\log\frac{1}{1-x}\right)^{n+1}+(-1)^n\sum_{k=n+1}^\infty\frac{B_k}{k(k-n-1)!}\left(\log\frac{1}{1-x}\right)^{k-n-1}$, where $B_k$ denotes the $k$th Bernoulli number.
Let $f(x)$ denote the solution found in Entry 3. Then $f(x)$ is the term independent of $n$ in $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\varphi^{(k)}(x)/n^k$, provided the series in the numerator converges.
If $n$ is even and positive, then $\psi_n(p)$ is divisible by $1-p$.

Define $A_k$ by $\displaystyle\psi_n(p-1)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}A_k\binom{n}{k}(-p)^k$. For $1\le r\le n$: (i) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^r\binom{r}{k}A_k=r^n$; (ii) $\displaystyle A_r=\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}(-1)^k\binom{r}{k}(r-k)^n$; (iii) $A_r/n!$ is the coefficient of $x^n/n!$ in $(e^x-1)^r$; (iv) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}k^n\bigl(\zeta(k+1)^{-1}\bigr)=(-1)^n+(-1)^n2^{-n-1}\psi_n(1)+\sum_{k=1}^n(-1)^{k+1}A_k(k+1)$.
If $n$ is an integer exceeding $1$, then $\displaystyle-(2n+1)B_{2n}=\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\binom{2n}{2k}B_{2k}B_{2n-2k}$.
For each positive integer $n$, $2(2^{2n}-1)B_{2n}$ is an integer.
The numerator of $B_{2n}$ is divisible by the largest factor of $2n$ that is relatively prime to the denominator of $B_{2n}$.

For each positive integer $n$, the Bernoulli number $B_{2n}$ equals an integer minus $\displaystyle\sum_{\substack{p\ \mathrm{prime}\\ (p-1)\mid 2n}}\frac{1}{p}$ (von Staudt–Clausen theorem). Equivalently, the denominator of $B_{2n}$ in lowest terms is the product of all primes $p$ with $(p-1)\mid 2n$.

For each nonnegative integer $n$, $\displaystyle(-1)^{n-1}B_{2n}+(-1)^n\bigl(1-F_{2n}\bigr)$ is an integer divisible by $1/(2n)$, where $F_{2n}$ is the sum of the reciprocals of those primes $p$ such that $(p-1)\mid 2n$.
If $n$ is a natural number, then $\displaystyle2^{2n}(2^{2n}-1)B_{2n}=2n\sum_{k=0}^n\binom{2n}{2k}E_{2k}E_{2n-2k-2}$, where $E_j$ denotes the $j$th Euler number.

For a positive integer $n$, with $\lambda(s)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty(2k+1)^{-s}$, $\eta(s)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}k^{-s}$, and $L(s)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty(-1)^k(2k+1)^{-s}$: (i) $\displaystyle\zeta(2n)=\frac{(-1)^{n-1}(2\pi)^{2n}}{2(2n)!}\,B_{2n}$; (ii) $\displaystyle\lambda(2n)=\frac{(-1)^{n-1}(1-2^{-2n})(2\pi)^{2n}}{2(2n)!}\,B_{2n}$; (iii) $\displaystyle\eta(2n)=\frac{(-1)^{n-1}(1-2^{1-2n})(2\pi)^{2n}}{2(2n)!}\,B_{2n}$; (iv) $\displaystyle L(2n-1)=\frac{(-1)^{n-1}(\pi/2)^{2n-1}}{2(2n-2)!}\,E_{2n-2}$.

For $a,b>0$ and $n>1$, let $\displaystyle f(a)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty(a+bk)^{-n}$. As $b/a\to 0$, $\displaystyle a^n f(a)\sim\frac{a}{b^{n-1}}-\frac{1}{2}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}(n+2k-2)!}{(n-1)!\,2k\,b^{n+2k-1}}$.
The Riemann zeta-function has a simple pole at $s=1$ with residue $1$, and the constant term in the Laurent expansion of $\zeta(s)$ about $s=1$ is Euler's constant $\gamma$.

Suppose $|a_p|\le p^{-c}$ for each prime $p$ and some constant $c>1$. Then $\displaystyle\prod_p(1-a_p)^{-1}=1+\sum_{n=2}^\infty a_{p_1}\cdots a_{p_k}$, where the product is over all primes and the suffixes on the right are the (not necessarily distinct) primes in the canonical factorization of $n$.

For $\Re s>1$, define $\displaystyle T(s)=\sum_{\substack{n\ge 1\\ \omega(n)\ \mathrm{odd}}}n^{-s}$. Then $\displaystyle T(s)=\frac{\zeta(2s)-\zeta(s)}{2\zeta(s)}$, where the sum is over positive integers with an odd number of prime factors.
$T(2)=\pi^2/20$ and $T(4)=\pi^4/1260$, where $T(s)$ is as in Entry 28, Corollary 3.

For $|x|<1$, with $p_1,p_2,\ldots$ the primes in ascending order, Ramanujan claimed $\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^\infty(1-x^{p_k})^{-1}=1+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{x^{p_1+\cdots+p_k}}{(1-x)(1-x^2)\cdots(1-x^k)}$. This identity is false: if $c_n$ and $d_n$ denote the coefficients of $x^n$ on the left and right, then $c_n=d_n$ for $2\le n\le 20$, but $c_{21}=30$ and $d_{21}=31$.

Suppose $|a_p|\le p^{-c}$ for each prime $p$ and some constant $c>1$. Then $\displaystyle\prod_p(1+a_p)=1+\sum_{\substack{n\ge 1\\ n\ \mathrm{squarefree}}} a_{p_1}\cdots a_{p_k}$, where $n=p_1\cdots p_k$ with distinct primes.
The sum of the reciprocals of the primes diverges.
If $p_n$ denotes the $n$th prime, then $p_n/n-\log n$ tends to a limit as $n\to\infty$ (Ramanujan struck this statement out; in fact $p_n\sim n\log n$).
The Euler--Maclaurin constant of the divergent series $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty 1$ is $-\tfrac{1}{12}$.
The Euler--Maclaurin constant of the divergent series $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty k$ is $-\tfrac{1}{2}$ (equivalently, the Abel sum $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty (-1)^{k+1}k=\tfrac{1}{4}$).

If $h$ and $n$ are positive integers with $h>n$ and $f$ is analytic for all real numbers, then $\displaystyle\varphi(h)=\varphi(n)-\sum_{k=1}^n f(k+h)+\sum_{j=0}^h\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{k^j f^{(j)}(n)}{j!}$.

Let $h$ and $n$ be positive integers with $h$ fixed. Then as $n\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^h\frac{1}{k}\sim\gamma+\log n-\sum_{k=1}^{n-h}\frac{1}{k+h}$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant.
If $h$ is not a negative integer, then $\displaystyle\Gamma(h+1)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n^h n!}{(h+1)(h+2)\cdots(h+n)}$.
If $x$ is a nonnegative integer, then $\displaystyle\frac{\Gamma'(x+1)}{\Gamma(x+1)}=-\gamma+\sum_{n\le x}\frac{1}{n}$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant.

For $x\ge 0$, with $S_n(x)$ defined by $\displaystyle S_n(x)=\frac{B_{n+1}(x+1)-B_{n+1}}{n+1}$ and $S_{-1}(x)$ defined by $\displaystyle S_{-1}(x)=\frac{\Gamma'(x+1)}{\Gamma(x+1)}+\gamma$, $\displaystyle\int_0^x S_{-1}(t)\,dt=\log\Gamma(x+1)+\gamma x$.
If $S_{13}(x)$ is defined by $\displaystyle S_n(x)=\frac{B_{n+1}(x+1)-B_{n+1}}{n+1}$ with $n=13$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^x S_{13}(t)\,dt=\frac{S_{14}(x)}{14}-\frac{1}{12}$.

Suppose that $\varphi$ is analytic at the origin. If $C_n$ denotes the constant of $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty f^{(n)}(k)$, then in some neighborhood of the origin, $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=-\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{C_n x^n}{n!}$.

Let $c_n'$ be the constant of $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty f(k/n)$; in particular, $c_1'=c$. Define $\displaystyle\psi(x)=\sum_{k=0}^n\varphi(x-k)$ and $\displaystyle\eta(x)=\sum_{k=1}^n f(k/n)$. Then $\psi(x)-nc=\eta(x)-c_n'$.

In general, if $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(a_{2k-1}-a_{2k})>\sum_{k=1}^\infty(a_{2k}-a_{2k+1})$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}a_k=a_1-\sum_{k=1}^\infty(a_{2k}-a_{2k+1})$; otherwise $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}a_k=a_1-\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}a_{k+1}$. For instance, the divergent series constants $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\{(2k-1)-2k\}=\tfrac{1}{4}$ and $1-\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\{2k-(2k+1)\}=\tfrac{1}{4}$.
Define the sum of two alternating series by $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}a_k+\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}b_k:=a_1+\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}(b_k-a_{k+1})$.

Put $\Delta a_k=a_k-a_{k+1}$ and $\Delta^n a_1=\Delta(\Delta^{n-1}a_1)$ for $n\ge 2$. If $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}a_k$ converges, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}a_k=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\Delta^n a_1}{2^{n+1}}$ (Euler's transformation of series).

Let $x$ be any complex number which is not nonpositive. Then formally $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k k!}{x^{k+1}}=\cfrac{1}{x+1-\cfrac{1}{x+3-\cfrac{2^2}{x+5-\cfrac{3^2}{x+7-\cdots}}}}$ (as an asymptotic expansion of $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\frac{e^{-u}}{x+u}\,du$).
As $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k+1}}{(x+1)(x+2)\cdots(x+k)}\sim\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^{k+1}B(k)}{x^k}$, where $B(k)$ is the $k$th Bell number.

Let $x>0$. Then, for $n\ge 1$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{1}{e^{kx}-1}=\frac{\gamma}{x}-\frac{\log x}{4}-\frac{1}{2x}+\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{B_{2k}x^{2k-1}}{(2k)(2k)!}+R_n$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant and $|R_n|<\dfrac{|B_{2n}B_{2n+2}|x^{2n}}{(2n)!}\left(\dfrac{x^2}{4n^2}+\dfrac{n^2}{6}\right)$.
The constant of the divergent series $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty k^{1/100}$ is approximately $-0.4909$.

For each positive integer $n$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{r^k}{k(1-\alpha x^k)}-\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{(\alpha x^k)^k}{k(1-\alpha x^k)}=\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{(\alpha x^{k-1})^k\bigl(1-(\alpha x^{k-1})^{n+1}\bigr)}{k(1-\alpha x)}$.

Let $|r|<1$ and $|x|\le 1$. If $|x|=1$, assume $|\alpha r|<1$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{r^k}{k(1-\alpha x^k)}-\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(\alpha x^k)^k}{k(1-\alpha x^k)}=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(\alpha x^{k-1})^k}{k(1-\alpha x)}$.

Let $a$ and $b$ be arbitrary, $|x|\le 1$, and $|m|<1$. If $|x|=1$, assume $|mn|<1$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(a+kb)x^k}{1-mx^k}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(a+kb)(1-mnx^{2k})(mnx^k)^k}{(1-mx^k)(1-nx^k)}+\frac{b}{m}\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(mnx^k)^{k+1}}{(1-nx^k)^2}$.
For $|x|>1$, $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{d(n)}{x^n}=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{1}{x^k-1}$, where $d(n)$ denotes the number of positive divisors of $n$.

Let $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)=\sum_{k=1}^x k^r$ (1.1), where $r$ is any complex number. If $r\ne -1$, then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)\sim\zeta(-r)+\frac{x^{r+1}}{r+1}+\frac{x^r}{2}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}r(r+1)x^{r-2k+1}}{(2k)!\,r(r-2k+2)}$ (1.2).

Let $\varphi_r(x)$ be defined by (2.4) and let $a$ and $b$ be complex numbers with $b\ne 0$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x(a+kb)^r=b^r\left\{\varphi_r\!\left(x+\frac{a}{b}\right)-\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)\right\}$.
For any complex number $r$, $\displaystyle\frac{\sin(\pi r/2)B^*_{1-r}}{\zeta(-r)}=\frac{(2\pi)^r B^*_r}{2^r(r+1)}$ (4.1).

As $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x\frac{1}{\sqrt{k}}\sim\sqrt{2x}+\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2x}}-\frac{1}{24x^{3/2}}+\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}\,1\cdot 3\cdots(4k-3)}{2^{2k-1}(2k)!\,x^{2k-1/2}}$ (4.3). (Berndt notes Ramanujan's notebook approximation $\sum_{k=1}^x k^{-1/2}\sim\sqrt{2+4x}+\zeta(-\tfrac12)$ is incompatible with (4.3).)

As $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x k^{1/2}\sim\frac{2x^{3/2}}{3}+\frac{x^{1/2}}{2}-\frac{\zeta(-\tfrac12)}{4x^{1/2}}+\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}\,1\cdot 3\cdots(4k-5)}{2^{2k}(2k)!\,x^{2k-3/2}}$ (4.5). (Berndt notes Ramanujan's claim $\sum_{k=1}^x k^{1/2}\sim\sqrt{(x+\tfrac12)(x+\tfrac14)(x+\tfrac34)-\tfrac14\zeta(-\tfrac12)}$ is incompatible with (4.5), though it gives a good numerical approximation.)

As $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x k^{3/2}\sim\frac{2x^{5/2}}{5}+\frac{x^{3/2}}{2}+\frac{x^{1/2}}{8}-\frac{3\zeta(-\tfrac12)}{16\pi^2}-\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}\,1\cdot 3\cdots(4k-7)}{2^{2k-1}(2k)!\,x^{2k-5/2}}$ (4.7). (Berndt notes Ramanujan's polynomial radical approximation for this sum is incompatible with (4.7).)

Let $a$ and $b$ be complex numbers with $b\ne 0$ and $a/b$ not a negative integer. If $\Re(r)<-1$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}(a+kb)^r=(2b)^r\left\{\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)-\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{a}{2b}\right)\right\}$ (5.1).
Let $x$ be a positive integer and assume that $n>0$. Then $\displaystyle(x^2+x)^n=2\sum_{k=0}^n\binom{n}{2k+1}\varphi_{2n-2k-1}(x)$.
Under the same hypotheses as Entry 6(i), $\displaystyle\left(x+\tfrac12\right)(x^2+x)^n=\sum_{k=0}^n\left\{2\binom{n}{2k+1}+\binom{n}{2k}\right\}\varphi_{2n-2k}(x)$.

Let $y=x^2+x$ and $a=x+\tfrac12$. Then $\varphi_1(x)=\tfrac12 y$, $\varphi_2(x)=\tfrac16 ay$, $\varphi_3(x)=\tfrac12 y^2$, $\varphi_4(x)=\tfrac16 ay(y-\tfrac12)$, $\varphi_5(x)=\tfrac12(y-\tfrac12)$, $\varphi_6(x)=\tfrac16 ay(y^3-2y^2+\tfrac14-\tfrac12)$, $\varphi_9(x)=\tfrac{1}{30}y^2(y-1)(y^2-\tfrac12+1)$, $\varphi_{10}(x)=\tfrac{1}{42}ay(y-1)(y^3-\tfrac12 y^2+\tfrac{1}{30}y-\tfrac16)$, and $\varphi_{11}(x)=\tfrac{1}{12}y^2(y^4-4y^3+\tfrac{127}{30}y^2-10y+5)$.

For each positive integer $n$, (i) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n(2k-1+\sqrt5)^9=\varphi_9\!\left(2n-1+\sqrt5\right)$; (ii) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n(2k-1+\sqrt5)^{10}=(2n-1+\sqrt5)\,\varphi_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{2n-1+\sqrt5}{2}\right)$; and (iii) if $p$ and $n$ are positive integers with $n$ even, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^p(2k-1)^n=2^n\varphi_n\!\left(p+\tfrac12\right)$.
If $r$ is a positive integer, then $\varphi_r(x-1)+(-1)^r\varphi_r(-x)=0$.
If $r$ is a positive integer exceeding $1$, then $\varphi_r(x)$ is divisible by $x^2(x+1)^2$ or $x(x+\tfrac12)(x+1)$ according as $r$ is odd or even.

If $r$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)=\frac1{r+1}\sum_{k=0}^{r+1}\binom{r+1}{k}B_k x^{r+1-k}+x^r=\frac{x}{r+1}+\frac{x^r}{2}-\frac{2}{r}\sum_{k=1}^{\lfloor r/2\rfloor}\binom{r+1}{2k}\frac{B_{2k}}{2k}\{(1-2k)x^{r+1-2k}-2k\}$ (8.1).

For each complex number $r$ and each positive integer $n$, $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)-n^r\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{x-k}{n}\right)=(1-n^{r+1})\zeta(-r)=\frac{n^{r+1}-1}{r+1}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi r}{2}\right)B^*_r$ (10.1).

If $r$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_{-r}(x-1)+(-1)^r\varphi_{-r}(-x)=\{1+(-1)^r\}\zeta(r)+\frac{(-1)^r}{(r-1)!}\frac{d^{r-1}}{dx^{r-1}}\bigl(\pi\cot(\pi x)\bigr)$ (11.1), where if $r=1$, the first expression on the right side of (11.1) is understood to be $0$.
If $r$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)-2^r\left\{\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)+\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{x-1}{2}\right)\right\}=(1-2^{r+1})\zeta(-r)$.
If $r$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac12\right)=(2-2^{-r})\zeta(-r)$.
If $r$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(-1)+\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac12\right)=(3-3^{-r})\zeta(-r)$.
If $r$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac13\right)+\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac23\right)=(2+2^{-r}-4^{-r})\zeta(-r)$.
If $r$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac16\right)+\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac12\right)=(1+2^{-r}+3^{-r}-6^{-r})\zeta(-r)$.
If $r$ is a positive odd integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(-1)=\frac{(3-3^{-r})\zeta(-r)}{2}$.
If $r$ is a positive odd integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac12\right)=(2^{-r}-2^{2r-1})\zeta(-r)$.
If $r$ is a positive odd integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac13\right)=\frac{(1+2^{-r}+3^{-r}-6^{-r})\zeta(-r)}{2}$.
If $r$ is a positive odd integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac12\right)+\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac23\right)=\frac{(5-5^{-r})\zeta(-r)}{2}$.
If $r$ is a positive odd integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac14\right)+\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac34\right)=(2+2^{-2r-1}-2^{-3r-1})\zeta(-r)$.

If $r$ is a positive odd integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac{11}{30}\right)+\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac{13}{30}\right)=\frac{(3+2^r+5^{-r}-10^{-r})\zeta(-r)}{2}$ (Berndt notes Ramanujan's notebook value for this example is incorrect).

If $r$ is a positive odd integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac{11}{12}\right)+\varphi_r\!\left(-\tfrac{13}{12}\right)=\frac{(4-2^{-r}+4^{-r}+6^{-r}-12^{-r})\zeta(-r)}{2}$ (Berndt notes Ramanujan's notebook value for this example is incorrect).
Example 1 is the trivial case of the general formulae for extended Bernoulli numbers $B^*_r$ cited below.
For a positive odd integer $p$, the value $B^*_{p-2n-1}\!\left(-\tfrac12\right)$ is given by Hansen's tables [6.3.10].
For a positive odd integer $p$, the value $B^*_{p-2n-1}\!\left(-\tfrac13\right)$ is given by Hansen's tables [6.3.18].
For a positive odd integer $p$, the value $B^*_{p-2n-1}\!\left(-\tfrac16\right)$ is given by Hansen's tables [6.3.23].

For each nonnegative integer $k$, define $\displaystyle c_k=\lim_{m\to\infty}\sum_{j=1}^m\left\{\frac{(\log j)^k}{j}-\frac{(\log j)^{k+1}}{j(k+1)}\right\}$. Then for all $s$, $\displaystyle\zeta(s)=\frac{1}{s-1}+\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k c_k(s-1)^k}{k!}$ (13.2). In particular, if $A_k=(-1)^k c_k/k!$, $0\le k<\infty$, then $A_0=\gamma=0.5772156649$, $A_1=0.0728158455$, $A_2=-0.00485$, and $A_3=-0.00034$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant (13.3).

Let $\eta>0$. Then as $\eta\to 0$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{1}{k(k\eta-1)}\sim\frac{a_0}{\eta}-\log\eta+a_1+\sum_{k=1}^\infty a_{k+1}\eta^{2k-1}$ (14.1), where $\displaystyle a_0=\lim_{m\to\infty}\left(\sum_{k=2}^m\frac{1}{k\log k}-\log\log m\right)=0.7946786$ (14.2), $a_1=\tfrac12(1-\gamma)=0.2113922$, and $\displaystyle a_{k+1}=-\frac{B_{2k}(2k-1)}{2k}$ for $k\ge 1$, where $B_j$ denotes the $j$th Bernoulli number and $A_j$ is defined in Entry 13, $0\le j<\infty$. In particular, $a_2=-0.0060680$ and $a_3=-0.000000475$ (Berndt notes Ramanujan's value $-0.0000028$ for $a_3$ is incorrect).

For $s>0$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{1}{k^{s+1}\log k}=-\log s+C+(1-\gamma)s-\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{A_{k-1}s^k}{k!}$ (14.11), where $\displaystyle C=\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{1}{k^2\log k}-1+\gamma+\sum_{k=2}^\infty\frac{A_{k-1}}{k!}$ (14.12), and where $A_k$, $1\le k<\infty$, is defined in Entry 13. Furthermore, $C=0.2174630$, $1-\gamma=0.4227843$, $-\tfrac12 A_1=-0.0364079$, $-\tfrac14 A_2=0.001615$, $-\tfrac18 A_3=0.000086$, and $-\tfrac1{16}A_4=-0.00002$.
Let $\Re(r)>-1$ and $0<x<1$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{\varphi_r(x-1)-\varphi_r(-x)}{4\Gamma(r+1)}=-\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi r}{2}\right)\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{\sin(2\pi k x)}{(2\pi k)^{r+1}}$.
Let $\Re(r)>-1$ and $0<x<1$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{\varphi_r(x-1)+\varphi_r(-x)-2\zeta(-r)}{4\Gamma(r+1)}=\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi r}{2}\right)\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{\cos(2\pi k x)}{(2\pi k)^{r+1}}$.

Let $p$ and $q$ be integers with $0<p<q$. Then if $r$ is any complex number, $\displaystyle\frac{(2\pi q)^r}{4\Gamma(r)}\left\{\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(\frac{p}{q-1}\right)-\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(-\frac{p}{q}\right)\right\}=-\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi r}{2}\right)\sum_{j=1}^{q-1}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi jp}{q}\right)\left\{\zeta(r)-\varphi_{-r}\!\left(\frac{j}{q}\right)\right\}$ (16.1).

Let $p$ and $q$ be integers with $0<p<q$. For any complex number $r$, $\displaystyle\frac{(2\pi q)^r}{4\Gamma(r)}\left\{\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(\frac{p}{q-1}\right)+\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(-\frac{p}{q}\right)-2(1-q^{-r})\zeta(1-r)\right\}=-\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi p}{q}\right)\sum_{j=1}^{q-1}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi jp}{q}\right)\left\{\zeta(r)-\varphi_{-r}\!\left(\frac{j}{q}\right)\right\}$ (16.2).
For each complex number $r$, $\displaystyle\frac{\cos(\pi r/2)E^*_{1-r}}{2^r}=2L(r)=\frac{(\pi/2)^r E^*_r}{\Gamma(r)}$ (18.1).

Assume the hypotheses of Corollary 16(i) with the additional assumption that $q$ is odd. Then $\displaystyle\frac{(2\pi q)^r}{4\Gamma(r)}\left\{\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(\frac{p}{q-1}\right)-\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)\right\}=\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi r}{2}\right)^{(q-1)/2}\sum_{j=1}^{q-1}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi jp}{q}\right)\left\{\varphi_{-r}\!\left(\frac{j}{q-1}\right)-\varphi_{-r}\!\left(\frac{j}{q}\right)\right\}$.

Suppose that all hypotheses of Corollary 16(ii) hold and that $q$ is odd. Then $\displaystyle\frac{(2\pi q)^r}{4\Gamma(r)}\left\{\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(\frac{p}{q-1}\right)+\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)-2\zeta(1-r)\right\}=\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi r}{2}\right)^{(q-1)/2}\sum_{j=1}^{q-1}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi jp}{q}\right)\left\{\varphi_{-r}\!\left(\frac{j}{q-1}\right)+\varphi_{-r}\!\left(-\frac{j}{q}\right)\right\}$ (Berndt notes Ramanujan's notebook version is incorrect).
Let $\Re(r)>0$ and suppose that $0\le x<1$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{2^{r-1}\pi^{r+1}}{\Gamma(r+1)}\varphi_r(-x)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{\sin(\pi k x)\cos(\pi k x+\pi r/2)}{k^{r+1}}$.

If $r$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\frac{(6\pi)^r}{2\sqrt3\,\Gamma(r)}\left\{\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(-\tfrac13\right)-\varphi_{r-1}\!\left(-\tfrac23\right)\right\}=\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi r}{2}\right)\left\{\varphi_{-r}\!\left(-\tfrac13\right)-\varphi_{-r}\!\left(-\tfrac23\right)\right\}$.

Let $u$ and $n$ be fixed, where $0\le u\le n$ and $n$ is an integer. Let $\varphi(z)$ be analytic in a disc centered at $u$ and containing the segment $[0,n]$. If $\varphi_\infty(u)$ is defined by (21.2), then $\displaystyle\varphi_\infty(u)=\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{\varphi^{(j)}(u)}{j!}\sum_{k=0}^n\binom{n}{k}(k-u)^j p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$ (21.3), where $p=u/n$ and $\varphi_\infty(u)=\sum_{k=0}^n\binom{n}{k}\varphi(k)p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$ (21.2).

Let $A_1=0$. For each nonnegative integer $r$, $r\ne 1$, set $A_r=\{1+(-1)^r\}\zeta(r)$. If $n$ is a natural number, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{k^n}{(k-\tfrac12)^n}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}A_{n-k}\!\left(-\tfrac12\right)$.
If $n=3$ in Entry 22 (equivalently Entry 35 of Chapter 9), then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{k^3}{(k-\tfrac12)^3}=K_3=10-\pi^2$.
When $x$ is large, $e^x\Gamma(x+1)/x^x\approx\sqrt{2\pi x+\pi/3}$ (Ramanujan's notebook approximation; Berndt notes the $x^{-2}$ correction terms have opposite sign to Stirling's expansion).
For every complex number $z$ and positive integer $n$, $\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^n\Gamma\!\left(z+\frac{k}{n}\right)=(2\pi)^{(n-1)/2}n^{-nz-1/2}\Gamma(nz+1)$.
For every complex number $z$, $\displaystyle\Gamma(z+1)=\Gamma\!\left(\frac{z}{2}\right)\Gamma\!\left(\frac{z+1}{2}\right)2^{1-z}\sqrt\pi$ (Legendre's duplication formula).

Suppose that $n$ is a natural number and that $|z|>n$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{(n!)^2}{2\pi z}\prod_{k=1}^n\left\{1+\left(\frac{z}{n+k}\right)^2\right\}=\frac{2}{\pi}\sinh(\pi z)\exp\left\{\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k\varphi_{2k}(n)}{k z^{2k}}\right\}$ (27.1).

Under the same hypotheses as Entry 27(i), $\displaystyle\frac{(n!)^2}{2\pi z}\prod_{k=1}^n\left\{1+\left(\frac{z}{n+k}\right)^2\right\}=2(n!)^2\sinh(nz)\exp\left\{2n-2z\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n}{z}\right)+\sum_{j=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^j B_{2j}S_{2j}}{jz^{2j}}\right\}$ (27.6), where $\displaystyle S_{2j}=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k(2k+2j-1)!(n)^{2k+1}}{(2j-1)!(2k+1)!\,n^{2k+1}}$, $j\ge 1$.

Let $n$ be a positive integer and suppose that $x>0$. Write $r^2=n^2+x^2$ with $r>0$ and put $\rho=\tan^{-1}(x/n)$. Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\log\left\{2n(x^2+n^2)^{n-1/2}\prod_{k=0}^n\left(1+\frac{x^2}{(n+k)^2}\right)\right\}\sim 2\log\Gamma(n)+2n+2x\rho-\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}\cos\{(2k-1)\rho\}}{k(2k-1)r^{2k}}$.
As $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^x\frac{1}{k}\sim\log x+\gamma-\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}}{2k\,x^{2k}}$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant and $B_{2k}$ are Bernoulli numbers.
If $x$ is real, then $\displaystyle\psi(x+1)+\gamma=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+x}\right)$ (3.1), where $\psi(x)=\Gamma'(x)/\Gamma(x)$ and $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant.
For $|x|<1$, $\displaystyle\psi(x+1)+\gamma=\sum_{k=1}^\infty(-1)^{k+1}\binom{k+x}{k+1}x^{k+1}$ (4.1), where the left side is interpreted as in (3.1).
For any complex number $x$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\left\{\frac{1}{k-1+x}-\frac{1}{k+x}\right\}=\pi\cot(\pi x)$ (5.1). (Ramanujan incorrectly multiplies the right side of (5.1) by $-1$.)
Let $n$ be a positive integer and let $x$ be any complex number. Then $\displaystyle n\psi(x+1)-\sum_{k=1}^n\psi\!\left(\frac{x+k}{n}+1\right)=n\log n$.
If $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\psi\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)=-(n-1)\gamma-n\log n$ (6.1).
If $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n\psi\!\left(\frac{2k-1}{2n}\right)=-n\gamma-n\log(4n)$.
If $x$ is a positive integer and $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary complex numbers, then $\displaystyle\psi\!\left(\frac{a}{b}+x+1\right)-\psi\!\left(\frac{a}{b}+1\right)=\sum_{k=1}^x\frac{b}{a+bk}$.
If $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary complex numbers, then $\displaystyle\psi\!\left(\frac{a+2b}{2b}\right)-\psi\!\left(\frac{a+b}{2b}\right)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{2b(-1)^{k+1}}{a+bk}$.

Let $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=1+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{1}{k^3-kx}$ (11.1). If $x$ is any complex number, then $\displaystyle\psi\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\psi\!\left(1-\frac{1}{x}\right)=-2\gamma-x\varphi(x)$.
If $\Re x>1$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^1\frac{u^{x-2}(1-u)^2}{1-u}\,du=\varphi(x)-1$, where $\varphi$ is defined by (11.1).

If $\Re x>0$, then $\displaystyle\varphi(2x)-\tfrac{1}{2}\varphi(x)-\tfrac{1}{2}\log 2=\int_0^1\frac{1}{1+ux}\,du-\frac{\pi}{2x}\csc\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2x}\right)$, where $\varphi$ is defined by (11.1).

Let $\ell$ and $n$ be positive integers with $\ell<n+1$. If $n$ is even, then $\displaystyle\int\frac{x^{\ell-1}}{x^n-1}\,dx=-\log(x-1)+\frac{(-1)^\ell}{n}\log(x+1)+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{\lfloor(\ell n-1)/n\rfloor}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi r\ell}{n}\right)\log\!\left(x^2-2x\cos\frac{2\pi r}{n}+1\right)-\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{\lfloor(\ell n-1)/n\rfloor}\frac{\sin(2\pi r\ell/n)}{\sin(2\pi r/n)}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x-\cos(2\pi r/n)}{\sin(2\pi r/n)}\right)$. If $n$ is odd, then $\displaystyle\int\frac{x^{\ell-1}}{x^n+1}\,dx=\frac{(-1)^{\ell-1}}{n}\log(x+1)-\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{\lfloor(\ell n-1)/n\rfloor}\cos\!\left(\frac{(2r-1)\pi\ell}{n}\right)\log\!\left(x^2-2x\cos\frac{(2r-1)\pi}{n}+1\right)+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{\lfloor(\ell n-1)/n\rfloor}\frac{\sin((2r-1)\pi\ell/n)}{\sin((2r-1)\pi/n)}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x-\cos((2r-1)\pi/n)}{\sin((2r-1)\pi/n)}\right)$.

Let $\ell$ and $n$ be positive integers with $\ell<n+1$. If $n$ is odd, then $\displaystyle\int\frac{x^{\ell-1}}{x^n-1}\,dx=-\log(x-1)+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{\lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi r\ell}{n}\right)\log\!\left(x^2-2x\cos\frac{2\pi r}{n}+1\right)-\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{\lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor}\frac{\sin(2\pi r\ell/n)}{\sin(2\pi r/n)}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x-\cos(2\pi r/n)}{\sin(2\pi r/n)}\right)$. If $n$ is even, then $\displaystyle\int\frac{x^{\ell-1}}{x^n+1}\,dx=-\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{n/2}\cos\!\left(\frac{(2r-1)\pi\ell}{n}\right)\log\!\left(x^2-2x\cos\frac{(2r-1)\pi}{n}+1\right)+\frac{2}{n}\sum_{r=1}^{n/2}\frac{\sin((2r-1)\pi\ell/n)}{\sin((2r-1)\pi/n)}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x-\cos((2r-1)\pi/n)}{\sin((2r-1)\pi/n)}\right)$.

We have (i) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k}{(3k+1)\,3^{2k+1}}=\dfrac{\pi}{6\sqrt{3}}+\dfrac{1}{6}\gamma$; (ii) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k(\sqrt{3}-1)\,3^{2k+1}}{(1+\sqrt{3})(3k+1)}=-\dfrac{\pi}{4\sqrt{3}}+\dfrac{1}{4\sqrt{3}}\gamma$; and (iii) $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k(2-\sqrt{3})^{2k+1}}{4k+1}=-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}-1}{16}\log\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ (Ramanujan inadvertently omitted a factor $\sqrt{2}$ in the denominator on the right side).

With $\varphi(x)$ defined by (11.1), we have (i) $\varphi(2)=2\log 2$; (ii) $\varphi(3)=\log 3$; (iii) $\varphi(4)=\tfrac{3}{2}\log 2$; (iv) $\varphi(6)=\tfrac{1}{2}\log 3+\tfrac{1}{4}\log 4$; (v) $\varphi(5)=\tfrac{1}{4}\log 5+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{2}\log\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2}\right)$; (vi) $\varphi(8)=\log 2+\tfrac{1}{4}\log(\sqrt{2}+1)$; (vii) $\varphi(10)=\tfrac{1}{2}\log 2+\tfrac{1}{4}\log 5+\dfrac{3}{2\sqrt{5}}\log\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2}\right)$; (viii) $\varphi(12)=\dfrac{3+\sqrt{3}}{6}\log 2+\tfrac{1}{4}\log 3-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{6}\log(\sqrt{3}-1)$; (ix) $\varphi(16)=\tfrac{1}{4}\log 2+\dfrac{1}{4\sqrt{2}}\log(\sqrt{2}+1)$; and (x) $\displaystyle\varphi(20)=\tfrac{1}{10}\log 2+\tfrac{1}{4}\log 5+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{4}\log\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2}\right)+\dfrac{\sqrt{10+2\sqrt{5}}}{40}\log\!\left(\dfrac{4+\sqrt{10+2\sqrt{5}}}{4-\sqrt{10+2\sqrt{5}}}\right)+\dfrac{\sqrt{10-2\sqrt{5}}}{40}\log\!\left(\dfrac{4+\sqrt{10-2\sqrt{5}}}{4-\sqrt{10-2\sqrt{5}}}\right)$.

Let $x$ be real and positive with $\log a=\psi(x+1)$ (15.1), and let $n$ be any complex number. Then as $a\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\left(\frac{a}{z}\right)^{4n}\sim 1-\frac{n}{6a^2}+\frac{n(7n-31)}{2880a^4}-\frac{533n}{10080a^6}+\cdots$ (15.2), where $z$ is defined implicitly by $\log a=\psi(z+1)$.

Let $A_k=\lfloor 3^k/2\rfloor-1$ for $k\ge 0$. Then $\displaystyle\gamma=\log 2-2\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{A_k}{k}\sum_{j=A_k-1+1}^{\lfloor 3^j/2\rfloor-1}\frac{1}{(3^j-3)}$ (16.1), where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant.

Let $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=\sum_{k=1}^x\frac{\log k}{k}$ (17.1), $c_1=\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\left\{\varphi(n)-\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 n\right\}$ (17.2), and $H_n=\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{1}{k}$ (17.3). Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\varphi(x)-\psi(x+1)\log x-\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 x+c_1+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}}{2^{2k-1}\,2k\,x^{2k}}$, where $B_n$ denotes the $n$th Bernoulli number.
Let $\varphi(x)$ and $c_1$ be defined by (17.1) and (17.2), respectively. Then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}\left\{\varphi(x)-\tfrac{1}{2}\psi^2(x+1)\right\}=c_1$.
If $\varphi(x)$ is defined by (17.7) and $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle n\varphi(x)-\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\varphi\!\left(\frac{x-k}{n}\right)=n\log n\,\psi(x+1)-\tfrac{1}{2}n\log^2 n$.
If $\varphi(x)$ is defined by (17.7), then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\varphi\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)=\gamma n\log n+\tfrac{1}{2}n\log^2 n$ (17.8).

Let $\varphi(x)$ be defined by (17.7). If $0<x<1$, then $\displaystyle\varphi(x-1)-\varphi(-x)=(\gamma+\log(2\pi))\pi\cot(\pi x)+2\pi\sum_{k=1}^\infty\sin(2\pi kx)\log k$ (17.11). (Berndt: right-side series diverges for $0<x<1$.)

Let $c_1$ and $H_n$ be defined by (17.2) and (17.3), respectively. Define $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=\sum_{k=1}^x\log^2 k$ (18.1) and $\displaystyle C=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left\{\sum_{k=1}^n\log^2 k-n\log^2 n\right\}$ (18.2). Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\varphi(x)-2\log x\,\log\!\left(\frac{\Gamma(x)}{\sqrt{\pi}}\right)\sim-\left(x+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\log^2 x+2x+C-2\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}H_{2k-2}}{(2k-1)(2k)\,x^{2k-1}}$.

Let $\varphi(x)$ be defined by (18.13) and let $C$ be given by (18.2). If $n$ is any natural number, then $\displaystyle\varphi(x)-\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\varphi\!\left(\frac{x-j}{n}\right)=2\log n\,\log\frac{\Gamma(x+1)}{\sqrt{2\pi}}-\left(x+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\log^2 n-C(n-1)$ (18.16), where $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=-\zeta'(0,x+1)+\zeta'(0)$ (18.13).

Let $\varphi(x)$, $c_1$, and $C$ be defined by (18.13), (17.2), and (18.2), respectively. If $0<x<1$, then $\displaystyle\tfrac{1}{2}\{\varphi(x-1)+\varphi(-x)\}=c_1-\tfrac{1}{4}\pi^2\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{\log k}{k}\cos(2\pi kx)+\tfrac{1}{2}(\gamma+\log(2\pi))\left\{\gamma-\log\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\pi\csc^2(\pi x)\right)\right\}$ (18.19).
Under the assumptions of Entry 18(ii), $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\varphi\!\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)=\log(2\pi)\log n+\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 n+C(n-1)$.

Let $\varphi(x)$ be defined by (18.13) and let $C$ be given by (18.2). Then $\displaystyle\varphi\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{4}\right)=\log(2\pi)\log 2+\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 2+C$; $\displaystyle\varphi\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right)+\varphi\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{4}\right)=\log(2\pi)\log 3+\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 3+2C$; $\displaystyle\varphi\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{4}\right)+\varphi\!\left(-\tfrac{3}{4}\right)=\log(2\pi)\log 2+\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 2+2C$; and $\displaystyle\varphi\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right)+\varphi\!\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right)=\log 2\log 3+2C$.

Let $n$ be a positive integer. Then, in the notation of (19.2), $\displaystyle\mathfrak{L}\psi_n(x)=\mathfrak{L}\sum_{j=0}^n(-1)^{j+1}\binom{n}{j}\log^j x\,\psi_{n-j}(x)$ (19.3), where $\mathfrak{L}f(x)$ denotes the logarithmic part of $f(x)$ as $x\to\infty$.

Let $\psi_n(x)$, $n\ge 0$, be defined by (19.2). Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^n(-1)^{n+k}\binom{n}{k}\log^k x\,\psi_{n-k}(x)\sim n!\left\{\frac{B_{n+1}}{(n+1)x^n}+\frac{nB_{n+2}}{2(n+2)x^{n+1}}+\frac{n(n+1)B_{n+3}}{2^2\cdot 2!(n+3)x^{n+2}}\right.$ $\displaystyle\left.+\frac{n(n+2)(n+3)B_{n+4}}{2^3\cdot 3!(n+4)x^{n+3}}\right.$ $\displaystyle\left.+\frac{n\{(n+2)(n+4)^2+\tfrac{1}{2}(n+2)+\tfrac{1}{3}\}B_{n+5}}{2^4\cdot 4!(n+5)x^{n+4}}\right.$ $\displaystyle\left.+\frac{n(n+4)(n+5)\{(n+3)(n+4)+\tfrac{1}{2}n+\tfrac{1}{6}\}B_{n+6}}{2^5\cdot 5!(n+6)x^{n+5}}+\cdots\right\}$ (19.7).

Let $\psi_n(x)$ be defined by (19.17), where $n\ge 1$. If $r$ is any positive integer, then $\displaystyle\psi_n(x)-\sum_{j=0}^{r-1}\psi_n\!\left(\frac{x-j}{r}\right)=\sum_{j=1}^n\binom{n}{j}(-1)^{j+1}\log^j r\,\psi_{n-j}(x)$ (19.19).
For each pair of positive integers $n$ and $r$, $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{r-1}\psi_n\!\left(\frac{j}{r}\right)=\sum_{j=1}^n\binom{n}{j}(-1)^{j+1}c_j\log^j r$, where $c_j$ is the constant in (19.1).

Let $\mathfrak{L}f(x)$ denote the logarithmic part of $f(x)$ as defined prior to Entry 19(i). If $x$ and $n$ are positive integers, then $\displaystyle\mathfrak{L}\sum_{j=0}^{x-1}\psi_n\!\left(\frac{x-j}{x}\right)=0$.

Let $r$ and $x$ be positive integers and suppose $k>0$. Define $F_k(x)=P\varphi(x)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^x n^{k-1}$ (20.1). If $k\neq 1$, then $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=\sum_{j=0}^r\binom{r}{j}\psi_{j(-k)}\frac{x^{r-j}}{(k-1)^{j+1}}+\frac{C_r(k)}{(k-1)^{r+1}}x^r+\frac{P}{(k-1)^{r+1}}+2\varphi(k)+kx+1$ (20.2), where $\displaystyle\psi_{j(-k)}=\frac{k(-1)^{j+1}\log^{j+1}k}{j!(k-1)^{j+1}}$ for $0\le j\le r$, $\displaystyle C_r(k)=\frac{(-1)^{r+1}r!}{k\log^{r+1}k}$, and $R$ is given by (20.5).

For each positive integer $r$, $\displaystyle\psi_r(-k)=\frac{(-1)^{r+1}}{(1-k)^{r+1}}$ and $\displaystyle k^j\psi_j(-k)=\frac{k}{j!}\,\psi_{j(-k)}$ for $0\le j\le r$, where $\psi_{j(-k)}$ is defined in Entry 20(i).

For $0<k<1$ and real $r$, let $\displaystyle C_r(k)=\sum_{m=1}^\infty m^r k^{m-1}$. If $x$ is real and $n$ is any positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^n F_k\!\left(\frac{x-j}{n}\right)=nC_r(k)+n^{-rk(1-n)/n}\left\{F_{k/n}(x)-C_r(k/n)\right\}$ (20.9), where $F_k$ is defined by (20.8).
Under the same hypotheses as Entry 20(iii), $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^n F_k\!\left(-\frac{j}{n}\right)=nC_r(k)-n^{-rk(1-n)/n}C_r(k/n)$.

Define $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)=\sum_{k=1}^x\frac{\log^r k}{k^r}$ (21.1) and $\displaystyle H_n(r)=\sum_{k=0}^n\frac{1}{k+r}$ (21.2). Let $\zeta(s,x)$ denote the extended Hurwitz zeta-function. Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)+\log x\,\zeta(r,x+1)-\zeta'(r)\sim\frac{1}{(r-1)^2 x^{r-1}}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}(r)_{2k-1}H_{2k-1}(r)}{(2k)!\,x^{r+2k-1}}$ (21.3).

Let $\varphi_r(x)$ be defined by (21.10). Then for $r>0$ and $|x|<1$, $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)=\sum_{j=1}^\infty\binom{-r}{j}\left\{\zeta(r+j)H_{j-1}(r)-\zeta'(r+j)\right\}x^j$, where $H_n(r)$ is defined by (21.2).
If $r>1$ and $n$ is any natural number, then $\displaystyle n^r\varphi_r(x)-\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{x-j}{n}\right)=(n-n^r)\zeta'(r)-nr\log n\,\zeta(r,x+1)$.
For $r>1$ and each positive integer $n$, $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n-1}\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{j}{n}\right)=nr\log n\,\zeta(r)+(nr-n)\zeta'(r)$.
If $c_r$ is the constant appearing in the asymptotic expansion of $\varphi_r(x)$ as $x\to\infty$, then $\displaystyle c_r=\lim_{x\to\infty}\left\{\varphi_r(x)-\frac{\log^{r+1}x}{r+1}\right\}$.

Let $r$ be any nonnegative integer and $n$ any positive integer. With $\varphi_r(x)$ defined by (22.2), $\displaystyle n\varphi_r(x)-\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\varphi_r\!\left(\frac{x-j}{n}\right)=\binom{n}{r}\log^{r+1}n+n\sum_{j=1}^r\binom{r}{j}(-1)^{j+1}\log^j n\left\{\varphi_{r-j}(x)-c_{r-j}\right\}+\frac{(-1)^r n}{r+1}\log^{r+1}n$.

Let $c_r$ be defined as in Entry 22(i). If $r\ge 0$ and $\Re s>0$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{\log^r k}{k^{s+1}}=\frac{r(r+1)}{s^{r+1}}+\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k c_{r+k}s^k}{k!}$ (23.1).

Let $\varphi(x)$ be defined by (24.1). Then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\varphi(x)-\left\{\sum_{k=1}^x\frac{1}{\sqrt{k}}-\zeta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\right\}\log x\sim-4\sqrt{x}-\zeta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\sqrt{x}+\tfrac{1}{2}\pi+\tfrac{1}{2}\log(8\pi)+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}(\tfrac{1}{2})_{2k-1}H_{2k-2}(\tfrac{1}{2})}{(2k)!\,x^{2k-1/2}}$, where $H_n(r)$ is defined by (21.2).

Let $\varphi(x)$ and $\psi(x)$ be defined by (24.2) and (24.7), respectively, for $x>-1$. If $n$ is any positive integer, then $\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\varphi(x)-\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\varphi\!\left(\frac{x-j}{n}\right)=\psi(x)\log n-\left(\tfrac{1}{2}n-1\right)c-\tfrac{1}{2}n\zeta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\log n$, where $c$ is given by (24.4).

Let $\varphi(x)$ and $\psi(x)$ be defined by (24.2) and (24.7), respectively. If $0<x<1$, then $\displaystyle\varphi(x-1)+\varphi(-x)-2c+(\gamma+\tfrac{1}{2}\pi+\log(8\pi))\left\{\psi(x-1)+\psi(-x)-2\zeta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\right\}=2\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{\log k}{\sqrt{k}}\cos(2\pi kx)$, where $c$ is given by (24.4).

Under the same hypotheses as Entry 24(iv), $\displaystyle\varphi(x-1)-\varphi(-x)+(\gamma-\tfrac{1}{2}\pi+\log(8\pi))\left\{\psi(x-1)-\psi(-x)\right\}=2\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{\log k}{\sqrt{k}}\sin(2\pi kx)$.

If $c$ is defined by (24.4), then $\displaystyle c\approx 4-\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{B_{2k}(\tfrac{1}{2})_{2k-1}H_{2k-2}(\tfrac{1}{2})}{(2k)!}$ (24.13), where the sign $\approx$ indicates semiconvergent interpretation.

For each positive integer $r$, define $S_r=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\bigl\{(2k+1-a)^{-r}-(2k+1+a)^{-r}\bigr\}$, where $a$ is real but not an odd integer. Assume that $|x|<\pi$. If $r$ is an odd positive integer, then $c_r(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2k+1-a)x-\cos(2k+1+a)x}{(2k+1-a)^r}=\sum_{k=0}^{(r-1)/2}\frac{(-1)^k S_{r-2k}\,x^{2k}}{(2k)!}$.

With $S_r$ as in Entry 1(i), if $r$ is an even positive integer, then $s_r(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\sin(2k+1-a)x-\sin(2k+1+a)x}{(2k+1-a)^r}=\sum_{k=0}^{r/2-1}\frac{(-1)^k S_{r-2k-1}\,x^{2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}$.

For each integer $r\ge 2$, define $S_r=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\bigl\{(2k+1-a)^{-r}+(2k+1+a)^{-r}\bigr\}$, where $a$ is real but not an odd integer. Assume that $0<x<\pi$. If $r$ is an even positive integer, then $c_r(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2k+1-a)x+\cos(2k+1+a)x}{(2k+1-a)^r}=\sum_{k=0}^{r/2-1}\frac{(-1)^k S_{r-2k}\,x^{2k}}{(2k)!}+\frac{(-1)^{r/2}\pi x^{r-1}}{2(r-1)!}$.

With $S_r$ as in Entry 2(i), if $r$ is an odd positive integer, then $s_r(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{\sin(2k+1-a)x+\sin(2k+1+a)x}{(2k+1-a)^r}=\sum_{k=0}^{(r-3)/2}\frac{(-1)^k S_{r-1-2k}\,x^{2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}+\frac{(-1)^{(r-1)/2}\pi x^{r-1}}{2(r-1)!}$.

Let $H_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}1/k$, and for a natural number $n$ and real $x$ define $\delta_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k\sin(2k+1)x}{(2k+1)^n}$, $C_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k\cos(2k+1)x}{(2k+1)^n}$, and $\varphi_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k H_{k+1}\cos(2k+1)x}{(2k+1)^n}$. If $n$ is an odd integer at least equal to $3$, then $\varphi_{n-2}(x)-\varphi_n(x)=x\delta_{n-2}(x)-x\delta_n(x)+nC_{n-1}(x)-nC_{n+1}(x)$ (3.2). (Berndt notes this claim is false for $n$ sufficiently large.)

For each nonnegative integer $n$, define $F_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{2k}{k}\frac{(-1)^k\sin(2k+1)x}{2^{2k}(2k+1)^n}-(-1)^n\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{2k}{k}\frac{(-1)^k\sin(2k+2)x}{2^{2k}(2k+2)^n}$, $\psi_n(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{2k}{k}\frac{(-1)^k\cos(2k+1)x}{2^{2k}(2k+1)^n}+(-1)^n\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{2k}{k}\frac{(-1)^k\cos(2k+2)x}{2^{2k}(2k+2)^n}-(-1)^n\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{2k}{k}\frac{(-1)^k\cos(2k+2)x}{2^{2k}(2k+2)^{n+1}}$, and $\varphi(n)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{2k}{k}\frac{1}{2^{2k}(2k+1)^{n+1}}$, where $\zeta$ denotes the Riemann zeta-function. Let $|x|<\pi/2$ and let $n$ be an integer. If $n\ge 0$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}\frac{(-1)^k x^{n-2k}}{(n-2k)!}\sum_{j=0}^{k}2^{-2j}S_{2j}\,\varphi(2k-2j)=\begin{cases}\sin(n\pi/2)\,F_n(x),&n\text{ odd},\\\cos(n\pi/2)\,\psi_n(x),&n\text{ even},\end{cases}$ (4.1).

With the notation of Entry 4(i), if $n\ge -1$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}\frac{(-1)^k x^{n-2k}}{(n-2k)!}\sum_{j=0}^{k}2^{-2j}S_{2j}\,\varphi(2k+1-2j)=\begin{cases}\sin(n\pi/2)\,F_{n+1}(x),&n\text{ odd},\cos(n\pi/2)\,\psi_{n+1}(x),&n\text{ even},\end{cases}$ (4.2).
Let $a$, $n$, and $\theta$ be real with $n\ge 0$ and $|\theta|\le\pi/2$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k}\sin(a+2k\theta)=2^n\cos^n\theta\sin(a+n\theta)$.
Let $a$, $n$, and $\theta$ be real with $n\ge 0$ and $|\theta|\le\pi/2$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k}\cos(a+2k\theta)=2^n\cos^n\theta\cos(a+n\theta)$.
For $|z|\le 1$, with $\operatorname{Li}_2$ and $\chi_2$ as in Berndt (6.1)--(6.2), $\operatorname{Li}_2(1-z)+\operatorname{Li}_2\!\bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{z}\bigr)=-\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 z$.

Let $\operatorname{Li}_3(z)$ be defined by (6.1). Then $\operatorname{Li}_3(1-z)+\operatorname{Li}_3\!\bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{z}\bigr)+\operatorname{Li}_3(z)=\zeta(3)+\tfrac{\pi^2}{6}\log z+\tfrac{1}{2}\log^3 z-\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 z\,\log(1-z)$.
Let $\operatorname{Li}_3(z)$ be defined by (6.1). Then $\operatorname{Li}_3(1-z)-\operatorname{Li}_3\!\bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{z}\bigr)=\tfrac{\pi^2}{6}\log z+\tfrac{1}{2}\log^3 z$.

For $|x|<1$, define $f(x)=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_k x^{2k}}{2k}$, where $H_k$ is defined by (3.1). Then, for $|x|<1$, $\displaystyle f\!\bigl(\frac{2x}{2-x}\bigr)=\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2(1-x)+\tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{Li}_2(x)$.

For $|z|\le 1$, define $g(z)=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_k z^{k+1}}{(k+1)^2}$, where $H_k$ is defined by (3.1). Then $g$ can be analytically continued to the entire complex plane, and $g(1-z)=\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 z\,\log(1-z)+\operatorname{Li}_2(z)\log z-\operatorname{Li}_3(z)+\zeta(3)$.
For $g$ as in Entry 9(i), $g(-z)+g\!\bigl(\tfrac{-1}{z}\bigr)=-\tfrac{1}{6}\log^3 z-\operatorname{Li}_2(-z)\log z+\operatorname{Li}_3(-z)+\zeta(3)$.

For $|z|\le 1$, define $h(z)=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_k z^{k+1}}{(k+1)^3}$. Then $h(z)$ can be analytically continued into the entire complex $z$-plane, and $h(1-z)-h\!\bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{z}\bigr)=-\tfrac{1}{4}\log^4 z+\tfrac{1}{6}\log^3 z\,\log(1-z)+\zeta(3)\log z-2\operatorname{Li}_4(z)+\operatorname{Li}_3(z)\log z+\tfrac{\pi^4}{45}$.
For $h$ as in Entry 10(i), $h(-z)-h\!\bigl(\tfrac{-1}{z}\bigr)=-\tfrac{1}{4}\log^4 z-\operatorname{Li}_3(-z)\log z+2\operatorname{Li}_4(-z)+\zeta(3)\log z+\tfrac{7\pi^4}{360}$.

For $-1\le x\le 1$, define $F(x)=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{h_k x^{2k}}{(2k)^2}$, where $h_k$ is defined by (8.2). Then for $0\le x\le 1$, $\displaystyle F\!\bigl(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\bigr)=\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 x\,\log\!\bigl(\tfrac{1-x}{1+x}\bigr)+h_2(x)\log x+\tfrac{1}{2}\chi_3(1)-\tfrac{1}{2}\chi_3(x)$.

For $F$ as in Entry 11(i) and $G$ defined by $xG'(x)=F(x)$, for $0\le x\le 1$, $\displaystyle G(x)+G\!\bigl(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\bigr)=F(x)\log x+F\!\bigl(\tfrac{1-x}{1+x}\bigr)\log\!\bigl(\tfrac{1-x}{1+x}\bigr)-\tfrac{1}{6}\log^2 x\,\log^2\!\bigl(\tfrac{1-x}{1+x}\bigr)+G(1)$.

For $|x|<1$, define $H(x)=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_k x^{2k-1}}{2k-1}$, where $H_k$ is defined by (3.1). Then for $0<x<1$, $\displaystyle H\!\bigl(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\bigr)=(\log 2-1)\log x+\tfrac{1}{2}\log\frac{1-x^2}{4}+\tfrac{\pi^2}{12}+\tfrac{1}{2}\log^2 x+\operatorname{Li}_2(-x)$.

If $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\int_0^x u^n\cot\!\bigl(\tfrac{u}{2}\bigr)\,du=\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{n\pi}{2}\bigr)\,n!\,\zeta(n+1)-\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=0}^{n}(-1)^{j}\frac{(j+1)^{-1}\Gamma(n+1)}{\Gamma(n+1-j)}\,x^{n-j}\operatorname{Cl}_{j+1}(x)$, where $\operatorname{Cl}_m$ is defined by (13.1)--(13.2).

If $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\int_0^x\frac{u^n}{2\csc u}\,du=\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{n\pi}{2}\bigr)\,n!\,x^{n+1}\chi_n(1)-\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=0}^{n}(-1)^{j}\frac{(j+1)^{-1}\Gamma(n+1)}{\Gamma(n+1-j)}\,x^{n-j}D_{j+1}(x)$, where $\chi_n$ is defined by (6.2) and $D_m$ by (14.1)--(14.2).
For each nonnegative integer $n$, define $f_n(x)=\int x^n\cot x\,dx$. Then if $n\ge 0$, $\displaystyle 2^n f_n(1-x)=\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^k\binom{n}{k}n^{n-k}\bigl\{f_k(2x)-2^k f_k(x)\bigr\}$.

$\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{2^{2k-1}(k!)^2}{(2k+1)!(2k+2)^2}=\tfrac{\pi^2}{64}\log 2+\tfrac{\pi}{8}G-\tfrac{5}{16}\chi_3(1)$, where $G$ denotes Catalan's constant and $\chi_3$ is defined by (6.2).

Let $x$, $y$, $\theta$, and $\varphi$ be real with $xe^{i\theta}+ye^{i\varphi}=1$, $0\le x,y\le 1$, and $-\pi<\theta,\varphi\le\pi$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{x^k\cos(k\theta)}{k^2}+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{y^k\cos(k\varphi)}{k^2}=\tfrac{\pi^2}{6}-\log x\,\log y+\theta\varphi$.
Under the hypotheses of Entry 24(i), $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{x^k\sin(k\theta)}{k^2}+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{y^k\sin(k\varphi)}{k^2}=-\varphi\log x-\theta\log y$.

Let $x$, $y$, $\theta$, and $\varphi$ be real with $xe^{i\theta}+ye^{i\varphi}=xye^{i(\theta+\varphi)}$, $0\le x,y\le 1$, and $-\pi<\theta,\varphi\le\pi$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{x^k\cos(k\theta)}{k^2}+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{y^k\cos(k\varphi)}{k^2}=\tfrac{1}{2}\log(1-2x\cos\theta+x^2)\,\log(1-2y\cos\varphi+y^2)-\tan^{-1}\!\bigl(\tfrac{x\sin\theta}{1-x\cos\theta}\bigr)\tan^{-1}\!\bigl(\tfrac{y\sin\varphi}{1-y\cos\varphi}\bigr)$.

Let $x$, $y$, $\theta$, and $\varphi$ be real with $xe^{i\theta}+ye^{i\varphi}+xye^{i(\theta+\varphi)}=1$, $0\le x,y\le 1$, and $-\pi<\theta,\varphi\le\pi$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^{2k+1}\cos(2k+1)\theta}{(2k+1)^2}+\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{y^{2k+1}\cos(2k+1)\varphi}{(2k+1)^2}=\tfrac{\pi^2}{8}-\tfrac{1}{2}\log x\,\log y+\tfrac{1}{2}\theta\varphi$.

Under the hypotheses of Entry 26(i), $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^{2k+1}\sin(2k+1)\theta}{(2k+1)^2}+\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{y^{2k+1}\sin(2k+1)\varphi}{(2k+1)^2}=-\tfrac{1}{2}\varphi\log x-\tfrac{1}{2}\theta\log y$.

Let $\varphi_r(x)=\sum_{k=1}^{x}k^r\log k$ for $r>-1$. For each nonnegative integer $k$, let $M_k(r)=\sum_{j=0}^{k}\frac{1}{r-j}$. Then there exists a constant $C_r$ such that as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)-\log x\Bigl\{\sum_{k=1}^{x}k^r-\zeta(-r)\Bigr\}\sim C_r-\frac{x^{r+1}}{(r+1)^2}+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{B_{2k}\Gamma(r+1)M_{2k-2}(r)\,x^{r-2k+1}}{(2k)!\,\Gamma(r-2k+2)}$, where $B_k$ denotes the $k$th Bernoulli number.

Let $C_r$ be as in Entry 27(a). Then if $r>0$, $\displaystyle C_r=\frac{2\Gamma(r+1)\zeta(r+1)}{(2\pi)^{r+1}}\Bigl\{\sin\!\bigl(\tfrac{\pi r}{2}\bigr)\log(2\pi)-\frac{\Gamma'(r+1)}{\Gamma(r+1)}-\tfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{\pi r}{2}\bigr)\Bigr\}+\frac{2\Gamma(r+1)\sin(\pi r/2)}{(2\pi)^{r+1}}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{\log k}{k^{r+1}}$.

If $r$ is an even positive integer, then $\displaystyle C_r=-\frac{\cos(\pi r/2)\Gamma(r+1)\zeta(r+1)}{2(2\pi)^r}$. In particular, $C_0=\tfrac{1}{2}\log(2\pi)$, $C_4=-\tfrac{3\zeta(5)}{4\pi^4}$, and $C_6=-\tfrac{45\zeta(7)}{32\pi^6}$.

$\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{\exp(x^2-\tfrac{1}{2}\log x+\tfrac{1}{8}-\gamma)\prod_{k=1}^{x}k^{2k}}{\bigl(x^x(x+3)\sqrt[3]{x^2+1}\bigr)^{2x/(x+3)}}=\exp\!\Bigl\{-\tfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{\log k}{k^2}\Bigr\}$ (27.13). (Berndt notes Ramanujan's notebook version is misprinted; the factor $i$ in a denominator may be omitted.)

If $|x|<1$ and $r$ is any positive integer, with $\varphi_r$ defined by (28.3), then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)=\frac{H_{r-1}}{r+1}\bigl\{B_{r+1}(x+1)-B_{r+1}\bigr\}+\gamma x^{r+1}-\sum_{1\le k\le(r+1)/2}\frac{B_{2k}H_{2k-1}x^{r+1-2k}}{2k(2k-1)(r+1)}-r!\sum_{k=0}^{r}\zeta(-k)\,c_k x^{r-k}+\sum_{k=2}^{\infty}(-1)^k\frac{r!}{(k-1)!}\,\zeta(k)\,x^{r+k}$ (28.8), where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant and $c_k=-\zeta(-k)$ for $k\ge 0$.

Let $\varphi_r(x)$ be defined by (28.3), let $C_r$ be defined by (27.7), let $-1<x\le 0$, and let $n$ and $r$ be natural numbers. Then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x)-n^r\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\varphi_r\!\bigl(x-\tfrac{k}{n}\bigr)=\frac{B_{r+1}(x+1)}{r+1}\log n+\Bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{n^{r+1}}\Bigr)C_r$.

If $n$ and $r$ are any positive integers, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\binom{k}{r}\log n-r\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}\varphi_r\!\bigl(-\tfrac{k}{n}\bigr)=-\frac{B_{r+1}}{(r+1)}\Bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{n^{r+1}}\Bigr)C_r$.
If $r$ is any positive integer, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r\!\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)=-\frac{B_{r+1}\log 2}{(r+1)2^r}+\Bigl(2-\tfrac{1}{2^r}\Bigr)C_r$.

Let $0<x<1$. If $r$ is positive and even, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x-1)+\varphi_r(-x)=2C_r+\frac{r!\zeta(r+1)}{(2\pi)^r}\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{\pi r}{2}\bigr)\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{\cos(2\pi kx)}{k^{r+1}}$.

Let $0<x<1$. If $r$ is positive and odd, then $\displaystyle\varphi_r(x-1)-\varphi_r(-x)=\frac{r!\zeta(r+1)}{(2\pi)^r}\sin\!\bigl(\tfrac{\pi r}{2}\bigr)\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{\sin(2\pi kx)}{k^{r+1}}$.

Suppose that $\varphi_1(x)$ is defined by (28.3), and define $\psi(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\binom{2k}{k}\frac{\sin^{2k+1}(\pi x)}{k\,2^{2k}(2k+1)^2}$. Then if $0<x\le\tfrac{1}{2}$, $\displaystyle\psi(x)=\pi\{\varphi_1(x-1)-\varphi_1(-x)\}+\pi x\log\!\bigl(2\sin(\pi x)\bigr)$.
Let $\psi(x)$ be defined by (31.1) and let $h_k$ be defined by (8.2). Then $\displaystyle\psi(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k h_{k+1}}{2k+1}\tan^{2k+1}(\pi x)$.

With $\psi$ as in Entry 31(b), for $0\le x<\tfrac{1}{2}$, $\displaystyle\psi(x)+\psi(\tfrac{1}{2}-x)=\tfrac{\pi}{2}\log\!\bigl(2\cos(\pi x)\bigr)+\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k\tan^{2k+1}(\pi x)}{(2k+1)^2}$.
With $\psi$ as in Entry 31(b), for $0\le x\le\tfrac{1}{4}$, $\displaystyle\psi(\tfrac{1}{4}-x)+\tfrac{1}{2}\psi(2x)-\psi(x)=\tfrac{\pi}{2}\log\!\bigl(2\cos(\pi x)\bigr)$.

With $\psi$ as in Entry 31(b), for $0\le x\le 1$, $\displaystyle\psi(\tfrac{1}{4}-x)+\psi(\tfrac{1}{4}+x)=\pi(1-2x)\log|2\cos(\pi x)|+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{k+1}\sin(2\pi kx)}{k^2}$ (Berndt's corrected form; Ramanujan's notebook omits the series term).
If $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2}x\cos^n x\sin(nx)\,dx=\frac{\pi}{2^{n+2}}\sum_{k=1}^{n}(-1)^{k+1}\binom{n}{k}\frac{1}{k}$.
If $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2}\cos^n x\sin(nx)\,dx=2^{n+1}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{k}$.
For each positive integer $n$, with $\varphi(x)=\int_1^2\frac{t^x-1}{t-1}\,dt$ as in (33.1), $\displaystyle\varphi(-n)=-\sum_{k=n}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k\,2^k}-H_{n-1}$.

Let $-\tfrac{1}{2}<x<1$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2k+1)^2}\binom{-x}{k+1}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k 2^{2k}(k!)^2 h_{k+1}}{(2k+1)!}\,x^{k+1}$ (34.1), where $h_k$ is defined by (8.2).

Define $K_r=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k^r(k+1)}$ and $A_n=(1+\cos(\pi n))\zeta(n)$, where $r$ is a positive integer and $n$ is any integer (with $A_1=0$, $A_n=0$ for $n<0$, and $A_0=-1$). Then for each positive integer $r$, $\displaystyle K_r=\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}\frac{A_{r-k}}{\binom{-k-1}{r}}$ (35.1).

Suppose that at least one of the quantities $x$, $y$, $z$, $u$, or $-x-y-z-u-2n-1$ is a positive integer. Then $\displaystyle{}_7F_6\!\left[\begin{array}{c}n,\tfrac12n+1,-x,-y,-z,-u,x+y+z+u+2n+1\\\tfrac12n,x+n+1,y+n+1,z+n+1,u+n+1,-x-y-z-u-n\end{array};1\right]$=\displaystyle\frac{\Gamma(x+n+1)\Gamma(y+n+1)\Gamma(z+n+1)\Gamma(u+n+1)\Gamma(x+y+z+n+1)}{\Gamma(n+1)\Gamma(x+y+n+1)\Gamma(y+z+n+1)\Gamma(x+u+n+1)\Gamma(z+u+n+1)}$$\displaystyle\times\frac{\Gamma(y+z+u+n+1)\Gamma(x+u+z+n+1)\Gamma(x+y+u+n+1)}{\Gamma(x+z+n+1)\Gamma(y+u+n+1)\Gamma(x+y+z+u+n+1)}$.

If either $x$, $y$, or $z$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle{}_3F_2\!\left[\begin{array}{c}-x,-y,-z\\n+1,-x-y-z-n\end{array};1\right]$=\displaystyle\frac{\Gamma(n+1)\Gamma(x+y+n+1)\Gamma(y+z+n+1)\Gamma(z+x+n+1)}{\Gamma(x+n+1)\Gamma(y+n+1)\Gamma(z+n+1)\Gamma(x+y+z+n+1)}$.

If $x$, $y$, $z$, or $-x-y-z-2n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle{}_6F_5\!\left[\begin{array}{c}\tfrac12n+1,1,-x,-y,-z,x+y+z+2n\\\tfrac12n,x+n+1,y+n+1,z+n+1,-x-y-z-n+1\end{array};1\right]$=\displaystyle\frac{(x+n)(y+n)(z+n)(x+y+z+n)}{n(x+y+n)(y+z+n)(x+z+n)}$.

If either $x$, $y$, $z$, or $-x-y-z-2n-1$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{(n+2k)(-x)_k(-y)_k(-z)_k(x+y+z+2n+1)_k}{k(n+k)(x+n+1)_k(y+n+1)_k(z+n+1)_k(-x-y-z-n)_k}$=\psi(x+n+1)+\psi(y+n+1)+\psi(z+n+1)+\psi(x+y+z+n+1)-\psi(n+1)-\psi(x+y+n+1)-\psi(y+z+n+1)-\psi(z+x+n+1)$.
If $x$ is a positive integer, then $1-\dfrac{3(x-1)^3(4x-1)}{x+1}+\dfrac{5((x-1)(x-2))^3(4x-1)(4x)}{(x+1)(x+2)(4x-3)(4x-4)}+\cdots$=\dfrac{\Gamma^4(x+1)\Gamma^4(3x-1)}{\Gamma^6(2x)\Gamma(4x-2)}$.

If $x$ is an odd positive integer, then $\dfrac{(x-1)^3(3x-1)}{x+1}+\dfrac{((x-1)(x-3))^3(3x-1)(3x+1)}{2(x+1)(x+3)(3x-3)(3x-5)}+\cdots$=\dfrac12\{\psi(\tfrac{x+1}{2})+3\psi(\tfrac{x+1}{3})-3\psi(x)-\psi(1)\}$.
If $x$ is a nonnegative integer, then $1+\dfrac{(\frac{x}{2})^2}{3x}+\dfrac{(\frac{x(x-1)}{2})^2}{3x(3x-1)}+\cdots$=\dfrac{\Gamma^3(2x+1)}{\Gamma^3(x+1)\Gamma(3x+1)}$.
If $x$ is a positive integer, then $1+\dfrac{x}{x+1}+\dfrac{x(x-1)}{2!}\dfrac{(x-1)(x-2)}{(x+1)(x+2)}+\cdots$=\dfrac{8\Gamma^3(3x+1)\Gamma(x+1)}{9\Gamma^3(2x+1)\Gamma(4x+1)}$.

If $\alpha+\beta+\gamma+1=n$, then as $m\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\frac{\Gamma(n+2)\Gamma(\alpha+1)\Gamma(\beta+1)\Gamma(\gamma+1)}{\Gamma(n-\alpha+1)\Gamma(n-\beta+1)\Gamma(n-\gamma+1)}$\displaystyle\times{}_5F_4\!\left[\begin{array}{c}\tfrac12(n+3),n+1,\alpha+1,\beta+1,\gamma+1\\\tfrac12(n+1),n-\alpha+1,n-\beta+1,n-\gamma+1\end{array};m\right]$\displaystyle\sim 2\log m-\psi(\alpha+1)-\psi(\beta+1)-\psi(\gamma+1)-\gamma_E$, where $\gamma_E$ denotes Euler's constant.

If $a$, $b$, $c$, and $a+b+c$ are not nonpositive integers, then as $m\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\frac{\Gamma(a+b+c)\Gamma(a)\Gamma(b)\Gamma(c)}{\Gamma(b+c)\Gamma(a+c)\Gamma(a+b)}$\displaystyle\times{}_5F_4\!\left[\begin{array}{c}\tfrac12(a+b+c+1),a+b+c-1,a,b,c\\\tfrac12(a+b+c-1),b+c,a+c,a+b\end{array};m\right]$\displaystyle=2\log m-\gamma_E-\psi(a)-\psi(b)-\psi(c)+O\!\left(\frac{\log m}{m}\right)$, where $\gamma_E$ denotes Euler's constant.
If $x$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(1-x)_k}{(k+1)^2(1+x)_k}$=\dfrac{x}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k+x}+\dfrac{x}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k^2}$.

Suppose that $f(x)=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}A_k x^k/k$ in some neighborhood of the origin. Define $P_k$, $0\le k<\infty$, by $e^{f(x)}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}P_k x^k$. Then $P_0=1$ and, for $n\ge 1$, $nP_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}A_k P_{n-k}$.

For positive integers $n$ and $r$, define $S_r=S_r(n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k$ and $\Omega_r=\Omega_r(n)=\sum_{1\le k_1<\cdots<k_r\le n}a_{k_1}\cdots a_{k_r}$, where $a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$ are arbitrary nonzero complex numbers. Then, if $r\ge 1$ and $\Omega_0=1$, $r\Omega_r=\sum_{k=1}^{r}(-1)^{k+1}S_k\Omega_{r-k}$.

If $\operatorname{Re}x>-1$ and $r$ is any positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-x)_k}{(n+k)^{r+1}k!}$=\dfrac{\Gamma(n)\Gamma(x+1)}{\Gamma(n+x+1)}\varphi(r)$, where $S_r=S_r(n,x)$ and $\varphi(r)=\varphi(n,x,r)$ are defined by (13.1) and (13.2).

Let $S_r(n,x)$ and $\varphi(n,x,r)$ be defined by (13.1) and (13.2), respectively. If $n=1$ and $x=-1$, then $S_1=2\log 2$, $S_r=(2^r-2)\zeta(r)$ for $r\ge 2$, and $\dfrac{1}{3^{r+1}}+\dfrac{(\frac12)}{5^{r+1}}+\dfrac{(\frac12\cdot\frac32)}{7^{r+1}\,2\cdot 4}+\cdots$=\dfrac{\pi}{2^{r+1}}\varphi(r)$ for $r\ge 1$.

Let $S_r$ and $\varphi(r)$ be defined by (13.1) and (13.2), respectively, with $n=1$ and $x=-\tfrac12$. Then $S_1=2-2\log 2$, $S_r=(2-2^r)\zeta(r)+2^r$ for $r\ge 2$, and $\dfrac{1}{2^{r+1}}+\dfrac{(\frac12)}{3^{r+1}}+\dfrac{(\frac12\cdot\frac32)}{5^{r+1}\,2\cdot 4}+\cdots$=2\varphi(r)$ for $r\ge 1$.

Let $n$ be an integer with $n\ge 2$. Then $\displaystyle 2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{k(k+x)^n}\sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\frac{1}{j}$=nS_{n+1}(x)-\sum_{r=1}^{n-2}S_{r+1}(x)S_{n-r}(x)$-2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(k+x)^n}\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\frac{1}{j+x}$+2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\frac{1}{(j+x)^n}\left(\frac{1}{k-j}-\frac{1}{k+x}\right)$, where $S_r(x)=S_r(n,x)$ is defined by (13.1).

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are arbitrary complex numbers, then as $n\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+k+1)\Gamma(\beta+k+1)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta+k+2)k!}\sim\log n-\psi(\alpha+1)-\psi(\beta+1)-\gamma_E$.
If $A_0,A_1,\ldots,A_r$ are any complex numbers and $P_r=\sum_{k=0}^{r}A_k(-1)^k\binom{r}{k}$ for $r\ge 0$, then $A_r=\sum_{k=0}^{r}(-1)^{r-k}\binom{r}{k}P_k$.

Suppose that $f(x)=f(r,x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(r)_k A_k}{k!\,x^{r+k}}$ is analytic for $|x|>R$. For $|x|>\max(R,|h|)$, write $f(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(r)_k B_k}{k!\,(x+h)^{r+k}}$. Then $B_n=\sum_{k=0}^{n}\binom{n}{k}A_{n-k}(-h)^k$ for $n\ge 0$.

Suppose that (17.1) holds. Assume also that $f(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k(r)_k A_k}{k!\,x^{r+k}}$ for $|x|>\max(R,1)$. Furthermore, assume that $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}A_k x^k/k!$ is analytic for $|x|<R^*$. Then for $|x|<R^*$, $\displaystyle e^x\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{A_k(-x)^k}{k!}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{A_k x^k}{k!}$.

Suppose that (17.1) and (18.1) hold. Then $\displaystyle\frac{1}{\varphi'(x)}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(r)_k A_k}{k!}\left\{\frac{\varphi(x)-\varphi(-x)}{\varphi(x)}\right\}^k$ is an even function of $x$.

Suppose that (17.1) and (18.1) hold. Then, if $n$ is an even integer with $n\ge 2$, $\displaystyle\frac{1}{n}A_{n-1}=\sum_{k=1}^{n/2}\binom{n}{2k}(2^{2k}-1)B_{2k}A_{n-2k}$, where $B_j$ denotes the $j$th Bernoulli number.

Let $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=\sum_{r=0}^{\infty}\frac{\varphi^{(r)}(1)}{r!}(x-1)^r$ be analytic for $|x-1|<R$, where $R>1$. Suppose that $m$ and $n$ are complex parameters such that the order of summation in $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\sum_{r=k}^{\infty}$ may be inverted. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(m)_k\varphi^{(k)}(0)}{(n)_k k!}$=\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k(n-m)_k\varphi^{(k)}(1)}{(n)_k k!}$.
