If $\alpha\beta=\pi$, then (i) $\displaystyle\frac{\alpha^{1/4}}{\beta^{1/4}}e^{-\pi\alpha^2/(4\beta)}\varphi(e^{-\pi\alpha^2/\beta})$=e^{\pi\beta/4}\varphi(e^{-\pi\beta})$; (ii) $\displaystyle\frac{\alpha^{1/4}}{\beta^{1/4}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi\alpha^2}{12\beta}\right)\bigl\{\varphi(e^{-\pi\alpha^2/(3\beta)})-\varphi(-e^{-\pi\alpha^2/\beta})\bigr\}$=\exp\!\left(\frac{\pi\beta}{12}\right)\bigl\{\varphi(e^{-\pi\beta/3})-\varphi(-e^{-\pi\beta})\bigr\}$; If $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, then (iii) $\displaystyle e^{-\pi i\alpha/12}\sqrt[4]{\beta}\,f(\sqrt{\beta}\,e^{-2\pi i\alpha})$=e^{-\pi\beta/12}\sqrt[4]{\alpha}\,f(-e^{-2\pi\beta})$; (iv) $\displaystyle e^{-\pi i\alpha/24}\sqrt[4]{\beta}\,f(e^{-\pi i\alpha})$=e^{-\pi\beta/24}\sqrt[4]{\alpha}\,f(e^{-\pi\beta})$; and (v) $\displaystyle\frac{e^{-\pi i\alpha/24}\chi(e^{-\pi i\alpha})}{f(e^{-\pi i\alpha})}$=\displaystyle\frac{e^{-\pi\beta/24}\chi(e^{-\pi\beta})}{f(e^{-\pi\beta})}$.
If $\rho=ab$ and $n$ is any natural number, then $\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{n}(-ap^{k-1};p^n)_\infty(-bp^{n-k};p^n)_\infty(p^n;p^n)_\infty$=\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{n}f(ap^{k-1},bp^{n-k})$.
We have $f(-q^2,-q^3)f(-q,-q^4)=f(-q)f(-q^5)$ and $\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{n}f(-q^k,-q^{2n+1-k})=f(-q)^{n-1}f(-q^{2n+1})$ for each positive integer $n$.

Let $U_n=a^{n(n+1)/2}b^{n(n-1)/2}$ and $V_n=a^{n(n-1)/2}b^{n(n+1)/2}$ for each integer $n$. Then $\displaystyle f\!\left(\frac{U_1}{U_n},\frac{V_1}{V_n}\right)$=\displaystyle\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}\frac{U_r}{U_n}f\!\left(\frac{U_{n+r}}{U_r},\frac{V_{n-r}}{V_r}\right)$.
For each natural number $n$, the coefficient of $q^n$ in the expansion of $\displaystyle\frac{q}{1-q}\,\psi(q^2)$ is the integer nearest to $\sqrt{n}$.

For each positive integer $n$, let $P_n=-\dfrac{B_n}{2n}+\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{k+1}k^{n-1}q^k}{k(1-q^k)}$, where $B_n$ denotes the $n$th Bernoulli number. For each nonnegative integer $n$, let $Q_n=\dfrac{1}{n+1}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{k+1}(2k-1)^{2n+1}q^{k(k-1)/2}}{k}$. Then for each positive integer $n$, $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n}\binom{2n-1}{2j-1}2^{2j+1}P_{2j}Q_{2n-2j}=2Q_{2n}$.

For each positive integer $n$, let $P_n=\dfrac{(1-2n)B_n}{2n}+\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{k+1}k^{n-1}q^k}{k(1+q^k)}$, where $B_n$ denotes the $n$th Bernoulli number. For each nonnegative integer $n$, define $Q_{2n}$ by $\displaystyle\sec x=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n E_{2n}}{(2n)!}x^{2n}$, where $E_{2n}$ denotes the $2n$th Euler number. If $n$ is any positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\binom{2n-1}{2k-1}2^{2k}P_{2k}Q_{2n-2k}=2Q_{2n}$.

Let $Q_n$, $n\ge 2$, be defined as in Entry 35(i). Let $L=1-24\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{kq^k}{1-q^k}$, $M=1+240\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{k^3q^k}{1-q^k}$, and $N=1-504\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{k^5q^k}{1-q^k}$. Then $3Q_2=L$.

Let $n$ and $x$ be real numbers with $0\le x<1$. Then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{\cos\{(1-2n)\sin^{-1}(\sqrt{x}\sin\varphi)\}}{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}}\,d\varphi=\frac{\pi}{2}\,{}_2F_1(1-n,n;1;x)$.

For $0<x<1$, let $\displaystyle U_x=\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{\cos\{(1-2n)\sin^{-1}(\sqrt{x}\sin\varphi)\}}{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}}\,d\varphi$. If $0<x<1$ and $n$ is nonintegral, then $\displaystyle\exp\!\left(-\frac{n\,U_{1-x}}{\sin(\pi n)}\right)=x\,\exp(\psi(n)+\psi(1-n)+2\gamma)$, where $\psi(x)=\Gamma'(x)/\Gamma(x)$ and $\gamma$ is Euler's constant.

Define $\displaystyle F(x)=\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi\,{}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;x)}{{}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;1-x)}\right)$ for $0<x<1$, extended by $F(0)=0$ and $F(1)=1$. If $0<x<1$, then $\displaystyle F(x)=\exp\!\left(\frac{\pi}{16\,{}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;x)}\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(k!)^2}\sum_{j=1}^{k}\frac{x^k}{2j(2j-1)}\right)$.

If $m$ is a nonnegative integer and $n=2^m$, then $\displaystyle\frac{F\!\left(\dfrac{\varphi^4(-q)}{\varphi^4(q)}\right)}{F\!\left(\dfrac{\varphi^4(-q^n)}{\varphi^4(q^n)}\right)}=n\,\frac{{}_2F_1(\tfrac12,\tfrac12;1;\varphi^4(-q)/\varphi^4(q))}{{}_2F_1(\tfrac12,\tfrac12;1;\varphi^4(-q^n)/\varphi^4(q^n))}$.
(Inversion formula.) For $|q|<1$, $\displaystyle F\!\left(1-\frac{\varphi^4(-q)}{\varphi^4(q)}\right)=q$, where $\displaystyle\varphi(q)=\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}q^{k^2}$ and $F$ is defined by (2.1).

Let $\displaystyle F(x)=\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi\,{}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;x)}{{}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;1-x)}\right)$ and $\displaystyle\varphi(q)=\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}q^{k^2}$. Then $\varphi^2(F(x))={}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;x)$. If furthermore $q=e^{-y}$, $\displaystyle z={}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;x)$, and $\displaystyle y=\frac{\pi\,{}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;1-x)}{{}_2F_1(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2};1;x)}$, then $\varphi^2(e^{-y})=z$.
Recall that $x$, $y$, and $z$ are related by (2.1) and (6.2)--(6.4). Then $\displaystyle\frac{dy}{dx}=x(1-x)z^2$.
If $x$, $y$, and $z$ are related by (6.2)--(6.4), then $\varphi(e^{-y})=\sqrt{z}$.
If $x$ is changed to $\bigl(\tfrac{1-\sqrt{x}}{1+\sqrt{x}}\bigr)^2$, then $y$ is changed to $2y$.
If $x$ is changed to $-x/(1-x)$, then $e^{-y}$ is changed to $-e^{-y}$.
If $n$ is a positive integer, then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^k}{(2k+1)\cosh(\tfrac{1}{2}(2k+1)n\pi/\sqrt{3})}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{24n}$.
If the left side of Entry 16(iv) vanishes, then $x=\tfrac{1}{2}$ or $x(1-x)=\tfrac{1}{16}$, and $\displaystyle\chi(e^{-y})=2^{1/6}\{x(1-x)e^y\}^{-1/24}$.
If the left side of Entry 16(v) vanishes, then $x(1-x)^{-1}=616\pm 24\sqrt{645}$, and $\displaystyle\chi(e^{-y})=2^{1/6}\{x(1-x)e^y\}^{-1/24}$.
If the left side of Entry 16(vi) vanishes, then $x=\tfrac{1}{2}$ or $x(1-x)^{-1}=16$ or $11056$, and $\displaystyle\chi(e^{-y})=2^{1/6}\{x(1-x)e^y\}^{-1/24}$.

Recall from Section 9 of Chapter 15 the definition $L(e^{-2y})=1-24\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{n}{e^{ny}-1}$. In the notation of Section 6 of Chapter 17, $\displaystyle{}_2F_1\!\left(-\tfrac12,-\tfrac12;1;x\right)=z(1-x)+\int_0^x\frac{z}{x}\,dx=\frac{3(1+x)}{2}+\frac{1}{3z}\,L(e^{-2y})$.
In the same notation as Entry 1, $\displaystyle{}_2F_1\!\left(-\tfrac12,\tfrac12;1;x\right)=z(1-x)+\int_0^x\frac{z}{x}\,dx=\frac{2-x}{3}+\frac{1}{3z}\,L(e^{-2y})$.

Consider the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ with eccentricity $e=\frac{1}{a}\sqrt{a^2-b^2}$. If $L=L(a,b)$ denotes the perimeter, then $L=2\pi a\,{}_2F_1\!\left(\tfrac12,-\tfrac12;1;e^2\right)=\pi(a+b)\,{}_2F_1\!\left(-\tfrac12,-\tfrac12;1;t\right)=\pi\{3(a+b)-\sqrt{(a+3b)(3a+b)}\}$ nearly, $=\pi(a+b)\left\{1+\frac{3t}{10+4\sqrt{1-3t}}\right\}$ very nearly, where $t=\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right)^2$ in the last two formulas.
According to Ramanujan, $\pi=\frac{355}{113}\left(1-\frac{0.0003}{3533}\right)$ very nearly, and $\pi\approx\left(97+\tfrac34-\tfrac14\right)^{1/4}$ nearly.

Let $x$ and $y$ be as in Section 6 of Chapter 17. Then $\displaystyle\sqrt{x}\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n}{n!}x^n=-2\sum_{n=0}^\infty(-1)^n(2n+1)\log\frac{1+e^{-(2n+1)y/2}}{1-e^{-(2n+1)y/2}}$.
Let $x$ and $y$ be as in Entry 4. Then $\displaystyle\log\frac{16}{x}-\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2}{n!^2}x^n=y-4\sum_{n=0}^\infty(-1)^n(2n+1)\log(1-e^{-(2n+1)y})$.
With $x$, $y$, and $z$ as in Section 6 of Chapter 17, $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y\}}=\frac{z}{2}\,{}_3F_2(1,1,1;\tfrac32,\tfrac32;x)$.
Let $x$ and $y$ be as above. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{1}{(2n+1)\sinh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y\}}=\frac14\log\frac{1+\sqrt{x}}{1-\sqrt{x}}$.

Let $\varepsilon$ be real. If $|\varepsilon|<\pi$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\{\cos(2n+1)\varepsilon+2\cos\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\varepsilon\}\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\sqrt3\,\varepsilon\}\}}{(2n+1)\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\pi\sqrt3\}}=-\frac{\pi}{8}$; if $|\varepsilon|<\pi/2$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\cos(2n+1)\varepsilon\{\cos(2n+1)\varepsilon+\cosh((2n+1)\sqrt3\,\varepsilon)\}}{(2n+1)\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\pi\sqrt3\}}=\frac{\pi}{12}$, $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\sin(2n+1)\varepsilon\{\cos(2n+1)\varepsilon-\cosh((2n+1)\sqrt3\,\varepsilon)\}}{(2n+1)^4\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\pi\sqrt3\}}=-\frac{\pi^3\varepsilon^3}{12}$, and $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\cos(2n+1)\varepsilon\{\cos(2n+1)\varepsilon+\cosh((2n+1)\sqrt3\,\varepsilon)\}}{(2n+1)^7\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\pi\sqrt3\}}=\frac{\pi^7\varepsilon^6}{11520}$.

If $z\neq\pm\omega^j(2n+1)$ for $0\le j\le 2$, $0\le n<\infty$, where $\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n(2n+1)^5}{\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\pi\sqrt3\}\{(2n+1)^6-z^6\}}=-\frac{1}{12\cos(\tfrac{z}{3})\{\cos(\tfrac{z}{3})+\cosh(\tfrac{\sqrt3\,z}{3})\}}$.

If $z\neq\pm\omega^j(2n+1)$ for $0\le j\le 2$, $0\le n<\infty$, where $\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n(2n+1)^5}{\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)\pi\sqrt3\}\{(2n+1)^6-z^6\}}=\frac{z^4}{12}\frac{\cosh(\tfrac{\sqrt3\,z}{3})\{\cos(\tfrac{z}{3})+\cosh(\tfrac{\sqrt3\,z}{3})\}-3}{\cos(\tfrac{z}{3})\{\cos(\tfrac{z}{3})+\cosh(\tfrac{\sqrt3\,z}{3})\}}$.

(i) If $|\operatorname{Im}\,\theta|<\pi$, then $\displaystyle\left\{1+2\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\cos(n\theta)}{\cosh(n\pi)}\right\}^{-1}+\left\{1+2\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\cosh(n\theta)}{\cosh(n\pi)}\right\}^{-1}=\frac{2}{\Gamma^4(\tfrac14)}$. (ii) Let $x'$, $y'$, and $z'$ denote the parameters associated with the complementary modulus $k'$. If $|\operatorname{Im}\,\theta|<y/2$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\cos(2n+1)\theta}{\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y\}}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\cosh(2n+1)\theta}{\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y'\}}=\frac{zz'}{4\sqrt{xx'}}$.

If $\eta>0$, then (i) $\displaystyle\frac12+\sum_{j=1}^\infty\frac{\operatorname{sech}(jy)}{1+(j\eta)^2}=\frac{(\eta z)^2}{2}+\frac{(\eta z)^2}{2^2}+\frac{(2\eta z)^2}{3^2}+\frac{(3\eta z)^2}{4^2}+\cdots$, and (ii) $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{\operatorname{sech}\{\tfrac12(2j+1)y\}}{1+(2j+1)^2\eta^2}=\frac{z\sqrt{x}}{(\eta z)^2}+\frac{z\sqrt{x}}{(2\eta z)^2}+\frac{z\sqrt{x}}{(3\eta z)^2}+\cdots$.

If $\eta>0$, then (i) $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^j\operatorname{csch}\{\tfrac12(2j+1)y\}}{1+(2j+1)^2\eta^2}=\frac{z\sqrt{x}}{(1-x)(\eta z)^2}+\frac{z\sqrt{x}}{(2\eta z)^2}+\cdots$; (ii) $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^j(2j+1)\operatorname{sech}\{\tfrac12(2j+1)y\}}{1+(2j+1)^2\eta^2}=\frac{z^2\sqrt{x(1-x)}}{1+(\eta z)^2(1-2x)}+\frac{2^2(2^2-1)x(1-x)(\eta z)^4}{1+(3\eta z)^2(1-2x)}+\cdots$; (iii) $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{(2j+1)\operatorname{csch}\{\tfrac12(2j+1)y\}}{1+(2j+1)^2\eta^2}=\frac{z^2\sqrt{x}}{1+(\eta z)^2(1+x)}-\frac{2^2(2^2-1)x(\eta z)^4}{1+(3\eta z)^2(1+x)}+\cdots$, where in (i) and (ii), $0<x<1/\sqrt2$.

If $\eta>0$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^j(2j+1)\operatorname{sech}\{\tfrac12(2j+1)\eta\}}{1+(2j+1)^2\eta^2}=\frac{\mu}{\eta^2}+\frac{\mu^2+1\cdot 3(\eta\mu)^4}{6}+\frac{15\cdot 21(\eta\mu)^4}{10}+\cdots$, where $\mu=\ln/\Gamma^2(\tfrac34)$ in Ramanujan's notation.

Define, for real $\theta$, $S(\theta)=\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{\sin((2j+1)\theta)}{\sinh\{\tfrac12(2j+1)y\}}$, $C(\theta)=\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{\cos((2j+1)\theta)}{\cosh\{\tfrac12(2j+1)y\}}$, and $C_1(\theta)=\tfrac12+\sum_{j=1}^\infty\frac{\cos(2j\theta)}{\cosh(jy)}$, so that $S=\tfrac12 z\sqrt{x}\,\mathrm{sn}(z\theta)$, $C=\tfrac12 z\sqrt{x}\,\mathrm{cn}(z\theta)$, and $C_1=\tfrac12 z\,\mathrm{dn}(z\theta)$. Then $C^2+S^2=\tfrac14 xz^2$, $C_1^2+S^2=\tfrac14 z^2$, $CS=\sum_{j=1}^\infty\frac{j\sin(2j\theta)}{\cosh(jy)}$, $\frac{dC_1}{d\theta}+2CS=0$, and $\frac{dS}{d\theta}=2CC_1$. Define $\varphi$, $0\le\varphi<2\pi$, by $C=\tfrac12 z\sqrt{x}\cos\varphi$ and $S=\tfrac12 z\sqrt{x}\sin\varphi$. Then $C_1=\tfrac12 z\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}$, $\displaystyle\frac{z\cos\varphi}{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}}=\frac{d\sin\varphi}{d\varphi}=\cos\varphi\,\frac{d\varphi}{d\theta}$, and $\displaystyle\frac{z\sqrt{x}\cos\varphi}{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}}=\frac{d\theta}{d\varphi}$.

Let $\varphi$ be defined by Entry 14. Then (i) $\displaystyle 1+2\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\cos(2n\theta)}{\cosh(ny)}=\frac{z\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}}$; (ii) $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\cos((2n+1)\theta)}{\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y\}}=\frac{2z}{\pi}\sqrt{x}\cos\varphi$; (iii) $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\sin((2n+1)\theta)}{\sinh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y\}}=\frac{2z}{\pi}\sqrt{x}\sin\varphi$; (iv) $\displaystyle\theta+\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{n\cosh(ny)}=\varphi$; (v) $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\sin((2n+1)\theta)}{(2n+1)\cosh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y\}}=\frac{2}{\pi}\sin^{-1}(\sqrt{yx}\sin\varphi)$; (vi) $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\cos((2n+1)\theta)}{(2n+1)\sinh\{\tfrac12(2n+1)y\}}$=-\frac12\log\frac{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}-\sqrt{x}\cos\varphi}{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}+\sqrt{x}\cos\varphi}$ (Berndt notes Ramanujan omitted the minus sign).

Suppose $\theta$ and $\varphi$ are related as in Entry 14. If $\theta$ is replaced by $\pi-\theta$ in any formula involving $\theta$, the corresponding conversions for functions of $\varphi$ are: $\cot\varphi\to\sqrt{1-x}\tan\varphi$; $\sin\varphi\to\cos\varphi$; $\cos\varphi\to\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}$; $\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}\to\frac{\sin\varphi}{\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}}$.

Let $\theta$ and $\varphi$ be related by Entry 14. Define $L$ as in Section 9 of Chapter 15 by $L(e^{-2y})=1-24\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{2n}{e^{ny}-1}$. Then (i) $\displaystyle\frac{\cos\theta}{\sin^3\theta}+16\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{n^2\sin(2n\theta)}{e^{2ny}-1}=-\frac{2z^2\cos\varphi}{\sin^3\varphi\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}}$; (ii) $\displaystyle\frac{z^2}{\sin^2\theta}-8\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{n\cos(2n\theta)}{e^{2ny}-1}=\frac{z^2}{\sin^2\varphi}-\frac{3z^2(1+x)}{2}+3L(e^{-2y})$; (iii) $\displaystyle\cot\theta+4\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{e^{2ny}-1}$=z\cot\varphi\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\varphi}+\frac{z}{\pi}\int_0^\varphi\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\psi}\,d\psi-\frac{2z}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2}\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\psi}\,d\psi$; (iv) $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{\sinh(ny)}$=-\frac{z}{\pi}\int_0^\varphi\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\psi}\,d\psi+\frac{z}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2}\sqrt{1-x\sin^2\psi}\,d\psi$.

If $\theta$ is replaced by $\tfrac{\theta}{2}$ and $y$ by $\tfrac{y}{2}$ in (18.1), then $\eta\!\left(\frac{4\sqrt{x}}{1+x},e^{-y/2},(1+\sqrt{x})z,\tfrac{\theta}{2},\tfrac12\{\varphi+\sin^{-1}(\sqrt{x}\sin\varphi)\}\right)=0$.
If $\theta$ is replaced by $\pi-\theta$ and $e^{-y}$ by $-e^{-y}$, then $\eta\!\left(\frac{x}{1-x},-e^{-y},z\sqrt{1-x},\pi-\theta,\pi-\varphi\right)=0$.

If $e^{-y}$ is replaced by $-e^{-y}$, then $\eta\!\left(\frac{x}{1-x},-e^{-y},z\sqrt{1-x},\theta,\frac{\cot\varphi}{\sqrt{1-x}}\right)=0$ (Ramanujan incorrectly wrote $\frac{\cot\varphi}{\sqrt{x-1}}$).

Let $z'=2F_1(\tfrac12,\tfrac12;1;1-x)$ and $y'=\pi/z'$. If $\theta$ is replaced by $i\theta z/z'$ and $y$ by $y'$, then $\eta(1-x,e^{-y'},z',i\theta z/z',i\log\tan(\tfrac{\pi}{4}+\tfrac{\varphi}{2}))=0$. Furthermore, $\sin\varphi$ is converted to $i\tan\varphi$ and $\cos\varphi$ to $\sec\varphi$.

Let an ellipse of eccentricity $e$ be given by $x=a\cos\varphi$, $y=b\sin\varphi$, $0\le\varphi\le 2\pi$. Let $P=(a\cos\varphi,b\sin\varphi)$ and $A=(a,0)$. Then the arc length $L(AP)=a\int_0^\varphi\sqrt{1-e^2\cos^2\psi}\,d\psi$.

Let a hyperbola of eccentricity $e$ be given by $x=a\sec\varphi$, $y=b\tan\varphi$, $0\le\varphi\le 2\pi$. Let $P=(a\sec\varphi,b\tan\varphi)$ and $A=(a,0)$. Then $L(AP)=a\tan\varphi\sqrt{e^2-\cos^2\varphi}-a\int_0^\varphi\sqrt{e^2-\cos^2\psi}\,d\psi$.

Let the perimeter $L$ of the ellipse $x=a\cos t$, $y=b\sin t$, $0\le t\le 2\pi$, be given by $L=\pi(a+b)(1+4\sin^2\theta)$, $0\le\theta\le\pi$, where $\sin\theta=\sqrt{x}\sin\varphi$ and $x=\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right)^2$. Then when $e=1$, $\varphi=30^\circ18'6''$, and as $e\to 0$, $\varphi$ tends monotonically to $30^\circ$.

Consider the same ellipse as in Entry 19(iii), but now set $L=\pi(a+b)\left(1+\frac{\sin^2\theta}{2+\cos^2\theta}\right)$, $0\le\theta\le\pi$, where $\sin\theta=\sqrt{x}\sin\varphi$ and $x=\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right)^2$. Then when $e=1$, $\varphi=60^\circ4'55''$, and when $e=0$, $\varphi=60^\circ$.

Let the perimeter $L$ of an ellipse be given by $L=\pi(a+b)\frac{\tan\theta}{\theta}$, $0<\theta<\pi/2$, where $\tan\theta=\sqrt{x}\cos\varphi$ and $x=\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right)^2$. As $e$ increases from $0$ to $1$, $\varphi$ decreases from $\pi/6$ to $0$. Furthermore, $\varphi$ is approximately $\frac{\pi}{6}-x(1-x)\left(11'22''+32'42''x\right)$.

Let $L(UV)$ denote the length of an arc $UV$ of an ellipse or circle. Suppose $PN$ is rotated until $\frac{L(AI)}{L(AB)}=\frac{L(AK)}{AN}$. Then $\varphi$ is approximately $30^\circ-x(1-x)(11'22''+32'42''x)$, where $x=\left(\frac{a-b}{a+b}\right)^2$ as before.

Let $O$ be the center and $PR$ a diameter of a circle. Bisect $OP$ at $H$ and trisect $OR$ at $T$ with $OT=2TR$. Let $TQ\perp OR$ with $Q$ on the circle, $RS=TQ$ with $S$ on arc $QR$, $OM\parallel RS$ and $TN\parallel RS$ with $M,N$ on $PS$, $PK=PM$ with $K$ on the circle opposite $Q$ and $S$, $PL\perp OP$ of length $MN$ on the same side of $PR$ as $K$, $RC=RH$ with $C$ on $RK$, and $CD\parallel KL$ with $D$ on $RL$. Then $RD=\frac{3}{4}RL$. Ramanujan asserts that the area of the circle is approximately $RD^2$, giving $\pi\approx 355/113$.

Inscribe an equilateral triangle of side $t$ in the circle of Entry 20(i). Let $m$ denote the first of two mean proportionals between $t$ and $PS$. Then $m/(t\sqrt{d/\pi})$ differs from unity by approximately $1/30000$.
If $\pi$ is approximated by $(97\pi-\sqrt{1})^{1/4}$ in the expression $\tfrac12 d\sqrt{\pi}$, then for a circle of one million square miles the error is approximately $1/100$th of an inch.
Parametric solutions of $A^3+B^3=C^2$ are given by $A=3n^3+6n^2-n$, $B=-3n^3+6n^2+n$, and $C=n^2(18n^2+6)$, where $n$ is arbitrary.

Parametric solutions of $A^3+B^3+C^3=D^3$ are given by $A=m^7-3(p+1)m^4+(3p^2+6p+2)m$, $B=2m^6-3(2p+1)m^3+(3p^2+3p+1)$, $C=m^6-(3p^2+3p+1)$, and $D=m^7-3pm^4+(3p^2-1)m$, where $m$ and $p$ are arbitrary.

Let $x$ be any complex number except at poles of the functions below. Then (i) $\displaystyle\frac{\pi}{\sqrt3\,x^2}\frac{\cosh(\pi\sqrt3\,x)+2\cos(\pi x)}{\cosh(\pi\sqrt3\,x)-\cos(\pi x)}=\frac{2}{\sqrt3\,x^4}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k k}{x^4+k^2x^2+k^4}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k k}{(x^4+k^2x^2+k^4)(e^{k\pi\sqrt3}-(-1)^k)}$; (ii) $\displaystyle\frac{\pi}{\sqrt3\,x^2}\frac{\cosh(\pi\sqrt3\,x)+2\cos(\pi x)+6\cosh(x/\sqrt3)}{\cosh(\pi\sqrt3\,x)-\cos(\pi x)}$=\frac{2}{\sqrt3\,x^4}-\frac{2}{\pi x^4}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k k}{x^4+k^2x^2+k^4}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k p(k)}{(x^4+k^2x^2+k^4)(e^{k\pi\sqrt3}-(-1)^k)}$, where $p(k)=\frac{2k}{\sinh(\tfrac12 k\pi\sqrt3)\cosh(\tfrac12 k\pi\sqrt3)}$ if $k$ is even and $p(k)=\frac{2k}{\cosh(\tfrac12 k\pi\sqrt3)\sinh(\tfrac12 k\pi\sqrt3)}$ if $k$ is odd; (iii) $\displaystyle\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt3\,x^2}\frac{e^{2\pi\sqrt3\,x}-2e^{\pi\sqrt3\,x}\cos(\pi x)+1}=\frac{2}{\sqrt3\,x^4}+\frac{1}{2x^3}+\frac{1}{3\sqrt3\,x^2}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k k}{(x^4+k^2x^2+k^4)(e^{k\pi\sqrt3}-(-1)^k)}$-\frac{1}{x}\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{1}{x^2+kx+k^2}$; (iv) $\displaystyle\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt3\,x^2}\frac{e^{2\pi\sqrt3\,x}-2e^{\pi\sqrt3\,x}\cos(3\pi x)+1}=\frac{2}{3\sqrt3\,x^4}+\frac{1}{6x^3}+\frac{1}{3\sqrt3\,x^2}+\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^k k}{(9x^4-3k^2x^2+k^4)(e^{k\pi\sqrt3}-(-1)^k)}$-\frac{1}{3x}\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{1}{3x^2+3kx+k^2}$ (Berndt notes Ramanujan's notebook statement of (ii) appears incorrect).

Let $x$ and $y$ be complex with $\operatorname{Re}(x\pm iy)>0$. Then (i) $\displaystyle\frac{\sqrt{y}}{\sqrt2}\left\{-\frac12+\sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-n^2\pi x/(x^2+y^2)}\cos\frac{n^2\pi y}{x^2+y^2}\right\}$+\sqrt{\frac{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}-x}{2}}\sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-n^2\pi x}\sin(n^2\pi y)$; (ii) $-\sqrt{\frac{\sqrt{x^2+y^2}+x}{2}}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-n^2\pi x}\sin(n^2\pi y)$.

Let $m=\dfrac{{}_2F_1(\tfrac12,\tfrac12;1;\alpha)}{{}_2F_1(\tfrac12,\tfrac12;1;\beta)}$ as in Section 24(i). Modular equations of degree $2$ are $m\sqrt{\alpha}+\sqrt{\beta}=1$ and $m\sqrt{1-\alpha}+\sqrt{1-\beta}=1$. Furthermore, $\dfrac{1+\beta}{1+\alpha}=\dfrac{1+\sqrt{\beta}}{1+\sqrt{1-\alpha}}$.

Modular equations of degree $4$ are $\sqrt{m}(1-\alpha)^{1/4}+\beta^{1/4}=1$ and $m(1-\alpha)^{1/4}+\sqrt{\beta}=1$. Furthermore, the multiplier is $m=\dfrac{1+\beta^{1/4}}{1+(1-\alpha)^{1/4}}=\dfrac{1+\sqrt{\beta}}{1+\alpha^{1/4}}$.

If $\alpha$ is replaced by $1-\beta$, $\beta$ by $1-\alpha$, and $m$ by $n/m$, where $n$ is the degree of the modular equation, one obtains a modular equation of the same degree (the method of reciprocation, or Fricke involution).

Let $AP$ denote the diameter of a circle with center $O$. Let $TB$ be perpendicular to $AP$, with $B$ on the circle and $T$ on $AP$. Draw chords $PR$ and $PR'$ equal in length to $TB$ with $R$ nearer to $B$. Form $AB$, $AR$, and $AR'$. Then a pendulum oscillating through $\angle BAR'$ takes $(AR-OT)/AO$ or $3AO/(AR+OT)$ times the time required to oscillate through $\angle BAR$.

Suppose that $T$ coincides with $O$. Then $\angle BAR=\pi/12$, $\angle BAR'=\pi/12$, and $\dfrac{AR-OT}{AO}=\dfrac{3AO}{AR+OT}=\sqrt{3}$. Furthermore, a pendulum oscillating through $300^\circ$ takes $\sqrt{3}$ times the time required to oscillate through $60^\circ$.

Let $AP$ denote a diameter and $PQ$ a chord of a circle. Let $B$ denote the midpoint of the arc $PQ$. Draw $AB$ and $PB$. Let $B'$ be the mirror image of $B$ in $AP$ and construct $AB'$ and $PB'$. Let $R$ be a point on the circle such that $PR=\tfrac12 PQ$ and $R$ is on the same side of $AP$ as $B$ and $Q$. Let $R'$ be the image of $R$ in $AP$. Form $PR$, $PR'$, $QR$, and $QR'$. Draw $AR$ and $AR'$, cutting $PB$ and $PB'$ at $C$ and $C'$, respectively. Construct a line perpendicular to $AP$ at $P$. Let the extensions of $AB$ and $AB'$ meet this line at $M$ and $M'$, respectively. Extend $BP$ and $AR'$ to their point of intersection $C_2$, and extend $B'P$ and $AR$ to their point of intersection $C_3$. Then a circle passes through $M$, $C$, $C'$, $M'$, $C_2$, and $C_3$, and this circle is orthogonal to the original circle. Furthermore, it is tangent to the lines $AB$ and $AB'$ at $M$ and $M'$, respectively. Let $O$ denote the center of this circle and form $OM$ and $OM'$. The circle also passes through the intersections of the circles with centers $A$ and $P$ and radii $AB$ and $PR$, respectively. The distances of any point on the circumference of this circle from $A$ and $P$ bear a constant ratio. Lastly, $QR\cdot QR'=3RP^2$.

With the notation of Entry 7(iv) (First Part), a pendulum oscillating through the angle $4\angle BAR'$ takes $QR/RP$ or $3R'P/R'Q$ times the time required to oscillate through the angle $4\angle BAR$.

Let $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ be of the first, third, fifth, and fifteenth degrees, respectively. Let $m$ denote the multiplier connecting $\alpha$ and $\beta$, and let $m'$ be the multiplier relating $\gamma$ and $\delta$. Then (i) $(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}=\sqrt{m}$; (ii) $(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m'}}$ and $(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}-\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}=(\alpha\delta)^{1/4}-\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}$; (iii) $(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}-\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}=(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}-\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}$; (iv) $1+(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}=4^{1/3}(\beta^2\gamma^2(1-\beta)^2(1-\gamma)^2)^{1/24}$; (v) $1-(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}-\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}=4^{1/3}(\alpha^2\delta^2(1-\alpha)^2(1-\delta)^2)^{1/24}$; (vi) $(\alpha\delta)^{1/16}(\{(1+\sqrt{\alpha})(1+\sqrt{\delta})\}^{1/4}+\{(1-\sqrt{\alpha})(1-\sqrt{\delta})\}^{1/4})^2+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/16}(\{(1+\sqrt{1-\alpha})(1+\sqrt{1-\delta})\}^{1/4}+\{(1-\sqrt{1-\alpha})(1-\sqrt{1-\delta})\}^{1/4})^2=\sqrt{2}$; (vii) $(\beta\gamma)^{1/16}(\{(1+\sqrt{\beta})(1+\sqrt{\gamma})\}^{1/4}-\{(1-\sqrt{\beta})(1-\sqrt{\gamma})\}^{1/4})^2+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/16}(\{(1+\sqrt{1-\beta})(1+\sqrt{1-\gamma})\}^{1/4}-\{(1-\sqrt{1-\beta})(1-\sqrt{1-\gamma})\}^{1/4})^2=\sqrt{2}$; (viii) $\dfrac{(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}-\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}}{\beta\gamma+(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)+\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}=\dfrac{\sqrt{m'}}{\sqrt{m}}$; (ix) $\dfrac{(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}}{\alpha\delta+(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)+\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}=-\dfrac{\sqrt{m}}{\sqrt{m''}}$; (x) $\dfrac{(\beta\delta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}-(\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta))^{1/4}}{\alpha\gamma+(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)+\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)}-\dfrac{4\{\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)\}^{1/6}}{\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)}=mm'$; (xi) $\dfrac{(\alpha\gamma)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}-(\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma))^{1/4}}{\beta\delta+(1-\beta)(1-\delta)+\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)}-\dfrac{4\{\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/6}}{\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)}=-\dfrac{9}{mm''}$; (xii) $\dfrac{\alpha\beta+(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)+\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}{\gamma\delta+(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)+\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)}-\dfrac{2\{\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\{1+(\gamma\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\}}{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}=\dfrac{2\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\{1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\}}{\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)}$; (xiii) $\dfrac{(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/4}+(\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta))^{1/4}}{\gamma\delta+(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)+\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)}-\dfrac{2\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\{1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\}}{\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)}=\dfrac{2\{\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\{1+(\gamma\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\}}{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}$; (xiv) $(\alpha\beta\gamma\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}+2^{1/3}\{\alpha\beta\gamma\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/24}=1$; (xv) If $P=\{256\alpha\beta\gamma\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/48}$ and $Q=\dfrac{(\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta))^{1/16}}{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}$, then $P(Q+1/Q)=\dfrac{2}{m'}(P^2+1)$.

If $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ are of degrees $3$, $7$, and $21$, respectively, with $m=\chi_1/\chi_3$ and $m'=\chi_7/\chi_{21}$, then (i) $\dfrac{(\beta\gamma)^{1/4}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}-(\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma))^{1/4}}{\alpha\delta+(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)+\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}+\dfrac{4\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/6}}{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}=\dfrac{m}{m''}$; (ii) $\dfrac{(\alpha\delta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}-(\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta))^{1/4}}{\beta\gamma+(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)+\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}+\dfrac{4\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/6}}{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}=\dfrac{m''}{m'}$; (iii) $\dfrac{(\gamma\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}-(\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta))^{1/8}}{\alpha\beta+(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)+\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}-\dfrac{2\{\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}}{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}=\left(\dfrac{\chi_1\chi_3}{\chi_7\chi_{21}}\right)^{1/2}$; (iv) $\dfrac{(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}-(\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta))^{1/8}}{\gamma\delta+(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)+\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)}-\dfrac{2\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/12}}{\gamma\delta(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)}=\left(\dfrac{7\chi_7\chi_{21}}{\chi_1\chi_3}\right)^{1/2}$; (v) $\dfrac{(\beta\delta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}+(\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta))^{1/4}}{\alpha\gamma+(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)+\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)}-\dfrac{2\{\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\{1+(\beta\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\}}{\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)}=mm'$; (vi) $\dfrac{(\alpha\gamma)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}-(\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma))^{1/4}}{\beta\delta+(1-\beta)(1-\delta)+\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)}-\dfrac{2\{\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}\{1+(\alpha\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}\}}{\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)}=\dfrac{1}{mm''}$.

Let $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ be of the third, eleventh, and thirty-third degrees, respectively, with multipliers $m$ and $m'$ for the pairs $(\alpha,\beta)$ and $(\gamma,\delta)$. Then (i) $\dfrac{(\beta\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}-(\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta))^{1/8}}{\alpha\gamma+(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)+\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)}-\dfrac{2\{\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}}{\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$; (ii) $\dfrac{(\alpha\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-(\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma))^{1/8}}{\beta\delta+(1-\beta)(1-\delta)+\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)}-\dfrac{2\{\alpha\gamma(1-\alpha)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/12}}{\beta\delta(1-\beta)(1-\delta)}=\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{m'}}$.

If $\beta$ is of the twenty-third degree with respect to $\alpha$, then (i) $(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}+2^{2/3}\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/24}=1$; (ii) $1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/4}+2^{4/3}\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/12}=\{2(1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/2}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/2})\}^{1/2}$; (iii) $\dfrac{23}{m}-\dfrac{m}{23}=2\{(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}-\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\}(-11\cdot 4^{1/3}\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/24}+18\cdot 2^{1/3}\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/12}-14\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}+25^{2/3}\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/6})$.

If $\beta$ is of degree $19$ with respect to $\alpha$ and $m$ is the multiplier, then (i) $\dfrac{((1-\beta)^3)^{1/8}}{1-\alpha}-\dfrac{(\beta^3)^{1/8}}{\beta}+\dfrac{(\beta^3(1-\beta)^3)^{1/8}}{\beta(1-\beta)}-\dfrac{2\{\beta^3(1-\beta)^3\}^{1/16}\{(\beta^3)^{1/8}-1-((1-\beta)^3)^{1/8}\}^{1/2}}{\alpha(1-\alpha)}=\dfrac{m}{2}\{1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/2}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/2}\}$; (ii) $\dfrac{(\alpha^3)^{1/8}}{1-\beta}-\dfrac{((1-\alpha)^3)^{1/8}}{\alpha}+\dfrac{(\alpha^3(1-\alpha)^3)^{1/8}}{\alpha(1-\alpha)}-\dfrac{2\{\alpha^3(1-\alpha)^3\}^{1/16}\{(\alpha^3)^{1/8}-1-((1-\alpha)^3)^{1/8}\}^{1/2}}{\beta(1-\beta)}=\dfrac{19}{2m}\{1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/2}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/2}\}$.

Let $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ have degrees $5$, $7$, and $35$, respectively, with multipliers $m$ and $m'$ for $(\alpha,\beta)$ and $(\gamma,\delta)$. Then (i) $(\alpha\delta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}+2^{4/3}\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}+(\beta\gamma)^{1/4}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}+2^{4/3}\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/12}=1+\{1+2^{4/3}\{\alpha\beta\gamma\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/24}\}^2$; (ii) $\{(\alpha\delta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}+2^{4/3}\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}\}$\times\{(\beta\gamma)^{1/4}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}+2^{4/3}\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/12}\}$ $=1-2^{7/3}\{\alpha\beta\gamma\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/24}((\alpha\beta\gamma\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8})$; (iii) $(\alpha\delta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}+2^{4/3}\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/12}=\left(\dfrac{m}{m'}\right)^{1/2}$; (iv) $(\beta\gamma)^{1/4}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}-2^{4/3}\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}=\left(\dfrac{m'}{m}\right)^{1/2}$; (v) $\dfrac{\{16\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/24}+\{16\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}}{m}=\{16\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}-\{16\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/24}=(m')^{1/2}$; (vi) $\dfrac{\{16\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}+\{16\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/24}}{m'}=\dfrac{\{16\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-\{16\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/24}}{m}$; (vii) $\dfrac{(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}-\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}}{\beta\gamma+(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)+\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}+\dfrac{2\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}}{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}=\dfrac{(m')^{1/2}}{m'}$ and $\dfrac{(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}}{\alpha\delta+(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)+\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}+\dfrac{2\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/12}}{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}=-\left(\dfrac{m}{m'}\right)^{1/2}$.

(i) $\varphi(q)\varphi(q^{63})-\varphi(q^7)\varphi(q^9)=2qf(q^3,q^{21})$; (ii) $\psi(q^7)\psi(q^9)-q^6\psi(q)\psi(q^{63})=f(-q^6)f(-q^{42})$; (iii) Let $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ have degrees $3,13,39$; or $5,11,55$; or $7,9,63$, respectively. With multipliers $m$ for $(\alpha,\beta)$ and $m'$ for $(\gamma,\delta)$, $\dfrac{1+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}+(\alpha\delta)^{1/4}}{1+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}+(\beta\gamma)^{1/4}}=\dfrac{\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}-(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}\pm\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}}{\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}-\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}}=\sqrt{m}$, where the plus sign is taken in the first two cases and the minus sign in the third; (iv) If $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ have degrees $3,13,39$ or $5,7,35$, respectively, then $\dfrac{(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}-(\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta))^{1/8}}{\beta\gamma+(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)+\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}+\dfrac{2\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}}{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}=\pm\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m}}$ and $\dfrac{(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-(\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma))^{1/8}}{\alpha\delta+(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)+\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}+\dfrac{2\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/12}}{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}=\pm\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m'}}$ (plus in the first case, minus in the second).

(i) Let $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ have one of the degree sequences $(3,21,63)$, $(5,19,95)$, $(11,13,143)$, $(7,17,119)$, or $(9,15,135)$. With multipliers $m$ for $(\alpha,\beta)$ and $m'$ for $(\gamma,\delta)$, $\alpha\{1+(\alpha\delta)^{1/2}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/2}\}^{1/2}=(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\pm\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}+2^{4/3}\dfrac{\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/2}}{\sqrt{m}}$, where the plus sign is taken in the first three cases and the minus sign in the last two; (ii) Let $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ have degree sequences $(5,19,95)$, $(7,17,119)$, or $(11,13,143)$. Then $\alpha\{1+(\beta\gamma)^{1/2}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/2}\}^{1/2}=(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}-\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}\pm 2^{4/3}\dfrac{\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/12}}{\sqrt{m'}}$ (minus in the first two cases, plus in the last).

(i) Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have degrees $(1,7)$, $(3,5)$, or $(1,15)$, respectively. Then $(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\pm\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}=\alpha\{1+\sqrt{\alpha\beta}+\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}\}^{1/2}$, where the minus sign is chosen in the first two cases and the plus sign in the last; (ii) Let $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ have degree sequences $(3,13,39)$, $(5,11,55)$, or $(7,9,63)$, with multipliers $m$ and $m'$. Then $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{m'}}\dfrac{\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}+(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}}{\sqrt{m}}=\alpha\{1+\sqrt{\alpha\delta}+\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}\}^{1/2}$ and $\dfrac{\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}+(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}\pm\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}}{\sqrt{m}}=\alpha\{1+\sqrt{\beta\gamma}+\sqrt{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}\}^{1/2}$ (minus in the first two cases, plus in the last).

Each of the following modular equations is of degree $31$. Define $\Theta(\alpha,\beta)=(\alpha\beta)^{1/32}(\{(1+\sqrt{\alpha})(1+\sqrt{\beta})\}^{1/8}\{1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/2}\}^{1/2}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\{1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1+\sqrt{\alpha})(1+\sqrt{\beta})\}^{1/2}\}^{1/2})$. Then (i) $\Theta(\alpha,\beta)+\Theta(1-\beta,1-\alpha)=8^{1/4}$; (ii) $1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/4}-2\{(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}+\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\}$ $=2\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/16}\{1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}\}^{1/2}$; (iii) $1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/4}-\{1+\sqrt{\alpha}+\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}\}^{1/2}$ $=(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}+\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}$.

(i) If $\beta$ is of degree $47$ with respect to $\alpha$, then $2\{1+\sqrt{\alpha\beta}+\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}\}^{1/2}=1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/4}+4^{1/3}\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/24}(1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8})$; (ii) If $\beta$ is of degree $71$ with respect to $\alpha$, then $1+(\alpha\beta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/4}-\{1+\sqrt{\alpha\beta}+\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}\}^{1/2}$ $=(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}-\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8}+4^{1/3}\{\alpha\beta(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/24}(1-(\alpha\beta)^{1/8}-\{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}^{1/8})$.

Let $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\delta$ have one of the degree sequences $(3,29,87)$, $(5,27,135)$, $(11,21,231)$, $(13,19,247)$, $(7,25,175)$, $(9,23,207)$, or $(15,17,255)$. Let $m$ and $m'$ denote the multipliers for $(\alpha,\beta)$ and $(\gamma,\delta)$. Then (i) $\{1+\sqrt{\beta\gamma}+\sqrt{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)}\}^{1/2}+(\beta\gamma)^{1/8}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}+\{\beta\gamma(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/8}=\{1+(\alpha\delta)^{1/4}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/4}\}\sqrt{m}$; (ii) $\{1+\sqrt{\alpha\delta}+\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)}\}^{1/2}+(\alpha\delta)^{1/8}+\{(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}\pm\{\alpha\delta(1-\alpha)(1-\delta)\}^{1/8}=\{1+(\beta\gamma)^{1/4}+\{(1-\beta)(1-\gamma)\}^{1/4}\}\sqrt{m'}$, where the minus sign is chosen in the first four cases and the plus sign in the last three.

Let $y>0$, $q=e^{-y}$, and write $x=k^2$ and $z=2K/\pi$ in the notation of Chapter 17. Suppose $y^2/\pi^2$ is rational. Then $\displaystyle L-\frac{3}{y}=1-24\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{n}{e^{2ny}-1}$ is a complete series which, when divided by $z^2$, can be expressed by radicals precisely in the same manner as $\displaystyle\frac{1}{z^4}\Bigl(1+240\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{n^3}{e^{2ny}-1}\Bigr)$ and $\displaystyle\frac{1}{z^6}\Bigl(1-504\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{n^5}{e^{2ny}-1}\Bigr)$ when divided by $z^4$ and $z^6$, respectively.
By differentiating the equation for $m$ once or the equation for $\alpha$, $\beta$ twice, one can calculate the value of the first series in Entry 2(i).

Let $\beta$ have degree $3$ over $\alpha$, and let $m$ denote the associated multiplier. Then $\displaystyle 1+12\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{2k}}{1-q^{2k}}-36\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{6k}}{1-q^{6k}}$ equals each of the following five expressions: $\displaystyle z_1z_3\left\{\frac{(3+m)^2+3(m-1)^2}{4m}\right\}^2$, $\displaystyle z_1z_3\left\{\frac{m(\alpha^3)^{1/4}}{4}+\frac{(\beta^3)^{1/4}}{4m}-\alpha\right\}^2$, $\displaystyle \varphi(q)\varphi(q^3)+4q\psi(q^2)\psi(q^6)$, $\displaystyle\frac{\{\psi^3(q^2)+2\psi^3(q^6)\}^2}{\{\psi(q^6)+3q\psi(q^2)\}^2}$, and $\displaystyle\frac{\{f^{12}(-q)+27qf^{12}(-q^3)\}^{1/3}}{f^3(-q)f^3(-q^3)}$.

Let $\beta$ have degree $3$ with respect to $\alpha$. Then $\displaystyle 1+12\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{2k}}{1-q^{2k}}-36\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{6k}}{1-q^{6k}}=\tfrac12\varphi^2(q)\varphi^2(q^3)\{1+\sqrt{\alpha\beta}+\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}\}$.

Let $\beta$ have degree $5$ over $\alpha$, and let $p$ denote the associated multiplier. Then $\displaystyle 1+6\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{2k}}{1-q^{2k}}-30\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{10k}}{1-q^{10k}}$ $\displaystyle =\frac{p(m^2+2m+5)z_1^2}{16m^2}$ $\displaystyle =\left(1-\frac{2qf^2(-q^{10})}{\varphi^2(q)\varphi^2(q^5)}\right)\left(1-\frac{4q}{\chi^4(q)\chi^4(q^5)}\right)^{1/2}$.

$\displaystyle 1+4\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{2k}}{1-q^{2k}}-28\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{14k}}{1-q^{14k}}$ $\displaystyle =z_1z_7(1-2t+2t^2)^4$ $\displaystyle =\{\varphi(q)\varphi(q^7)-2q\psi(-q)\psi(-q^7)\}^2$ $\displaystyle =(2q)^{2/3}\frac{f^2(q)}{f^2(q^7)}\varphi(q^7)(-6t+18t^2-56t^4+24t^5+8t^6)^{1/3}$ $\displaystyle =\frac{f^4(q)}{f^4(q^7)}\,\frac{2+t+23t^2-64t^3+72t^4-48t^5+16t^6}{t(1-t)}$, where $t$ is defined as in Chapter 19, Entry 19(2).

$\displaystyle 1+3\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^k}{1-q^k}-27\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{9k}}{1-q^{9k}}$ $\displaystyle =\frac{q^{1/3}f^6(q^3)}{f(q)f(q^9)}\left\{\frac{(1+t+t^2)(1-2t+4t^2)}{t(1+2t)^2(1-t)}\right\}^{1/3}$ $\displaystyle =\frac{q^{21/12}\psi(-q^3)\psi(-q)\psi(-q^9)}{z_3z_9}\left\{\frac{t(1+t+t^2)}{1+2t}\right\}^{1/2}\left\{\frac{t(1+t+t^2)}{1+2t}-3\right\}^{1/2}$, where $t$ is as in Chapter 20, Section 3.

Let $\beta$ have degree $5$ with respect to $\alpha$. Then $\displaystyle 1+6\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{2k}}{1-q^{2k}}-30\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kq^{10k}}{1-q^{10k}}$ $\displaystyle =\varphi^2(q)\varphi^2(q^5)\left(\tfrac12\{1+\alpha\beta+(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\}\right.\left.-16\{1-\sqrt{\alpha\beta}-\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}\}^2\right)^{1/2}$.

If the principal branch of each root is taken, then $\displaystyle\frac{\varphi(q)-\varphi(-q)}{\varphi(q)+\varphi(-q)}=\left(\frac{\varphi^2(q^2)-\varphi^2(-q^2)}{\varphi^2(q^2)+\varphi^2(-q^2)}\right)^{1/2}=\left(\frac{\varphi^4(q^4)-\varphi^4(-q^4)}{\varphi^4(q^4)}\right)^{1/4}$.
If the principal branch of each root is taken, then $\{\varphi(q)+i\varphi(-q)\}^{1/2}=\{\varphi(q)+\varphi(q^2)\sqrt{i}\}^{1/2}+\{\varphi(q)-\varphi(q^2)\sqrt{i}\}^{1/2}$.

In the notation of the right triangle figure on p.~263, with $c:=BA=BQ$, $b:=CA=CP$, and $a:=BC$, we have $PQ^2=2BP\cdot QC$ and $(b+c-a)^2=2\bigl(\sqrt{b^2-c^2}-b\bigr)\bigl(\sqrt{c^2-b^2}-c\bigr)$. Also, for any numbers $a$ and $b$, $\bigl(\sqrt[3]{(a+b)^3}-\sqrt[3]{a^3-ab+b^2}\bigr)\bigl(\sqrt[3]{(a+b)^3}-\sqrt[3]{b^3-ab+a^2}\bigr)=3\bigl(\sqrt[3]{a^3+b^3}-a\bigr)\bigl(\sqrt[3]{a^3+b^3}-b\bigr)$.

Let $x^2=y+a$, $y^2=z+a$, and $z^2=x+a$. Then $x$ satisfies the quartic factors obtained from Ramanujan's published solution (Collected Papers, p.~328); in particular, $x^2-x-a$ is a factor, and the remaining degree-$4$ factors are those displayed as (4.2) and (4.3) in Berndt's edition.

Let $\{a_n\}$, $\{b_n\}$, $\{c_n\}$, $\{d_n\}$, $\{e_n\}$, and $\{f_n\}$ be the six sequences of period-$3$ nested radicals associated with (4.1), with signs $-,+,+$; $+,+,-$; $-,+,-$; $+,-,-$; and $-,-,+$, respectively. Let $x_1,\ldots,x_6$ be the six roots in Theorem~4.1. Then $\lim a_n=x_4$, $\lim b_n=x_5$, $\lim c_n=-x_6$, $\lim d_n=-x_1$, $\lim e_n=-x_2$, and $\lim f_n=x_3$.

Let $x=Ap+Bq+Cr$, $y=Bp+Cq+Ar$, and $z=Cp+Aq+Br$. Then $x^2+2yz=(A^2+2BC)(p^2+2qr)+(B^2+2CA)(q^2+2rp)+(C^2+2AB)(r^2+2pq)$. Furthermore, if $x=\tfrac12(p-2q-2r)$, $y=\tfrac12(q-2r-2p)$, and $z=\tfrac12(r-2p-2q)$, then $x^2+2yz=p^2+2qr$, $y^2+2zx=q^2+2rp$, and $z^2+2xy=r^2+2pq$.

If $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ are roots of $x^3-ax^2+bx-1=0$, then for a suitable determination of cube roots, $\alpha^{1/3}+\beta^{1/3}+\gamma^{1/3}=(a+b+3t)^{1/3}$ and $(\alpha\beta)^{1/3}+(\beta\gamma)^{1/3}+(\gamma\alpha)^{1/3}=(b+6+3t)^{1/3}$, where $t^3-3(a+b+3)t-(ab+6a+6b+9)=0$.

Let $x$ and $y$ be positive, and define positive numbers $\alpha$ and $\beta$ by $\dfrac{1+\sqrt5}{y}=\dfrac{\alpha^{1/5}}{\beta^{1/5}}$ and $\alpha\beta=1$. Define $z$ by $\dfrac{x-\beta}{y}=\alpha+\dfrac1z$. Then $z=5xy^4+25x^2y^3+15x^3y^2+5x^4y+x^5y$ and $\dfrac{1+\sqrt5}{x}=\dfrac{\gamma^{1/5}}{\delta^{1/5}}$, where $\gamma\delta=1$ and $\gamma=(5y)^{5/2}x^{5/2}z^{1/2}$.

Let $f$, $F$, $\varphi$, and $\psi$ be functions related by $f(x)=\varphi(x)F(x+p)+\psi(x)F(x+q)$, where $p$ and $q$ are constants. Let $\chi(x+p)/\chi(x)=\varphi(x)/\psi(x)$. Define $F_1$ by $F(x)=F_1(x)\chi(x)$. Then $\varphi(x)F_1(x+p)+\psi(x)F_1(x+q)=\chi(x+p)\varphi(x)$.

If $a$, $b$, $c$ are constants in arithmetic progression and $u$, $v$, $w$ are functions of $x$ in geometric progression, solve $uF(x+a)+vF(x+b)+wF(x+c)=\varphi(x)$. Find $\chi(x)$ so that $\dfrac{\chi(x+3a/2)}{\chi(x+3c/2)}=\sqrt{\dfrac{u}{w}}$, and substitute $F(x)=\chi(x)\bigl\{\chi(x+a/2)F_1(x+3a/2)-\chi(x+c/2)F_1(x+3c/2)\bigr\}$.

Let $F$, $F_1$, $f$, $\varphi$, and $\psi$ satisfy $F(x+p)\{\varphi(x)+\psi(x)F(x+q)\}=f(x)$ and $F_1(x+q)=\varphi(x)+\psi(x)F_1(x+p)$, where $p$ and $q$ are constants. Then $\varphi(x)F(x+p+q)+\psi(x)F_1(x+2q)=f(x)F_1(x+2p)$.

Solve $x^5-a$ and $y^5-b$ for $x$ and $y$, where $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary. Writing $x=\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ and $y=\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha$ with $\alpha\beta\gamma=1$, the values of $x$ are obtained from the fifth roots of unity acting on $(\alpha,\beta,\gamma)$; there are $25$ solutions for $x$ and corresponding values for $y$.

Solve $x^7-a$ and $y^7-b$ for $x$ and $y$, where $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary. With $x=\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ and $y=\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha$ and $\alpha\beta\gamma=1$, there are $49$ values of $x$ (and corresponding $y$) obtained using seventh roots of unity.

Solve the system $x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n=a_1$, $x_1y_1+x_2y_2+\cdots+x_ny_n=a_2$, $\ldots$, $x_1y_1^{2n-2}+x_2y_2^{2n-2}+\cdots+x_ny_n^{2n-2}=a_{2n-1}$ for the $2n$ unknowns $x_1,\ldots,x_n$ and $y_1,\ldots,y_n$.

Let $a$, $b$, $c$, and $d$ be arbitrary. Solve $x^2+ay=b$ and $y^2+cx=d$. Using $x=\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ and $y=-\tfrac{2}{a}(\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha)$ with $\alpha\beta\gamma=-ca^2/8$, the four values of $x$ are $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$, $\alpha-\beta-\gamma$, $-\alpha+\beta-\gamma$, and $-\alpha-\beta+\gamma$.
Let $\tan^{-1}z$ have its principal value, and let $n$ be a positive integer. Then $(x^2+1)^{n/2}\sin(n\tan^{-1}x)=nx\prod_{k=1}^{\lfloor(n-1)/2\rfloor}\bigl(1+\tan^2(k\pi/n)\,x^2\bigr)$.

Let $\sin^{-1}z$ take its principal value, and let $n$ be a positive integer. If $n$ is even, then $\sin(n\sin^{-1}x)=nx\sqrt{1-x^2}\prod_{k=1}^{n/2-1}\bigl(1-\sin^2(k\pi/n)\,x^2\bigr)$; if $n$ is odd, then $\sin(n\sin^{-1}x)=nx\prod_{k=1}^{(n-1)/2}\bigl(1-\sin^2(k\pi/n)\,x^2\bigr)$.

Let $a_0,a_1,\ldots,a_n$ satisfy $a_0=1$ and $a_k\neq0$ for $1\le k\le n$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(x+a_0)(x+a_1)\cdots(x+a_t)}=\frac1{x+1}+\frac1{(x+1)(x+a_1)}+\cdots+\frac1{(x+1)\cdots(x+a_n)}$.

Let $n$ be any real number, and let $a_0,\ldots,a_{r-1}$ and $b_0,\ldots,b_r$ be numbers with $a_k\neq0$ for $1\le k\le r-1$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{r}\binom{n}{j}\frac{(a_0\cdots a_{j-1})(b_0\cdots b_{r-j})}{(x+a_0)\cdots(x+a_j)}=\frac{(a_0\cdots a_{r-1})(b_0\cdots b_r)}{(x+a_0)\cdots(x+a_r)}$.
If $x^3+ax+b=y$ and $y^3+ay+b=x$, then $(x^3+(a-1)x+b)(x^2+ax+a^2+1+a)(x^2+\beta x+\beta^2+1+a)(x^2+\gamma x+\gamma^2+1+a)=0$, where $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ are the roots of $z^3+(a+2)z+b=0$.

Let $p,q,r,s$ be given and define $x=p+q+r+s$, $a=qr+ps$, $b=pq^2+qs^2+rp^2+sr^2$, $a^2+c=pr^3+qp^3+rs^3+sq^3+3pqrs$, and $d=p^5+q^5+r^5+s^5+5(ps-qr)(pq^2-qs^2-rp^2+sr^2)$. Then $x^5-5ax^3-5bx^2-5cx-d=0$.

If $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ are the roots of $x^3-ax^2+bx-1=0$, then $(\alpha/\beta)^{1/3}+(\beta/\gamma)^{1/3}+(\gamma/\alpha)^{1/3}$ and $(\beta/\alpha)^{1/3}+(\gamma/\beta)^{1/3}+(\alpha/\gamma)^{1/3}$ are the roots of $z^2-tz+a+b+3=0$, where $t^3-3(a+b+3)t-(ab+6a+6b+9)=0$; they are also roots of $y^6-(ab+6a+6b+9)y^3+(a+b+3)^3=0$.

The equalities $x^2=a+y$, $y^2=a+z$, $z^2=a+u$, and $u^2=a+x$ determine a polynomial in $x$ (or $y$, $z$, or $u$) of degree $16$. This polynomial factors into four quartics, one of which is $x^4-2ax^2-x+a^2-a$. Each remaining quartic has the form $(x^2+px+\tfrac12(p^2-2a-1/p))(x^2+qx+\tfrac12(q^2-2a-1/q))$ with $pq=-1$ and $p+q$ a root of $z^3+3z=4(1+az)$.

The following are approximations to $\pi$: $\pi\approx\sqrt[4]{97+\tfrac{9}{10}}$; $\pi\approx\tfrac{92+29\sqrt2}{25}$; $\pi\approx\tfrac{63}{20}\sqrt{2+\sqrt2}$; $\pi\approx\tfrac{19}{6}\sqrt3$; $\pi\approx\tfrac{25}{8}$; and $\pi\approx355/113$.
We have $2^{2/3}=1.259921049894873164767208\ldots$ (eighteen of the twenty-four displayed decimals are correct).
If $p^3+q^3+r^3=s^3$ and $a$ and $b$ are arbitrary, then $(pa^2+mab-rb^2)^3+(qa^2-nab+sb^2)^3+(ra^2-mab-pb^2)^3=(sa^2-nab+qb^2)^3$.
Let $p$, $q$, and $r$ satisfy $p+3a^2=q+3ab=r+3b^2=(a+b)^2$. Let $m$ and $n$ be arbitrary. Then $n(mp+nq)^3+m(mq+nr)^3=mn(np+mnq)^3+n(nq+mnr)^3$.
For each real number $p$, the nearest integer to $p$ equals $[p+\tfrac12]$.
Let $p$ and $n$ be positive integers. The coefficient of $x^n$ in the Maclaurin series of $\dfrac1{(1-x)(1-x^p)}$ equals $[n/p]$.

Let $\varphi$ be a continuous increasing function on $[1,\infty)$ tending to $\infty$. For the formal series $F(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}x^{\lceil\varphi(i-1)\rceil}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}x^k$, the coefficient of $x^n$ ($n>1$) equals $[\varphi(n)]$.
Let $d(n)$ denote the number of positive divisors of $n$. If $p$ is a natural number, then $\sum_{n=1}^{p}d(n)=\sum_{k=1}^{p}[p/k]=2\sum_{k=1}^{\lfloor\sqrt p\rfloor}[p/k]-[\sqrt p]$.
With the notation of Entry~8, $\sum_{n=1}^{p}d(n)$ is odd or even according as $[\sqrt p]$ is odd or even, and is approximately equal to $p(2\gamma-1+\log p)$, where $\gamma$ is Euler's constant.
Let $n$ and $p$ be positive integers with $n>\sqrt p$. Then $\#\{(x,y)\in\mathbb Z_{>0}^2:xy\le p,\ x\le n\}=np-[n^2/2]-\sum_{k=1}^{n}[p/k]$.
If $p$ is the $n$th prime, then $dp/dn\approx\log p$ and hence $n\approx p/(\log p-1)$.

Let $\varphi$ be a positive monotonically decreasing function with a continuous first derivative on $[2,\infty)$. Then $\sum_{p}\varphi(p)$ and $\int_2^{\infty}\varphi(t)\,dt/(\log t)$ are either both convergent or both divergent, where the sum is over all primes $p$.

The number of integers between $A$ and $B$ representable as a sum of two squares is approximately $\displaystyle K\int_A^B\frac{dx}{\sqrt x\,\log x}$, where $K=\prod_{p\equiv3\pmod4}\bigl(1-\tfrac1{p^2}\bigr)^{-1/2}\approx0.764$.

If quantities $A,B,C,\ldots$ satisfy asymptotically $\dfrac1{A^k}+\dfrac1{B^k}+\dfrac1{C^k}+\cdots=-\dfrac1{(k-a)^r}$ as $k\to a$, then the number of such quantities less than $z$ is $\displaystyle\int_{z^{1-a}}^{z}\frac{(\log z)^{r-1}}{\Gamma(r)(k-a)^r}\,dk$.
All numbers of the form $a^pb^q$ not exceeding $n$ equal $\dfrac{1}{2\log a\log b}\bigl(\log(an)\log(bn)\bigr)$, plus $\tfrac12$ if $n$ itself has this form.
If $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}e^{-nx}=\int_0^x e^{-tf(t)}\,dt+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n$, then the average value of $a_n$ equals $f(n)$ exactly.
Each positive integer can be represented as a sum of four squares.
A positive integer $n$ is a sum of three squares if and only if $n$ is not of the form $4^m(8k+7)$ with $k,m\ge0$.
A positive integer $n$ is a sum of two squares if and only if in the canonical factorization of $n$, each prime $p\equiv3\pmod4$ occurs with an even exponent.
If a prime of the form $An+B$ can be expressed as $ax^2-by^2$, then a prime of the form $An-B$ can be expressed as $bx^2-ay^2$ (under appropriate hypotheses on $a,b,A,B$).
Every prime $p\equiv1\pmod8$ is $x^2-2y^2$; hence every prime $q\equiv7\pmod8$ is also $x^2-2y^2$.
Every prime $p\equiv1\pmod{12}$ is $x^2-3y^2$; hence every prime $q\equiv11\pmod{12}$ is $3X^2-Y^2$.
With $a=5$, $b=13$, $A=65$, $B=7$, primes $q\equiv-7\pmod{65}$ are representable by both $5X^2-13Y^2$ and $13X^2-5Y^2$.

Let $g$ be the greatest common divisor of any one of $\{2^p-1,2^p,2^p+1\}$ and any one of $\{3^p-1,3^p,3^p+1\}$, where $p$ is a fixed positive integer. Then $g\mid(n^p\pm1)$ for every positive integer $n$.

The coefficients of $x^{100}$ and $x^{99}$ in the Maclaurin expansions of $\dfrac{x^7}{(1-x^2)(1-x^3)(1-x)(1-x^2)(1-x^3)}$ and $\dfrac{x^2-x^3}{(1-x^2)(1-x^3)(1-x)(1-x^2)(1-x^3)}$ are each $[95/2]-[95/3]=16$.
For each positive integer $n$, writing $n=N^2+a$ with $0\le a<2N+1$, we have $[\sqrt n+\sqrt{n+1}]=[\sqrt{4n+1}]$.
Let $\varphi(q)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}q^{n(n+1)/2}$. The coefficient of $x^n$ in the Maclaurin expansion of $\varphi(x^2)/(1-x)$ equals the largest integer $m$ with $m(m+1)\le n$.
Let $n=\prod_{i=1}^{r}p_i^{a_i}$ be the canonical factorization into distinct primes. Then $d(n)=\prod_{i=1}^{r}(a_i+1)$.
Let $n=p^k$ with $p$ prime and $k$ a positive integer. Then $d(n)\le\log(pn)/\log p$.
Let $n=p^jq^k$ with distinct primes $p,q$ and positive integers $j,k$. Then $d(n)\le\dfrac{j\log(pn)+k\log(qn)}{\log p\,\log q}$.
With the notation of Entry~25, $d(n)\le\bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{r}a_i\log(p_in)\bigr)/\bigl(\prod_{i=1}^{r}\log p_i\bigr)$.
Let $1,\alpha,\alpha^2,\alpha^3,\alpha^4,\alpha^5,\alpha^6$ be the seven seventh roots of unity. Then $\alpha+\alpha^2+\alpha^4=-\tfrac12+\tfrac{i\sqrt7}{2}$.

Let $\alpha$ be as in Entry~29. For each complex $x$, define $\varphi(x)=7\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{x^{14j+7}}{(14j+7)!}$ and $\psi(x)=7\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{x^{14j+6}}{(14j+6)!}$ (equivalently, $\varphi(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\bigl(\sum_{k=0}^{6}\alpha^k\bigr)\dfrac{x^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!}$ with the inner sum equal to $7$ when $n\equiv3\pmod7$ and $0$ otherwise, and $\psi(x)$ the analogous cosine series).

Let $\varphi$, $\psi$, and $\alpha$ be as in Entries~29--30. Then $64\sum_{k=0}^{6}\sin(\alpha^k x)=-\varphi(2x)+\varphi(2(\alpha+\alpha^2+\alpha^4)x)+\cdots$ (seven terms), and a corresponding cosine identity holds with all signs positive.
If $a_n=(2+\sqrt3)^n+(2-\sqrt3)^n$ for $n\ge0$, then $a_ma_n=a_{m+n}+a_{m-n}$ for nonnegative integers $m\ge n$.

Let $a_n$ be as in Entry~32 and $B_n$ the $n$th Bernoulli number. For $|x|<2\pi$, four $6$-interval formulas relate power series in $x^{12j+k}$ with coefficients $a_n/(12n+k)!$ to Bernoulli numbers $B_{12n+k}$ (see Berndt (33.1)--(33.4)).

The expressions $\dfrac{12}{\pi}\sqrt{\log\bigl((3+\sqrt{10})x/\sqrt5+\sqrt{10}\bigr)}$, $\dfrac{24}{\pi}\sqrt{\log\bigl(\sqrt{10+11\sqrt2}+\sqrt{142+99\sqrt2}\bigr)}$, and $\log\bigl((3+\sqrt{10})x/\sqrt5+\sqrt{10}\bigr)$ are approximations to $\pi$ valid for $14$, $15$, and $18$ decimal places, respectively.
Let $a\ge2$ and $n$ be positive integers. Then $\dfrac{n}{a-1}\log a\le\log(n+1)\le\dfrac{n}{a-1}\log(a(n+1))$; both inequalities are sharp.

Let $x>0$ and $n>1$. Then $\sum_{k=1}^{\lfloor x/n\rfloor}[n/k]=\sum_{k=1}^{\lfloor\sqrt x\rfloor}[x/k]-\sum_{k=1}^{\lfloor\sqrt x\rfloor}[x/(kn)]$ as $x\to\infty$ (with an error term $O(x^{1/2+\varepsilon})$ in Ramanujan's notation).
Let $\sigma(n)$ denote the sum of positive divisors of $n$, and let $n=\prod_{i=1}^{r}p_i^{a_i}$. Then $\sigma(n)=\prod_{i=1}^{r}(p_i^{a_i+1}-1)/(p_i-1)$.
The sum of the divisors of $N$ is approximately $e^\gamma N\log\log N$.
Let $p(n)$ be the number of unrestricted partitions of $n$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}p(n)q^n=\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}(1-q^k)^{-1}$.
Let $\sigma(n)$ be the sum of divisors of $n$ and $p(n)$ the partition function. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}\sigma(k)\,p(n-k)=n\,p(n)$ for every positive integer $n$.
Formally, $\dfrac{1}{(1-a_1)(1-a_2)\cdots}=\dfrac1{1-a_1}+\dfrac{a_1}{(1-a_1)(1-a_2)}+\cdots$ with the numerator of the $k$th term containing all products of $k$ distinct $a_j$.
For $|x|<1$, $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}x^k\log k=\sum_{p}\log p\sum_{i=1}^{\infty}\frac{x^{ip}}{1-x^p}$, where the outer sum is over all primes $p$.
As $s\to1^+$, $\displaystyle\sum_{p}\frac{\log p}{p^s}\sim\frac1{s-1}$, where the sum is over all primes $p$.

Let $\mu(n)$ denote the Möbius function. For $\Re s>1$, $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\mu(n)}{n^s}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\mu(n)}{n^s(n^s-1)}=\sum_{p}\frac{\log p}{p^s}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\mu(n)}{n^s(n^s-1)}+f(s)$, where $f(s)$ is analytic for $\Re s>1$.
For $\Re s>1$, $\displaystyle\int_1^x\frac{dt}{t^s}=\sum_{p}\frac{\log p}{p^s}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\mu(n)}{n^s(n^s-1)}+f(s)$, where $f(s)$ is analytic for $\Re s>1$.
If $x$ is a function of $n$ such that $\displaystyle\int_1^x e^{-at}\log x\,d\eta(x)=\dfrac1{a^2}$, then there are $n$ prime numbers between $1$ and $x$.

For $x>0$, define $\varphi(x)=\sum_{p}\log p\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}e^{-pkx}=\log2+2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{k-1}e^{-2kx}+\psi(x)$, where the sum on the left is over all primes $p$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{k-1}\varphi(kx)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n-1}e^{-nx}\log p_n$.
As $x\to\infty$, $\pi(x)\approx\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\mu(n)}{n}\,\Li(x^{1/n})=:R(x)$, where $\Li(x)=\int_\mu^x\dfrac{dt}{\log t}$ and $\mu=1.4513692349\ldots$ is Soldner's constant.
As $x\to\infty$, $\pi(x)\approx\dfrac{x}{\log x}+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{2k-1}{2k}\,\dfrac{B_{2k}}{2k}\,\left(\dfrac{x}{\log x}\right)^{2k}=:G(x)$, where $B_{2k}$ are Bernoulli numbers.

Let $n$ be a positive integer, $\mu$ Soldner's constant, and $\delta=n-\log x$. Define $\theta=\theta(n)$ by $\Li(x^{1/n})=\dfrac{\log x}{n}+\dfrac{\theta}{n\log^{n-1}x}$. Then as $x\to\infty$, $\theta\approx\delta-\tfrac12\delta^2+\tfrac{1}{6}\delta^3-\tfrac{1}{12}\delta^4+\tfrac{1}{5}\delta^5-\tfrac{1}{6}\delta^6$.
Let $\mu$ be Soldner's constant and $\gamma$ Euler's constant. Then $\Li(x)=\gamma+\log\log x+\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\log^k x}{k!\,k}-\log\mu$.
We have $\displaystyle\int_0^{\log x}\dfrac{(\log t)^k\,dt}{\Gamma(k+1)\,\zeta(k+1)}=:J(x)$, where $\zeta$ is the Riemann zeta-function.
Let $\mu$ be Soldner's constant and $\gamma$ Euler's constant. Then $\Li(\sqrt x)=\sqrt x\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^k\log^k x}{(2k)!}\left(\gamma-1+\log4+\sum_{j=1}^{k}\dfrac1j\right)$.

Let $f(a,b)$ be defined by (0.1). Let $m$, $n$, $p$, $r$, and $k$ be positive numbers such that $m+n=p+r=k$. Then as $q\to 1^-$, $\displaystyle\frac{f(-q^m,-q^n)}{f(-q^p,-q^r)}\sim\frac{\sin(\pi m/k)}{\sin(\pi p/k)}$.
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive. Then as $q\to 1^-$, $\displaystyle\frac{f(-q^m,-q^n)}{f(-q^{(m+n)/2},-q^{(m+n)/2})}\sim\sqrt{\frac{m+n}{mn}}$.
Suppose that $|ab|<1$, and let $\chi$ denote the Legendre symbol. Then $f^3(a^2b,ab^2)+a\chi(a^2b,a^3b^2)f^3(b,a^3b^2)+b\chi(b,a^2b^3)f^3(a,a^2b^3)=0$.
Let $\omega$ be a primitive cube root of unity. Then, for $|ab|<1$, $f(\omega a,\omega b)=\omega f(a,b)+(1-\omega)f(a^6b^3,a^3b^6)$.
If $|ab|<1$ and $|cd|<1$, then $f(a i,b i)f(c i,d i)-f(-a i,-b i)f(-c i,-d i)=i\{f(a,b)f(c,d)-f(-a,-b)f(-c,-d)\}$.
If $|ab|<1$ and $|cd|<1$, then $f(a i,b i)f(c i,d i)+f(-a i,-b i)f(-c i,-d i)=f(a,b)f(-c,-d)+f(-a,-b)f(c,d)$.

The double series $\displaystyle\sum_{m=-\infty}^\infty\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty a^{m(m+1)/2}b^{m(m-1)/2}p^{mn+n(n+1)/2}q^{-mn+n(n-1)/2}$ is unaltered by interchanging $a$ and $c$ while at the same time interchanging $b$ and $d$.

Let $|ab|<1$, and let $n$ be any real number such that $n\ne -(ab)^k$ for any integer $k$. Then $\displaystyle\frac{f(a/n,bn)f(-ab)}{f(-n,-b)f(nab,1/n)}$=\frac{n}{n+1}\frac{1-(ab)^{1/2}}{1+n(ab)^{1/2}}\frac{1-(ab)^{1/2}/n}{1-(ab)^{1/2}/n^2}$.
Let $\displaystyle\varphi(q)=\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty q^{n^2}\left(\frac{n}{7}\right)$, where $\left(\frac{n}{7}\right)$ denotes the Legendre symbol. Then $\varphi(q)+\varphi(-q)=2\varphi(q^4)$.

Let $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ be defined as in (30.2). Then $\displaystyle\frac{\alpha^2}{\beta}+\frac{\beta^2}{\gamma}+\frac{\gamma^2}{\alpha}=8\frac{f(-q^7,-q^{14})}{\eta(q)\eta(q^7)}$, where $\eta$ denotes the Dedekind eta-function.
Let $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ be defined as in (30.2). Then $\displaystyle\frac{\alpha}{\beta^2}+\frac{\beta}{\gamma^2}+\frac{\gamma}{\alpha^2}=8\frac{f(-q^7,-q^{14})}{\eta(q)\eta(q^7)}$.
Let $u$, $v$, and $w$ be as in Entry 32(i). Then $\displaystyle\frac{u^2}{v^2}+\frac{v^2}{w^2}+\frac{w^2}{u^2}=0$.
Let $u$, $v$, and $w$ be as in Entry 32(i). Then $\displaystyle\frac{v^2}{u^2}+\frac{w^2}{v^2}+\frac{u^2}{w^2}=\frac{f^4(-q^7)}{f^4(-q)}+13+49\left(\frac{f(-q)}{f(-q^7)}+3\right)$.
As a consequence of Entry 32(ii), $\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{6}\frac{\sin(2r\pi/7)\cos(2r\theta)}{\sin(r\pi/7)}=0$ for $0<\theta<\pi/2$.

There exist functions $\mu$ and $
u$ such that $f(a,b)=f(a^{15}b^{10},a^{10}b^{15})+\mu^{1/5}+
u^{1/5}$, where $\mu=a^4b f(a^{20}b^{15},a^5b^{10})$ and $

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have degrees $1$ and $9$, respectively. Let $t$ be the parameter from Chapter 20. Then $\alpha=16t^9(1+t)$.
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have degrees $1$ and $9$, respectively. Then $\beta=16t^8(1+t)$.
Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ have degrees $1$ and $9$, respectively. Then $\alpha\beta=(1+2t)^9$.

Let $0\le\theta\le\pi/2$ and $0\le v\le 1$. Define $\mu$ to be the constant obtained by setting $\theta=\pi/2$ and $v=1$ in $\displaystyle\frac{\theta\mu}{\sqrt{2}}=\int_0^v\frac{dt}{\sqrt{1-t^4}}$=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(1/4)_n v^{4n+1}}{n!(4n+1)}$. Then for $0<\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle v=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\mathrm{sd}(z\theta)=\frac{\mathrm{sn}(z\theta)}{\sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{dn}(z\theta)}$, where $z=2K/\pi$ and $q=e^{-\pi}$.
With $\theta$, $v$, and $\mu$ as in Entry 1 and $0<\theta<\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\frac{v}{\sqrt{2}}=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{(4n)!}{(4n-1)!\,n!}\sin(2n\theta)$.

With $\theta$, $v$, and $\mu$ as in Entry 1 and $0<\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\log v+\frac{\pi}{6}-\log 2+\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{(1/4)_n v^{4n}}{(3/4)_n\,4n}$=\log(\sin\theta)+\frac{\theta^2}{2\pi}-2\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\cos(2n\theta)}{n(e^{2\pi n}-1)}$+2\log f(-e^{-\pi})$, where $f(-q):=\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1-q^n)$ for $|q|<1$.

With $\theta$, $v$, and $\mu$ as in Entry 1 and $0\le\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\mathrm{sn}(z\theta)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\cos\{(2n+1)\theta\}}{(2n+1)\cosh\{(2n+1)\pi/2\}}$.

With $\theta$, $v$, and $\mu$ as in Entry 1 and $0\le\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,\mathrm{dn}(z\theta)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\sin\{(2n+1)\theta\}}{(2n+1)\cosh\{(2n+1)\pi/2\}}$.

With $\theta$, $v$, and $\mu$ as in Entry 1 and $0<\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\frac{{}_2F_1(\tfrac14,\tfrac34;\tfrac32;v^4)}{\sqrt{1-v^4}}-\frac{\pi\theta}{2\mu}$=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\sin\{(2n+1)\theta\}}{(2n+1)!(4n+3)}\frac{8}{(2n+1)^2\cosh\{(2n+1)\pi/2\}}$.

Let $0\le\theta\le\pi/2$ and $0\le v\le 1$. Put $\displaystyle\frac{\theta\mu}{2}=\int_0^v\frac{dt}{\sqrt{1+t^4}}$, where $\mu$ is the constant obtained by setting $\theta=\pi/2$ and $v=1$. Then $\displaystyle\mathrm{am}(z\theta)=2\tan^{-1}v$, equivalently $\displaystyle 2\tan^{-1}v=\theta+\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{n\cosh(n\pi)}$, where $z=2K/\pi$ and $q=e^{-\pi}$.
With $\theta$, $v$, and $\mu$ as in Entry 7 and $0<\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\frac{2\tan^{-1}v}{\sqrt{1+v^4}}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\cos\{(2n+1)\theta\}}{(2n+1)\cosh\{(2n+1)\pi/2\}}$.
With $\theta$, $v$, and $\mu$ as in Entry 7 and $0<\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\frac{\sqrt{1+v^4}}{\sqrt{1-v^4}}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\sin\{(2n+1)\theta\}}{(2n+1)(e^{(2n+1)\pi/2}-1)}$.

Let $0\le\theta\le\pi/2$ and $0\le x$. Define $\mu$ by setting $\theta=\pi/2$ and $x=\infty$ in $\displaystyle\theta\mu=\int_0^x\frac{dt}{\sqrt{1-t^2+t^4}}$. Let $\rho=e^{2\pi i/3}$. Then for $0<\theta\le\pi/2$, $\displaystyle\wp(\theta;\pi,\rho\pi)=\frac{\pi^2}{6}+\csc^2\theta\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{n e^{-n\pi/3}\cos(2n\theta)}{1-e^{-2n\pi/3}}$.
Let $|q|<1$. Suppose that $a\ne 0$ and $1-bq^n\ne 0$ for $n\ge 1$. Then $\displaystyle(-aq;q)_\infty=\frac{(-b/a;q)_\infty}{(-bq;q)_\infty}\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-b/a)_n a^n q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(q)_n}$.

Let $|q|<1$, and suppose that $(aq)_n\ne 0$ for $n\ge 1$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n q^{n(n-1)/2}}{(aq)_n(1-q^n)}$=\frac{1}{(aq;q)_\infty}\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n a^n q^{n^2}}{(q)_n}$.

Let $|q|<1$, $a\ne 0$, and $(b)_n\ne 0$ for $n\ge 1$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n(b/a)_n a^n q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(q)_n(b)_n}$=\frac{1}{(b/a;q)_\infty}\left\{\frac{a}{1-a}-\frac{b}{1-b}+\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n(b/a)_n a^n q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(q)_n(bq)_n}\right\}$.

Let $|a|,|q|<1$. Then as $q\to 1^-$, $\displaystyle\log\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(a)_n(1-q^n)}$\sim\frac{1}{1-q}\left\{\frac{\pi^2}{6}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1+a}{1-a}\right)\log^2 q\right\}$+\frac{B_2}{2!}\log q+\frac{B_4}{4!}\left(a-\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{a^3}-a^3\right)\log^3 q+\cdots$, where $B_n$, $n>0$, and $\mathrm{Li}_2$ are defined by (0.2) and (0.3), respectively.

Let $a>0$, $|q|<1$, and $b$ and $c$ be integers with $b>0$. Let $z$ denote the positive root of $az^{2b}+z=1$. Then as $q\to 1^-$, $\displaystyle\log\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(q)_n(a;q)_n}$\sim\frac{1}{1-q}\left\{\mathrm{Li}_2(az^{2b})+b\log^2 z\right\}$+c\log z-\tfrac12\log\{z+2\sqrt{z(1-z)}\}$.

Let $\mathrm{Li}_2$ denote the dilogarithm defined by (0.3). Suppose that $a>0$ and $|q|<1$. Let $x$ denote the positive root of $x^2+x=a$. Then as $q\to 1^-$, $\displaystyle\log\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{a^n q^{n^2}}{(q)_n(a;q)_n}$\sim\frac{1}{1-q}\left\{\mathrm{Li}_2(-x)-\tfrac12\log^2(1+x)\right\}$ (equivalently, when $a=1$, $\displaystyle\log\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{q^{n^2}}{(q)_n}\sim\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{15(1-q)}}$).

As $q\to 1^-$, $\displaystyle\log\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(q)_n}$\sim\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{15(1-q)}}$; this is the asymptotic expansion for the logarithm of the second Rogers–Ramanujan identity $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(q)_n}=\frac{1}{(q;q^5)_\infty(q^4;q^5)_\infty}$.
If $a>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty e^{-ax}\sin(ax)\{\cot x+\coth x\}\,dx=\frac{\pi}{2\sinh(\pi a)}$.

With $I(a)$ as in Entry 4 and $\alpha\beta=4\pi^2$, Ramanujan asserts that $I(\alpha)\approx\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\alpha}\,\Gamma(\alpha)\,\zeta(\alpha+1)(2\pi)^{-\alpha-1/2}$ agrees \"nearly\" with the asymptotic expansion obtained from Watson's lemma.

Let $\alpha,\beta>0$, $\alpha\beta=4\pi^2$, and $m>1$. Define $\displaystyle I(\alpha)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\alpha^{\alpha-1}x^{m-1}}{e^{4\pi x/\beta}-1}\,dx$. As $\alpha\to0$, $I(\alpha)\sim(2\pi)^{(\alpha+m)/2}\alpha^{(\alpha-m)/2}\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{B_n\Gamma(m+n)\zeta(m+n)}{(2\pi)^n n!}\alpha^n$; as $\beta\to0$, the symmetric expansion holds with $\alpha$ and $\beta$ interchanged.

If $n>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\frac{x\sin(2nx)}{e^{2\pi\sqrt{x}}-1}\,dx=\frac{n}{2}\sum_{k=0}^\infty{}'\,e^{-2n\sqrt{k}}\cos(2n\sqrt{k}\,\pi)$, where the prime indicates that the $k=0$ term is multiplied by $\tfrac12$.

Let $\chi$ denote the primitive character modulo $4$. If $n>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\frac{x\sin(2nx)}{e^{2\pi\sqrt{x}}+1}\,dx=\frac{n}{2\pi}\sum_{k=1}^\infty\chi(k)\,e^{-n\sqrt{k}}\sin(n\sqrt{k}\,\pi)$.

For nonzero real $\theta$, Ramanujan records divergent asymptotic series relating $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{\theta^{2k+1}}{(2k+1)!}\left(\frac{\pi}{2\theta}\right)^{2k+1}$ to integrals of $\cos(\pi x^2)/(e^{\theta x^2}-1)$ and $\sin(\pi x^2)/(e^{\theta x^2}-1)$ as $\theta\to0$ and $\theta\to\infty$.

With $\displaystyle\varphi(n)=\varphi^*(n/n)$ and $\displaystyle\varphi^*(n)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\cos(\pi nx)}{e^{n x^2}-1}\,dx$, for nonzero real $n$, $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\frac{\sin(nx)}{e^{nx^2}-1}\,dx=\frac{1}{n}\left\{\frac{\pi}{2n}-\varphi(n)\right\}$.

For $a>0$, with $\displaystyle\psi(n)=\frac{4\pi e^{-2\pi n^2}}{e^{2\pi n^2}-1}$ and $\displaystyle G(a)=\frac{2}{\cosh(2\pi a)-\cos(2\pi a)}$, $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty e^{-2\pi a^2 n}\,\psi(n)\,dn=\frac{1}{G(a)}$.

Let $\varphi$ be as in Entry 10. The non-transcendental part of $\varphi(\pi n)$ for rational $n=a/b$ is expressible by finite trigonometric sums $\sum_k\sin(k^2\pi a/b)$ and $\sum_k\cos(k^2\pi a/b)$.

If $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty f(n)\,dn=\sum_{k=1}^\infty e^{-2\pi k p}$ for $p>0$ and $\displaystyle f(n)=\frac{e^{-2\pi np}}{\sqrt{n}}$ with $|\arg p|<\pi/4$, then $f$ is the inverse Laplace transform of $e^{-2\pi p\sqrt{s}}/\sqrt{s}$.

For $n>0$, with $\psi$ as in Entry 11, $\displaystyle\psi(n\pi)=\sum_{r=1}^\infty\sum_{j=0}^\infty\frac{2}{j!}\left\{\frac{(2r+j)\cos(\pi j(2r+j)/n)+j\sin(\pi j(2r+j)/n)}{(2r+j)!}\right\}e^{-2\pi(r+j/2-1/2)^2/n}$.

For real $p,n,a$ with $0<p<a$ and $n<1$, $\displaystyle I(p,n,a):=\int_0^1 x^{n-1}(1-x^p)^a\,dx=\frac{\pi}{p\sin(\pi n)}\sum_{j=0}^\infty(-1)^j\frac{\Gamma(a+j+1)}{\Gamma(a+1+j/n)}\frac{1}{(p+j/n)(a+j/n)}$.

Formally, $\displaystyle\varphi(x)\sim\sum_{k=0}^\infty\varphi^{(k)}(0)\,\frac{x^k}{k!}$ in Ramanujan's sense of termwise identification of integrals and series.
As $a\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\frac{dx}{x(e^{ax}+e^{-ax})}\sim\sum_{k=0}^\infty(-k)^k a^{-k-1}$ (a formal divergent asymptotic series).

Let $0<p<1$. If $x^{p-1}F(x)$ and $x^{-p}f(x)$ are absolutely integrable on $(0,\infty)$ and $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty F(ax)f(bx)\,dx=\frac{1}{ab}$ for all $a,b>0$ with $a+b=1$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty x^{p-1}F(x)\,dx\int_0^\infty y^{-p}f(y)\,dy=\frac{\pi}{\sin(\pi p)}$.

Let $a,b,m,n,p>0$ with $a/m=(n-b)/n=p$. If $a>0$ and $0\le b<2$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\frac{\sinh(bx)\sin(ax)}{e^{2x}-1}\,dx=\int_0^\infty e^{-ax}\cot x\sin(bx)\,dx$.

Let $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty a(k)x^k$ and $\displaystyle\psi(x)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty b(k)x^k$ near $x=0$, with $\lim_{x\to0}\varphi(x)=\lim_{x\to0}\psi(x)=0$ and $a(0)=b(0)$. If $a,b>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\{\varphi(ax)-\psi(bx)\}\,\frac{dx}{x}=\int_0^\infty\{\varphi(x)-\psi(x)\}\,\frac{dx}{x}$.

Let $F(z)$ be analytic on $\Re z\ge0$ (except possibly $z=0$), with $F(z)=O(z^\alpha)$ as $z\to\infty$ for some $\alpha<1$ and $F(z)=O(z^\delta)$ as $z\to0$ for some $\delta>0$. For $\Re a>0$, define $\displaystyle\varphi(a)=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^\infty e^{-z/a}F(z)\,dz$. Then for $w>0$, $\displaystyle F(w)=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^\infty\frac{F(iw/u)+F(-iw/u)}{u^2+1}\,du$.

If $n>0$ and $a$ is a positive integer, then for each nonnegative integer $k$, $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\frac{\cos(nx)\,dx}{(2k+1)^2+x^2}=\frac{\pi}{2}\,\frac{\Gamma(1)\Gamma(a+\tfrac12)}{\Gamma(\tfrac12)\Gamma(a)\Gamma(a+1)}\,\frac{1}{(\tfrac12+k,a)}$.

If $\varphi(x)$ is continuous on $[0,\infty)$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}\varphi(x)=\varphi(\infty)$ exists, then $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to0}n\int_0^\infty x^{n-1}\varphi(x)\,dx=\varphi(0)-\varphi(\infty)$.

Let $\varphi(z)$ be analytic for $\Re z\le0$ and $n>0$. If contour integrals of $(-iz)^{n-1}\varphi(z)$ and $(iz)^{n-1}\varphi(z)$ over large rectangles in the left half-plane tend to $0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty x^{n-1}\{\varphi(ix)+\varphi(-ix)\}\,dx=2\cos(\pi n/2)\int_0^\infty x^{n-1}\varphi(x)\,dx$.

For $n\in\mathbb{C}\setminus[-1,0]$, $\displaystyle\Li_2\!\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)+\Li_2(-n)=\frac{\pi^2}{6}+\frac12\Log^2 n+\Log(1+n)\Log n$, with principal values of logarithms.

If $p,q,r,s$ are real with $p+qx\ne0$ and $r+sx\ne0$, then $\displaystyle\int\frac{\log(p+qx)}{r+sx}\,dx$ can be evaluated in terms of $\Li_2$ and elementary functions.

If $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty\varphi(t)t^{s-1}\,dt=\psi(s)$ converges absolutely on $\Re s=c$ and $\varphi(t)$ is a continuous function of bounded variation near $t=x>0$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-iT}^{c+iT}\psi(s)x^{-s}\,ds=\varphi(x)$ (Mellin inversion).

Under certain conditions, if $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^\infty x^{n-1}\varphi(x)\,dx=\psi(n)$, then formally $\displaystyle\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-i\infty}^{c+i\infty}e^{nz}\psi(z)\,dz=\varphi(\log n)$.

If $\varphi$ is absolutely integrable on $[0,R]$ for every $R>0$, $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty e^{-sx}\varphi(x)\,dx=\psi(s)$ converges absolutely for $\Re s=c$, and $\varphi(t)$ is of bounded variation near $t=x>0$, then $\displaystyle\varphi(x)=\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-iT}^{c+iT}e^{sx}\psi(s)\,ds$ (Laplace inversion).

If $\varphi$ is absolutely integrable on every finite interval, $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-sx}\varphi(x)\,dx=\psi(s)$ converges absolutely on $\Re s=c$, and $\varphi(t)$ is of bounded variation near $t=x$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-iT}^{c+iT}e^{sx}\psi(s)\,ds=\varphi(x)$ (bilateral Laplace inversion).
For $n>0$ and real $x$, Binet's formula gives $\log\Gamma(n+xi)+\log\Gamma(n-xi)=(n+\tfrac12)\log(n^2+x^2)-2nx\arctan(x/n)-n+x\arctan(x/n)+\log\Gamma(n+1)$.

As $n\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\log\prod_{m=1}^{n-1}\prod_{k=1}^{n-1}\left(1+\frac{6m+4k^2}{2m+2k-1}\right)=-\tfrac12\log2-\tfrac14\log2+n-\tfrac12 n\log n-2\log(2\pi n)+O(1)$ with a full Bernoulli-number correction series.

As $x\to\infty$, if $\sum_{k=1}^m A_k=\sum_{k=1}^n B_k$ and $\prod A_k=\prod B_k$, then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{\prod_{k=1}^m\Gamma(A_k x+a_k+1)}{\prod_{k=1}^n\Gamma(B_k x+b_k+1)}=(2\pi)^{(m-n)/2}x^{\sum a_k-\sum b_k}\,e^{\pi i(\sum a_k-\sum b_k)/2}$.

Let $A_k>0$, $B_k>0$, and $a_k$ be complex with $\prod_{k=1}^m A_k=\prod_{k=1}^n B_k$ and $\sum_{k=1}^m a_k=\sum_{k=1}^n b_k$. Then $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{\prod_{k=1}^m\Gamma(A_k x+a_k+1)}{\prod_{k=1}^n\Gamma(B_k x+b_k+1)}=(2\pi)^{(m-n)/2}x^{\sum a_k-\sum b_k}\,e^{\pi i(\sum a_k-\sum b_k)/2}$.

If $n$ is not an integer, then as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\frac{I_n(2x)-I_{-n}(2x)}{\sin(n\pi)}\sim\frac{e^{2x}}{\sin(n\pi)\sqrt{4\pi x}}\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{\Gamma(n+k+\tfrac12)\Gamma(n-k+\tfrac12)}{k!\,(2x)^k\,(n^2-(k-\tfrac12)^2)\cdots(n^2-(k-\tfrac32)^2)}$.
If $n$ is not an integer, $\displaystyle\frac{I_n(2x)-I_{-n}(2x)}{\sin(n\pi)}=-\frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^\pi e^{2x\cos\theta}\cos(n\theta)\,d\theta$.

Let $\varphi(x)$ be continuous on $[0,\infty)$, $p,a>0$, and $\Re(n)>0$.
