u-5)$.
If $\lambda(n)$ is the coefficient of $q^n$ in $q\,(q^{121};q^{121})_\infty/(q;q)_\infty$, then $\tau(n)-\lambda(n)\equiv 0\pmod{11}$.

If $n$ is odd, then $	au(2^{4\lambda-1}n)\equiv 0\pmod{11}$; if $3
mid n$, then $	au(3^{11\lambda-1}n)\equiv 0\pmod{11}$; if $5
mid n$, then $	au(5^{5\lambda-1}n)\equiv 0\pmod{11}$; if $7
mid n$, then $	au(7^{10\lambda-1}n)\equiv 0\pmod{11}$.
For every integer $n$, $\tau(2n)+24\,\tau(n)+2^{11}\,\tau\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right)=0$, with $\tau(x)=0$ when $x$ is not an integer.

For each prime $\varpi>3$, Ramanujan deduces congruences of the form $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}p\!\left(n\varpi+\varpi\left\lfloor\dfrac{\varpi}{24}\right\rfloor-\dfrac{\varpi^2-1}{24}\right)q^{n+\lfloor\varpi/24\rfloor}(q^\varpi;q^\varpi)_\infty=(Q^3-R^2)^{1+\lfloor\varpi/24\rfloor}\sum K_{\ell,m}Q^\ell R^m+\varpi J$, where the sum is over nonnegative $\ell,m$ with $4\ell+6m=\varpi-13-12\lfloor\varpi/24\rfloor$.

If $\lambda$ is an odd integer greater than $1$, then $p\!\left(\dfrac{5^\lambda n-5^{\lambda-1}-1}{24}\right),\,p\!\left(\dfrac{5^\lambda n-5^{\lambda+1}-1}{24}\right),\,p\!\left(\dfrac{5^\lambda n-49\cdot 5^{\lambda-1}-1}{24}\right)\equiv 0\pmod{5^\lambda}$; if $\lambda$ is a positive even integer, then $p\!\left(\dfrac{5^\lambda n-5^\lambda-1}{24}\right)\equiv 0\pmod{5^\lambda}$.
If $p(n)$ denotes the ordinary partition function, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p(5n+4)q^n = 5\frac{(q^5;q^5)_{\infty}^5}{(q;q)_{\infty}^6}$.
If $q_5(n)$, $n\ge 0$, is defined by (6.1.3), then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} q_5(5n)q^n = \frac{(q;q)_{\infty}^6}{(q^5;q^5)_{\infty}}$.
If $q_7(n)$, $n\ge 0$, is defined by (6.1.3), then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} q_7(7n)q^n = \frac{(q;q)_{\infty}^8}{(q^7;q^7)_{\infty}} + 49q\frac{(q;q)_{\infty}^4}{(q^7;q^7)_{\infty}^3}$.

Let $n$ be the least positive integer such that $24n-1$ is divisible by a positive integer $k$. Then $p(n+vk)-p(n)q(v)$ is divisible by $k$ for all positive integral values of $v$, where $(x;x)_{\infty}^{(24n-1)/k}=\displaystyle\sum_{\lambda=0}^{\infty} q(\lambda)x^{\lambda}$. Here $q(v)$ depends on $n$ and $k$.

For each positive integer $s$, define the coefficients $u_n=u_n(s)$ by $\displaystyle\frac{1}{(q;q)_{\infty}^{24s}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} u_n q^n = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} u_n(s)q^n$. Let $B_j$, $0\le j<\infty$, denote the $j$th Bernoulli number, and let $\sigma_k(n)=\sum_{d|n} d^k$. If $s$ is any positive integer, then $\dfrac{B_{12s+2}}{24s}+4u_s(s) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{s-1}\sigma_{12s+1}(k+1)u_k(s)$.

For any positive integer $s$, $\dfrac{B_{12s+2}}{24s}+4u_s(s) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{s-1}\sigma_{12s+1}(s-k)u_k(s)$, where $u_n(s)$ is defined in (6.4.2). Equivalently, if $[q^n]\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}a_k q^k$ denotes $a_n$ and $\Delta(\tau)=q(q;q)_{\infty}^{24}$, where $q=e^{2\pi i\tau}$, then $[q^s]E_{12s+2}(\tau)/(q;q)_{\infty}^{24s} = [q^0]E_{12s+2}(\tau)/\Delta^s(\tau)=0$.

Let $\delta$ denote any integer, and let $n$ denote a nonnegative integer. Suppose that $\varpi$ is a prime of the form $6\lambda-1$. Then $p_{\delta\varpi-4}\!\left(n\varpi-\dfrac{\varpi+1}{6}\right)\equiv 0 (\mathrm{mod}\,\varpi)$.
The coefficient of $x^n$ in $(1-2x+2x^4-2x^9+\cdots)^{-1}$ is nearly $\dfrac{1}{4n}\left(\dfrac{\cosh(\pi\sqrt{n})-\sinh(\pi\sqrt{n})}{\pi\sqrt{n}}\right)$.

If $c_n$, $n\ge 0$, is defined by $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n q^n = \frac{1}{\varphi(-q)}$, then $c_n=\dfrac{d}{dn}\frac{\cosh(\pi\sqrt{n})-1}{2\pi\sqrt{n}} + \sqrt{3}\cdot 2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2n\pi}{3}-\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \dfrac{d}{dn}\frac{\cosh(\frac{1}{3}\pi\sqrt{n})-1}{2\pi\sqrt{n}} + \cdots$.

If $c_n$, $n\ge 0$, is defined by $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n q^n = \frac{1}{(q;q^2)_{\infty}} = \frac{1}{(-q;q)_{\infty}}$, then $\sqrt{2}\,c_n=\dfrac{d}{dn}J_0\!\left(i\pi\sqrt{\frac{n+1}{24}}\right) + 2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2n\pi}{3}-\dfrac{\pi}{9}\right) \dfrac{d}{dn}J_0\!\left(\frac{1}{3}i\pi\sqrt{\frac{n+1}{24}}\right) + \cdots$, where $J_0(x)$ denotes the ordinary Bessel function of order $0$.

If $c_n$, $n\ge 0$, is defined by $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n q^n = (-q;q^2)_{\infty}$, then $c_n=\dfrac{d}{dn}J_0\!\left(i\pi\sqrt{\frac{n-1}{24}}\right) + 2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2n\pi}{3}-\dfrac{2\pi}{9}\right) \dfrac{d}{dn}J_0\!\left(\frac{1}{3}i\pi\sqrt{\frac{n-1}{24}}\right) + \cdots$.

If $c_n$, $n\ge 0$, is defined by $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n q^n = \frac{1}{(aq;q)_{\infty}}$, then $c_n\sim \dfrac{\sqrt{1-a}(kn)^{1/4}}{2n\sqrt{\pi}}e^{2\sqrt{kn}}$, where $k=\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{a^m}{m^2}=:\mathrm{Li}_2(a)$ and $\mathrm{Li}_2(a)$ is the dilogarithm.

For each positive integer $n$ and $|ab| < 1$, $\displaystyle\sum_{-n/2 < r \leq n/2} \left(\sum_{\substack{k=-\infty \\ k \equiv r \pmod{n}}}^{\infty} a^{k(k+1)/(2n)} b^{k(k-1)/(2n)}\right)^n = f(a,b)\, F_n(ab)$, where $F_n(q) := 1 + 2n q^{(n-1)/2} + \cdots$ for $n \geq 3$.
A positive integer $N$ is highly composite if $d(M)<d(N)$ for every integer $M<N$, where $d(n)$ denotes the number of divisors of $n$.
A positive integer $N$ is largely composite if $d(M)\le d(N)$ for every integer $M\le N$.
Let $Q_2(N)$ denote the number of representations of $N$ as $m^2+n^2$, counting $m^2+n^2$ in two ways when $m\ne n$ and in one way when $m=n$ or one of $m,n$ is zero.
If $N=2^{a_2}3^{a_3}5^{a_5}\cdots p^{a_p}$ with $a_\lambda\ge 0$ and some $a_3,a_7,a_{11},\ldots$ (primes $4n-1$) is odd, then $Q_2(N)=0$.
If every $a_3,a_7,a_{11},\ldots$ (primes $4n-1$) is even or zero, then $Q_2(N)=(1+a_5)(1+a_{13})(1+a_{17})\cdots$, where $5,13,17,\ldots$ are the primes of the form $4n+1$.
Without assuming the prime number theorem, the maximum order of $Q_2(N)$ is $2^{\log N}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{\log\log N}+O\!\left(\dfrac{1}{(\log\log N)^2}\right)\right\}$.
Assuming the prime number theorem, the maximum order of $Q_2(N)$ is $2^{\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{Li}(2\log N)+O(\log N\,e^{-a\sqrt{\log N})}}$ for some positive constant $a$.
For $N=2^{a_2}3^{a_3}\cdots p_n^{a_n}$ and $s>0$, Ramanujan gives $\sigma_{-s}(N)<\left\{1-(p_1p_2\cdots p_n N)^{-s/n}\right\}^n\prod_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{1-p_i^{-s}}$.

The indices $a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$ can be chosen so that $\sigma_{-s}(N)=\left\{1-(p_1p_2\cdots p_n N)^{-s/n}\right\}^n\prod_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{1-p_i^{-s}}\left\{1-O\!\left(N^{-s/n}(\log N)^{-2/(n-1)}\right)\right\}$.

A positive integer $N$ is a generalized highly composite number if $\sigma_{-s}(N)>\sigma_{-s}(N')$ for all $N'<N$; then $N=2^{a_2}3^{a_3}5^{a_5}\cdots p^{a_p}$ with $a_2\ge a_3\ge a_5\ge\cdots\ge a_p=1$, except that $N=36$ when $2^s+4^s+8^s>3^s+9^s$ and $N=4$ in all cases.

A positive integer $N$ is a generalized superior highly composite number if there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that $\sigma_{-s}(N)/N^\varepsilon\ge\sigma_{-s}(N')/(N')^\varepsilon$ for all $N'<N$ and $\sigma_{-s}(N)/N^\varepsilon>\sigma_{-s}(N')/(N')^\varepsilon$ for all $N'>N$.

Ramanujan gives $\Sigma_{-1}(N)=e^\gamma\!\left\{\log\log N-2(\sqrt{2}-1)\sqrt{\log N}+S_1(\log N)+O\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\log N}\,\log\log N}\right)\right\}$, where $\Sigma_{-1}(N)=\sigma_{-1}(N)/N$ and $S_1$ is a bounded function.

Let $F(x):=[x]$ if $x$ is not an integer and $F(x):=x-\tfrac{1}{2}$ if $x$ is an integer. If $0<\theta<1$ and $x>0$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}F\!\left(\dfrac{x}{n}\right)\sin(2\pi n\theta)=\dfrac{\pi x}{1/2-\theta}-\dfrac{1}{4}\cot(\pi\theta)+\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\dfrac{J_1\!\bigl(4\pi\sqrt{m(n+\theta)x}\bigr)}{\sqrt{m(n+\theta)}}-\dfrac{J_1\!\bigl(4\pi\sqrt{m(n+1-\theta)x}\bigr)}{\sqrt{m(n+1-\theta)}}\right)$.

Let $F(x):=[x]$ if $x$ is not an integer and $F(x):=x-	frac{1}{2}$ if $x$ is an integer, and let $I_
u(z):=-Y_
u(z)-\dfrac{2}{\pi}K_
u(z)$. If $x>0$ and $0<	heta<1$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}F\!\left(\dfrac{x}{n}ight)\cos(2\pi n	heta)=\dfrac{1}{4}-x\log(2\sin(\pi	heta))+\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\dfrac{I_1\! igl(4\pi\sqrt{m(n+	heta)x} igr)}{\sqrt{m(n+	heta)}}+\dfrac{I_1\! igl(4\pi\sqrt{m(n+1-	heta)x} igr)}{\sqrt{m(n+1-	heta)}}ight)$.

Let $\sigma_k(n):=\sum_{d\mid n}d^k$ and let $\zeta(s)$ denote the Riemann zeta function. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive with $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$ and $s$ is any complex number, then $\sqrt{\alpha}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-s}(n)\,n^{s/2}K_{s/2}(2n\alpha)-\sqrt{\beta}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-s}(n)\,n^{s/2}K_{s/2}(2n\beta)=\dfrac{1}{4\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{s}{2}\bigr)}\,\zeta(s)\bigl(\beta^{(1-s)/2}-\alpha^{(1-s)/2}\bigr)+\dfrac{1}{4\Gamma\!\bigl(-\tfrac{s}{2}\bigr)}\,\zeta(-s)\bigl(\beta^{(1+s)/2}-\alpha^{(1+s)/2}\bigr)$.

Let $\sigma_k(n):=\sum_{d\mid n}d^k$ and let $\zeta(s)$ denote the Riemann zeta function. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive with $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$ and $s$ is any complex number, then $\sqrt{\alpha}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-s}(n)\,n^{s/2}K_{s/2}(2n\alpha)-\sqrt{\beta}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-s}(n)\,n^{s/2}K_{s/2}(2n\beta)=\dfrac{1}{4\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{s}{2}\bigr)}\,\zeta(s)\bigl(\beta^{(1-s)/2}-\alpha^{(1-s)/2}\bigr)+\dfrac{1}{4\Gamma\!\bigl(-\tfrac{s}{2}\bigr)}\,\zeta(-s)\bigl(\beta^{(1+s)/2}-\alpha^{(1+s)/2}\bigr)$.

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be positive with $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1}(n)e^{-2n\alpha}-\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1}(n)e^{-2n\beta}=\dfrac{\beta-\alpha}{12}+\dfrac{1}{4}\log\dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$.

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be positive with $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, and let $\gamma$ denote Euler's constant. Then $\sqrt{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\gamma-\dfrac{1}{4}\log(4\beta)+\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}d(n)K_0(2n\alpha)\right)=\sqrt{\beta}\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\gamma-\dfrac{1}{4}\log(4\alpha)+\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}d(n)K_0(2n\beta)\right)$.

If $a>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{dx}{x(e^{2\pi x}-1)(e^{2\pi a/x}-1)}=2\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}d(n)K_0(4\pi\sqrt{an})=\dfrac{a}{\pi^2}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{d(n)\log(a/n)}{a^2-n^2}-\dfrac{1}{2}\gamma-\left(\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{1}{4\pi^2 a}\right)\log a-\dfrac{\log(2\pi)}{2\pi^2 a}$.

If $a>0$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1/2}(n)e^{-2\pi\sqrt{an}}=K\,\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sigma_{-1/2}(n)}{(n+a)(\sqrt{n}+\sqrt{a})}$ plus two unspecified trivial terms, where $K$ is an unspecified constant.

If $a>0$ and $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant, then $2\sqrt{a}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1}(n)\sqrt{n}\,K_1(4\pi\sqrt{an})=-\dfrac{a^2}{2\pi}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sigma_{-1}(n)}{n(n+a)}+\dfrac{a}{2\pi}\bigl((\log a+\gamma)\zeta(2)+\zeta'(2)\bigr)+\dfrac{1}{4\pi}(\log(2a\pi)+\gamma)+\dfrac{1}{48a\pi}$.

If $a>0$ and $k\ge0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{a^x x^k}{\Gamma(x+1)}\,dx+\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{e^{-ax}x^{k-1}}{\pi^2+\log^2 x}\left(\cos(\pi k)-\dfrac{1}{\pi}\sin(\pi k)\log x\right)dx=e^a$.

If $a>0$, $0\le\lambda<\epsilon$, and $1/\epsilon$ is a positive integer, then $\epsilon\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{a^{\lambda+n\epsilon}}{\Gamma(1+\lambda+n\epsilon)}=e^a-\dfrac{\epsilon}{\pi}\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-ax}x^{-\lambda-1}\dfrac{\sin(\pi(\lambda-\epsilon))-x^\epsilon\sin(\pi\lambda)}{2\cos(\pi\epsilon)-(x^\epsilon+x^{-\epsilon})}\,dx$.

If either $\operatorname{Re}(a+b)>\tfrac{3}{2}$, or $2(a-b)$ is an odd integer and $\operatorname{Re}(a+b)>1$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{dx}{\Gamma(a+x)\Gamma(a-x)\Gamma(b+x)\Gamma(b-x)}=\dfrac{\Gamma(2a+2b-3)}{2\Gamma(2a-1)\Gamma(2b-1)\{\Gamma(a+b-1)\}^2}$.
If $n>0$, then the principal value $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{\cos(nx)}{1-x^2}\,dx=\dfrac{\pi}{2\sin n}$.

Let $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, $\delta$, and $\xi$ be complex numbers with $\operatorname{Re}(\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta)>3$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\dfrac{\xi+2n}{\Gamma(\alpha+\xi+n)\Gamma(\beta-\xi-n)\Gamma(\gamma+\xi+n)\Gamma(\delta-\xi-n)}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(\beta+n)\Gamma(\gamma-n)\Gamma(\delta+n)}=\dfrac{\sin(\pi\xi)\,\Gamma(\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta-3)}{\pi\,\Gamma(\alpha+\gamma+\xi-1)\Gamma(\beta+\delta-\xi-1)}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta-1)\Gamma(\beta+\gamma-1)\Gamma(\gamma+\delta-1)\Gamma(\delta+\alpha-1)}$.

Define, for real $s$ and $\theta$ with $0<\theta<2\pi$ and complex $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, $\delta$ with $\operatorname{Re}(\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta)>4$, $\phi_s(\theta):=\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\dfrac{e^{(n+s)i\theta}}{\Gamma(\alpha+s+n)\Gamma(\beta-s-n)\Gamma(\gamma+s+n)\Gamma(\delta-s-n)}$. Then $\dfrac{d}{d\theta}\dfrac{\phi_s(\theta)}{\phi_t(\theta)}=i\sin\{\pi(s-t)\}\left(2\sin\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)^{\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta-4}\dfrac{e^{i(\pi-\theta)(\alpha-\beta+\gamma-\delta+2s+2t)/2}}{\pi\,\phi_t^2(\theta)\,\Gamma(\alpha+\beta-1)\Gamma(\beta+\gamma-1)\Gamma(\gamma+\delta-1)\Gamma(\delta+\alpha-1)}$.

Let $\phi(a,x):=\dfrac{1}{1+\dfrac{x}{a+1}\left(1+\dfrac{x}{a+3}\left(1+\dfrac{x}{a+5}\left(1+\cdots\right)\right)\right)}$. If $a+1>0$, $b+1>0$, and $s$ is not purely imaginary, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{\phi(a,x)\phi(b,x)}{1+s^2x^2}\,dx=\dfrac{2\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{1+a}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{1+b}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{1+a+b}{2}\bigr)}{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{a}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{b}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{a+b}{2}\bigr)}\times\dfrac{1}{a+b+1+\dfrac{1\cdot(a+1)(b+1)(a+b+1)s^2}{a+b+3+\dfrac{2(a+2)(b+2)(a+b+2)s^2}{a+b+5+\cdots}}}$.

If $s=1$, the continued fraction in Entry 5.1.3 equals $\dfrac{1}{a+b+1+\dfrac{1\cdot(a+1)(b+1)(a+b+1)}{a+b+3+\dfrac{2(a+2)(b+2)(a+b+2)}{a+b+5+\cdots}}}$=\dfrac{1}{a+b+1}\bigl(1-A_1+A_1A_2-A_1A_2A_3+\cdots\bigr)$, where $A_t:=\dfrac{(a+t)(b+t)-ab\cos^2(\pi t/2)}{(a+1+t)(b+1+t)-ab\cos^2(\pi t/2)}$.

Let $\varphi(\alpha,x):=\dfrac{1}{1+\bigl(\tfrac{x}{\alpha}\bigr)^2\left(1+\bigl(\tfrac{x}{\alpha+1}\bigr)^2\left(1+\bigl(\tfrac{x}{\alpha+2}\bigr)^2\left(1+\cdots\right)\right)\right)}$. If $\alpha>0$, $\beta>0$, and $s$ is not purely imaginary, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{\varphi(\alpha,x)\varphi(\beta,x)}{1+4s^2x^2}\,dx=\dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\alpha+\tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\!\bigl(\beta+\tfrac{1}{2}\bigr)\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)\Gamma(\alpha+\beta+\tfrac{1}{2})}\,\chi_1(s)$, where $\chi_1(s):=\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{2\cdot1\cdot(2\alpha)(2\beta)s^2}{2\alpha+2\beta+1}+\dfrac{2(2\alpha+1)(2\beta+1)(2\alpha+2\beta)s^2}{2\alpha+2\beta+3}+\dfrac{3(2\alpha+2)(2\beta+2)(2\alpha+2\beta+1)s^2}{2\alpha+2\beta+5}+\cdots$.
Let $p$, $q$, and $r$ be positive. Then $\displaystyle\int_0^1\left(\dfrac{x^{p-1}}{1-x}-\dfrac{r x^{q-1}}{1-x^r}\right)dx=\psi(q/r)-\psi(p)+\log r$.

Suppose $a$, $b$, and $c$ are positive with $b>1$. Then $\displaystyle\int_0^1\left(\dfrac{x^{c-1}}{1-x}-\dfrac{b x^{bc-1}}{1-x^b}\right)\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}x^{abk}\,dx=\psi\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b}+c\right)-\log\dfrac{a}{b}$.
If $a$, $b$, and $c$ are positive with $b>1$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^1\dfrac{x^{c-1}-x^{bc-1}}{\log x}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}x^{abk}\,dx=-\log\!\left(1+\dfrac{bc}{a}\right)$.

Let $\epsilon>0$ be given. If $a$ is any nonzero integer, then there exist infinitely many positive integers $N$ such that $Ne^{2/a}-[Ne^{2/a}]<\dfrac{(1+\epsilon)\log\log N}{|a|N\log N}$, and for all sufficiently large positive integers $N$, $Ne^{2/a}-[Ne^{2/a}]>\dfrac{(1-\epsilon)\log\log N}{|a|N\log N}$.

If $a$ is any odd integer and $\epsilon>0$ is given, then there exist infinitely many positive integers $N$ such that $1+[Ne^{2/a}]-Ne^{2/a}<\dfrac{(1+\epsilon)\log\log N}{4|a|N\log N}$, and for all positive integers $N$ sufficiently large, $1+[Ne^{2/a}]-Ne^{2/a}>\dfrac{(1-\epsilon)\log\log N}{4|a|N\log N}$.

$\varphi(x)$ is the number of numbers not exceeding $x$ whose number of prime divisors does not exceed $k$. Then $\varphi(x)\sim\dfrac{x}{\log x}\left\{1+\dfrac{\log\log x}{1!}+\dfrac{(\log\log x)^2}{2!}+\cdots+\dfrac{(\log\log x)^{[k]}}{[k]!}\right\}$.

Let $\varphi(x)$ denote the number of numbers of the form $2^{a_2}3^{a_3}5^{a_5}\cdots p^{a_p}$, $p\le x^\epsilon$, not exceeding $x$. For $\tfrac{1}{2}\le\epsilon\le1$, $\varphi(x)\sim x\left\{1-\displaystyle\int_{1}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}\right\}$; for $\tfrac{1}{3}\le\epsilon\le\tfrac{1}{2}$, $\varphi(x)\sim x\left\{1-\displaystyle\int_{1}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}+\displaystyle\int_{1/2}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_1}{u_1}\displaystyle\int_{1-u_1}^{u_1}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}\right\}$; for $\tfrac{1}{4}\le\epsilon\le\tfrac{1}{3}$, $\varphi(x)\sim x\left\{1-\displaystyle\int_{1}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}+\displaystyle\int_{1/2}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_1}{u_1}\displaystyle\int_{1-u_1}^{u_1}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}-\displaystyle\int_{1/3}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_2}{u_2}\displaystyle\int_{\frac{1}{2}(1-u_2)}^{u_2}\dfrac{du_1}{u_1}\displaystyle\int_{1-u_1}^{u_1}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}\right\}$; and for $\tfrac{1}{5}\le\epsilon\le\tfrac{1}{4}$, $\varphi(x)\sim x\left\{1-\displaystyle\int_{1}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}+\displaystyle\int_{1/2}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_1}{u_1}\displaystyle\int_{1-u_1}^{u_1}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}-\displaystyle\int_{1/3}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_2}{u_2}\displaystyle\int_{\frac{1}{2}(1-u_2)}^{u_2}\dfrac{du_1}{u_1}\displaystyle\int_{1-u_1}^{u_1}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}+\displaystyle\int_{1/4}^{1/\epsilon}\dfrac{du_3}{u_3}\displaystyle\int_{\frac{1}{3}(1-u_3)}^{u_3}\dfrac{du_2}{u_2}\displaystyle\int_{\frac{1}{2}(1-u_2)}^{u_2}\dfrac{du_1}{u_1}\displaystyle\int_{1-u_1}^{u_1}\dfrac{du_0}{u_0}\right\}$, and so on.

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=4\pi^3$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{e^{n^2\alpha}-1}=\dfrac{\pi^2}{6\alpha}+\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{\sqrt{\beta}}{4\pi}\left\{\zeta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)+\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\cos(\sqrt{n\beta})-\sin(\sqrt{n\beta})-e^{-\sqrt{n\beta}}}{\sqrt{n}\bigl(\cosh(\sqrt{n\beta})-\cos(\sqrt{n\beta})\bigr)}\right\}$.

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=4\pi^3$. If $\varphi(n)$ and $\psi(n)$, $n\ge1$, are defined by $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{x^{j^2}}{1-x^{j^2}}=\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\varphi(n)x^n$ and $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{jx^{j^2}}{1-x^{j^2}}=\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\psi(n)x^n$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\varphi(n)e^{-n\alpha}=\dfrac{\pi^2}{6\alpha}+\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{\sqrt{\beta}}{2\pi}\left\{\tfrac{1}{2}\zeta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)+\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\psi(n)}{\sqrt{n}}e^{-\sqrt{n\beta}}\bigl[\cos(\sqrt{n\beta})-\sin(\sqrt{n\beta})\bigr]\right\}$.

If $\dfrac{1+53x+9x^2}{1-82x-82x^2+x^3}=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_n x^n$, $\dfrac{2-26x-12x^2}{1-82x-82x^2+x^3}=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}b_n x^n$, and $\dfrac{2+8x-10x^2}{1-82x-82x^2+x^3}=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}c_n x^n$, then $a_n^3+b_n^3=c_n^3+(-1)^n$.

If $d(N!)$ is the number of divisors of $N!$, then $\dfrac{C\sqrt{N}}{\log N}(1-\epsilon)<d(N!)<\dfrac{C\sqrt{N}}{\log N}(1+\epsilon)$, where $C=\left(1+\dfrac{1}{1}\right)^{\!1+\frac{1}{2}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{3}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{4}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{5}\cdots\right)\right)\right)\!}$.

If $S(N)$ is the number of ways in which $N$ can be expressed as the sum of two squares, then the maximum order of $S(N)$ equals $\sqrt{\text{max.\ order of }d(N^2+aN+b)}\cdot e^{O(\log N)^{1/2+\epsilon}}$.

For each integer $k\ge2$, let $R_{k,2}(x):=\displaystyle\sum_{\substack{m^k+n^k\le x\\ m,n\ge0}}1$, where $m^k+n^k$ and $n^k+m^k$ are not counted as distinct. Then, as $x\to\infty$, for each fixed $\epsilon>0$, $R_{k,2}(x)=\dfrac{x^{2/k}}{4k}\,\dfrac{\{\Gamma(1/k)\}^2}{\Gamma(2/k)}+O\!\left(x^{1/k+\epsilon}\right)$.

Let $s$ be an integer greater than $1$, let $r_{2s}(n)$ denote the number of representations of the positive integer $n$ as a sum of $2s$ squares, and set $R(s):=\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{\cos(k\pi s)}{(2k+1)^s}$, $s>1$. Then, as $n\to\infty$, $r_{2s}(n)=\dfrac{\pi n^{s-1}}{(s-1)!}\,R(s)\sin(\pi n)\left\{\dfrac{1}{1^s}\sin(\pi n)+\dfrac{1}{2^s}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\pi n+\tfrac{1}{2}\pi s\right)+\dfrac{1}{3^s}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\pi n+\pi s\right)+\dfrac{1}{4^s}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\pi n+\tfrac{3}{2}\pi s\right)+\dfrac{1}{5^s}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{1}{5}\pi n+2\pi s\right)+\cdots\right\}$.

If $s\ge5$, then, as $n\to\infty$, $r_s(n)=\dfrac{(\pi n)^{\frac{1}{2}s}}{n\,\Gamma\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2s}\right)}\displaystyle\sum_{p,q}\left(\dfrac{e^{-2n\pi ip/q}}{q}\displaystyle\sum_{\lambda=0}^{q-1}e^{2\pi i\lambda^2 p/q}\right)^{\!s}+O\!\left(n^{\frac{1}{4}s}\right)$, where the outer sum is over all positive integers $p$ and $q$ with $(p,q)=1$ and $p<q$.

Let $s$ and $r$ be real numbers satisfying $s+\tfrac{1}{2}r<0$, $s+r<1$, and $s<1$. Define $S(s,r):=\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}k^s\sigma_r(k)$. Then, for $n$ sufficiently large, $S(s,r)\le\zeta(-s)\zeta(-s-r)+\dfrac{n^{s+1}}{s+1}\zeta(1-r)+\dfrac{n^{s+r+1}}{s+r+1}\zeta(r+1)+\dfrac{n^{s+\frac{1}{2}(r+1)}}{1-r^2}+\dfrac{n^s}{2}\zeta(-r)+\dfrac{n^{s+r}}{2}\zeta(r)$, provided that either $s>0$ or $s+r>0$. Furthermore, if $n$ is sufficiently large, $S(s,r)\ge\zeta(-s)\zeta(-s-r)+\dfrac{n^{s+1}}{s+1}\zeta(1-r)+\dfrac{n^{s+r+1}}{s+r+1}\zeta(r+1)-\dfrac{n^{s+\frac{1}{2}(r+1)}}{1-r^2}-\dfrac{n^s}{2}\zeta(-r)-\dfrac{n^{s+r}}{2}\zeta(r)+o(1)$.

For unspecified parameters $r$ and $s$ and $t=[\sqrt{n}]$, $n^\alpha\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}k^{s-\alpha}\sigma_r(k)-\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}k^s\sigma_r(k)=n^\alpha\zeta(\alpha-s)\zeta(\alpha-r-s)-\zeta(-s)\zeta(-r-s)+\dfrac{\alpha n^{1+s}}{(1+s)(1-\alpha+s)}\zeta(1-r)+\dfrac{\alpha n^{1+r+s}}{(1+r+s)(1-\alpha+r+s)}\zeta(1+r)-\dfrac{\alpha}{2}n^{s-1}\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{t}\left(\tfrac{1}{6}-\epsilon_m+\epsilon_m^2\right)\left(\dfrac{n^r}{m^{r+1}}+\dfrac{n}{m^{r-1}}\right)+O(\cdots)$, which lies between $n^\alpha\zeta(\alpha-s)\zeta(\alpha-r-s)-\zeta(-s)\zeta(-r-s)+\dfrac{\alpha n^{1+s}}{(1+s)(1-\alpha+s)}\zeta(1-r)+\dfrac{\alpha n^{1+r+s}}{(1+r+s)(1-\alpha+r+s)}\zeta(1+r)-\dfrac{\alpha}{12}n^{s-1}\zeta(-r-1)-\dfrac{\alpha}{12}n^{r+s-1}\zeta(r-1)-\dfrac{\alpha n^{s+r/2}}{3(4-r^2)}$ and $n^\alpha\zeta(\alpha-s)\zeta(\alpha-r-s)-\zeta(-s)\zeta(-r-s)+\dfrac{\alpha n^{1+s}}{(1+s)(1-\alpha+s)}\zeta(1-r)+\dfrac{\alpha n^{1+r+s}}{(1+r+s)(1-\alpha+r+s)}\zeta(1+r)-\dfrac{\alpha}{24}n^{s-1}\left\{\dfrac{5}{3(1-2r^{-1})}\zeta(1-r)-\zeta(-r-1)\right\}-\dfrac{\alpha}{24}n^{r+s-1}\left\{3\left(1-\dfrac{1}{2r+1}\right)\zeta(1+r)-\zeta(r-1)\right\}+\dfrac{\alpha n^{s+r/2}}{6(4-r^2)}$.

If $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant, then, as $x\to\infty$, $\displaystyle\sum_{n\le x}\dfrac{2\gamma+\log n}{\sqrt{n}}=2\sqrt{x}(\log x+2\gamma-2)+C+o(1)$, where $C=\zeta\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\!\left\{\tfrac{3}{2}\gamma-\tfrac{1}{4\pi}-\tfrac{1}{2}\log(8\pi)\right\}$.
Let $d'(n)=d(n)-2\gamma-\log n$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{d'(n)}{\sqrt{n}}=\zeta^2\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)-C$, where $C$ is defined as in Entry 9.7.1.

Let $C$ be defined as in Entry 9.7.1. If $w$ is any positive number except $2\sqrt{n}$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{2\sqrt{n}<w}d(n)\!\left(1-\dfrac{w}{\pi\sqrt{n}}\right)+\displaystyle\sum_{2\sqrt{n}>w}d(n)\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{w}{2\sqrt{n}}-\dfrac{w}{\pi\sqrt{n}}\right)=\displaystyle\sum_{2\sqrt{n}<w}d(n)-\dfrac{w}{\pi}\!\left\{\zeta^2\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)-C\right\}+\displaystyle\sum_{2\sqrt{n}>w}d'(n)\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{w}{2\sqrt{n}}-\dfrac{w}{\pi}\right)-\displaystyle\sum_{2\sqrt{n}<w}\dfrac{(2\gamma+\log n)w}{\pi\sqrt{n}}+\displaystyle\sum_{2\sqrt{n}>w}(2\gamma+\log n)\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\pi}\sin^{-1}\!\dfrac{w}{2\sqrt{n}}-\dfrac{w}{\pi\sqrt{n}}\right)$.

Let $\operatorname{Re}x\ge0$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{e^{-n^2\pi x}}{n^2}=\dfrac{\pi^2}{6}-\pi\sqrt{x}+\dfrac{1}{2}\pi x-2\pi^2 x^{3/2}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\int_0^{\infty}te^{-\pi(n+tx)^2/x}\,dt$, where the principal value of the square root is taken.

Let $\operatorname{Re}x\ge0$. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\cos(n^2\pi x)}{n^2}=\dfrac{\pi^2}{6}-\dfrac{\pi\sqrt{x}}{2}+2\pi^2 x^{3/2}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\int_0^{\infty}te^{-2n\pi t}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}-\dfrac{\pi n^2}{x}+\pi t^2 x\right)dt$ and $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sin(n^2\pi x)}{n^2}=\dfrac{\pi\sqrt{x}}{2}-\dfrac{1}{2\pi x}+2\pi^2 x^{3/2}\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\int_0^{\infty}te^{-2n\pi t}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}-\dfrac{\pi n^2}{x}+\pi t^2 x\right)dt$, where the principal value of the square root is taken.

Let $a$ be an even positive integer. Then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\cos(\pi n^2/a)}{n^2}=\dfrac{\pi^2}{6}-\dfrac{\pi^2}{\sqrt{a}\,a}\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{a}\dfrac{r}{a}\!\left(1-\dfrac{r}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\dfrac{\pi r^2}{a}\right)$, $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sin(\pi n^2/a)}{n^2}=-\dfrac{\pi^2}{\sqrt{a}\,a}\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{a}\dfrac{r}{a}\!\left(1-\dfrac{r}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\dfrac{\pi r^2}{a}\right)$, and $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\dfrac{\pi n^2}{a}\right)}{n^2}=\dfrac{\pi^2}{6\sqrt{2}}-\dfrac{\pi^2}{\sqrt{a}\,a}\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{a}\dfrac{r}{a}\!\left(1-\dfrac{r}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi r^2}{a}\right)$.

If $a$ is an even positive integer, then $\dfrac{4\pi^2}{a^{3/2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{8\pi}+\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{n\cos(\pi n^2/a)}{e^{2n\pi}-1}\right)-\dfrac{2^{3/2}\pi^2}{8\pi a}\left(\dfrac{1}{8\pi a}+\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{n}{e^{2n\pi a}-1}\right)=-\dfrac{\pi^2}{a^{5/2}}\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{a}r(a-r)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi r^2}{a}\right)$.

Let $p$ be the greatest prime not exceeding $x$, $\pi(x)$ the number of primes $\le x$, and $\vartheta(x)=\log 2+\log 3+\log 5+\cdots+\log p$. With $\pi(x)=n$, Ramanujan has $\pi(x)\log x-\vartheta(x)=\int_2^x \dfrac{\pi(t)}{t}\,dt$.

Without assuming the prime number theorem, if $p$ is the greatest prime not exceeding $x$ and $\pi(x)=n$, then $\log x=\log n+\log\log n+O(1)$, and hence $\log 2+\log 3+\log 5+\cdots+\log p=n\log n+n\log\log n+O(n)$.

Assuming the Riemann hypothesis, Ramanujan gives $\vartheta(x)=x-\sqrt{x}-\sum_\rho \dfrac{x^\rho}{\rho}+O(x^{1/3})$, where $\rho$ runs over the nontrivial zeros of the zeta function, and $\pi(x)=\mathrm{Li}\!\left(x-\sqrt{x}-\sum_\rho \dfrac{x^\rho}{\rho}+O\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{x}}{\log x}\right)\right)$ with $\mathrm{Li}(x)=\int_0^x \dfrac{dt}{\log t}$.

Under the Riemann hypothesis, with $\pi(x)=n$, Ramanujan claims $\limsup\dfrac{\vartheta(x)-\mathrm{Li}^{-1}(n)}{\sqrt{\log n/n}}=4+\gamma-\log(4\pi)$ and $\liminf\dfrac{\vartheta(x)-\mathrm{Li}^{-1}(n)}{\sqrt{\log n/n}}=\log(4\pi)-\gamma$.

If $\varphi'(t)$ is continuous between $2$ and $x$, then $\varphi(2)+\varphi(3)+\varphi(5)+\cdots+\varphi(p)=\pi(x)\varphi(x)-\int_2^x \varphi'(t)\pi(t)\,dt$, and $\varphi(2)\log 2+\varphi(3)\log 3+\cdots+\varphi(p)\log p=\vartheta(x)\varphi(x)-\int_2^x \varphi'(t)\vartheta(t)\,dt$.

For $s>1$, Ramanujan gives $\sum_{p\le x}\dfrac{\log p}{p^s-1}=-\dfrac{\zeta'(s)}{\zeta(s)}+\dfrac{x^{1-s}}{1-s}+O(x^{1-s}e^{-a\sqrt{\log x}})$; for $s=1$, $\sum_{p\le x}\dfrac{\log p}{p^s-1}=\log x-\gamma+O(e^{-a\sqrt{\log x}})$; and for $s<1$, $\prod_{p\le x}(1-p^{-s})=e^{\mathrm{Li}(x^{1-s})}+O(x^{1-s}e^{-a\sqrt{\log x}})$.

For monotonic continuous $\varphi$ with $\log|\varphi'(x)|=O(x)$, Ramanujan defines a continuous function $F(x)$ and deduces $\sum_{p\le x}\varphi(\vartheta(p))\log p=\int_0^{\vartheta(x)}\varphi(z)\,dz+\tfrac12\varphi(\vartheta(x))\log x-\tfrac12\int_1^x \dfrac{\varphi(\vartheta(z))}{z}\,dz+R(x)$, where $R(x)=O\!\left(\dfrac{1}{|\varphi'(\vartheta(x))|\,(\log x)^2}\right)$.

Ramanujan gives $\sum_{p\le x}e^{-s\vartheta(p)}\log p=\dfrac1s-\dfrac{\log x}{x^s-1}e^{-s\vartheta(x)}-\int_1^x \dfrac{1-z^s+sz^s\log z}{z(z^s-1)^2}\,e^{-s\vartheta(z)}\,dz$, and the generalization $\sum_{p\le x}e^{-s\vartheta(p)}f(p)=\dfrac{f(2)}{2^s-1}-\dfrac{f(x)}{x^s-1}e^{-s\vartheta(x)}+\int_2^x e^{-s\vartheta(z)}\!\left(\dfrac{f'(z)}{z^s-1}-\dfrac{sz^{s-1}f(z)}{(z^s-1)^2}\right)dz$.

Ramanujan obtains $2^{-s}\log 2+6^{-s}\log 3+30^{-s}\log 5+\cdots=\dfrac1s-\int_1^\infty e^{-s\vartheta(x)}\dfrac{1-x^s+sx^s\log x}{x(1-x^s)^2}\,dx$, and as $s\to 0$ claims $(1-2^{-s})(1-6^{-s})(1-30^{-s})(1-210^{-s})\cdots=\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\pi^2}{6s\log s}\right)+O\!\left(\dfrac{1}{s(\log s)^2}\right)$.

Letting $x\to\infty$ in the prime-sum formulas, Ramanujan gives $\varphi(\vartheta(2))\log 2+\varphi(\vartheta(3))\log 3+\cdots=\int_0^\infty \varphi(x)\,dx-\tfrac12\int_1^\infty \dfrac{\varphi(\vartheta(x))}{x}\,dx+R_\infty$, and $2^{-s}+6^{-s}+30^{-s}+\cdots=\dfrac{1}{2^s-1}-s\int_2^\infty \dfrac{x^{s-1}e^{-s\vartheta(x)}}{(x^s-1)^2}\,dx$.

Let $\chi(n)$ denote the nonprincipal primitive character of modulus $4$, i.e., $\chi(2n)=0$ and $\chi(2n+1)=(-1)^n$ for each nonnegative integer $n$. Let $d(n)$ denote the number of positive divisors of the positive integer $n$. If $x\neq in$ for each integer $n$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\chi(n)d(n)n}{n^2+x^2}=\frac{\pi}{4}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\chi(n)}{n}\operatorname{sech}\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{2n}\right)$.

Let $\chi$ be defined as in Entry $12.2.1$, and let $\sigma_k(n)$ be as in Entry $12.2.2$. Then for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$ and $\operatorname{Re}(s-r)>1$, $\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{\chi(m)}{m^s}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\chi(n)}{n^{s-r}}=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\chi(n)\sigma_r(n)}{n^s}$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, then $\dfrac{1}{2w}+\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{m\alpha\coth(m\alpha)}{w+m^2\alpha}+\frac{m\beta\coth(m\beta)}{w-m^2\beta}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}\cot(\sqrt{w\alpha})\coth(\sqrt{w\beta})$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, then $\alpha\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{m}{e^{2m\alpha}-1}+\beta\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{m}{e^{2m\beta}-1}=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{24}-\frac{1}{4}$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, and if $\sigma(m)=\sum_{d\mid m}d$, then $\alpha\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma(m)e^{-2m\alpha}+\beta\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma(m)e^{-2m\beta}=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{24}-\frac{1}{4}$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, and if $\sigma_k(m)=\sum_{d\mid m}d^k$, then $\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{m(e^{2m\alpha}-1)}-\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{m(e^{2m\beta}-1)}=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1}(m)e^{-2m\alpha}-\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1}(m)e^{-2m\beta}=\frac{1}{4}\log\frac{\alpha}{\beta}-\frac{\alpha+\beta}{12}$.

Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, and let $B_m$, $m\ge0$, denote the $m$th Bernoulli number. If $r$ is a positive integer with $r\ge2$, then $\alpha^r\!\left(\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{m^{2r-1}}{e^{2m\alpha}-1}-\frac{B_{2r}}{4r}\right)=(-\beta)^r\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{m^{2r-1}}{e^{2m\beta}-1}-\frac{B_{2r}}{4r}\right)$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, and if $r$ is a positive integer with $r\ge2$, then $\alpha^r\!\left(\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{2r-1}(m)e^{-2m\alpha}-\frac{B_{2r}}{4r}\right)=(-\beta)^r\!\left(\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{2r-1}(m)e^{-2m\beta}-\frac{B_{2r}}{4r}\right)$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, and if $r$ is a positive integer, then $(4\alpha)^{-r}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\zeta(2r+1)+\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{m^{2r+1}(e^{2m\alpha}-1)}\right)-(-4\beta)^{-r}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\zeta(2r+1)+\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{m^{2r+1}(e^{2m\beta}-1)}\right)=(4\alpha)^{-r}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\zeta(2r+1)+\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1-2r}(m)e^{-2m\alpha}\right)-(-4\beta)^{-r}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\zeta(2r+1)+\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sigma_{-1-2r}(m)e^{-2m\beta}\right)=-\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{r+1}\frac{(-1)^kB_{2k}B_{2r+2-2k}\alpha^{r+1-k}\beta^k}{(2k)!(2r+2-2k)!}$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2/4$, and if $w\neq-(2m+1)^2\alpha$, $-(2m+1)^2\beta$ for $0\le m<\infty$, then $\dfrac{\pi}{4}\sec(\sqrt{w\alpha})\operatorname{sech}(\sqrt{w\beta})=\displaystyle\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}(-1)^m\left(\frac{(2m+1)\alpha\operatorname{sech}((2m+1)\alpha)}{w+(2m+1)^2\alpha}-\frac{(2m+1)\beta\operatorname{sech}((2m+1)\beta)}{w-(2m+1)^2\beta}\right)$.

If $\alpha\beta=\pi^2/4$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers, and if $r$ is any positive integer, then $\alpha^r\displaystyle\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^m(2m+1)^{2r-1}}{\cosh((2m+1)\alpha)}+(-\beta)^r\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^m(2m+1)^{2r-1}}{\cosh((2m+1)\beta)}=0$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2/4$, and if $r$ is any positive integer, then $\alpha^r\displaystyle\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}(-1)^m\sigma_{2r-1}(m)e^{-(2m+1)\alpha}+(-\beta)^r\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}(-1)^m\sigma_{2r-1}(m)e^{-(2m+1)\beta}=0$.

If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=\pi^2/4$, if $r$ is any positive integer, and if $\chi$ denotes the nonprincipal primitive character of modulus $4$, as in Section $12.2$, then $2\alpha^{1-r}\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{\chi(m)m^{1-2r}}{\cosh(m\alpha)}+2(-\beta)^{1-r}\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{\chi(m)m^{1-2r}}{\cosh(m\beta)}=4\alpha^{1-r}\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\chi(m)\sigma_{1-2r}(m)e^{-m\alpha}+2(-\beta)^{1-r}\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\chi(m)\sigma_{1-2r}(m)e^{-m\beta}=\dfrac{\pi}{2}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}\frac{(-1)^kE_{2k}E_{2r-2-2k}}{(2k)!(2r-2-2k)!}\alpha^{r-1-k}\beta^k$, where $E_{2k}$ are the Euler numbers.

If $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\varphi(x)\sin(nx)\,dx=\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}\,\varphi(n)$ and $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\varphi(x)e^{-nx}\,dx=:\psi(n)$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\psi(x)\cos(nx)\,dx=\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}\,\psi(n)$. If $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\varphi(x)\cos(nx)\,dx=\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}\,\varphi(n)$ and $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\varphi(x)e^{-nx}\,dx=:\psi(n)$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\psi(x)\sin(nx)\,dx=\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}\,\psi(n)$.
For $n>0$, $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\left(\frac{1}{e^{2\pi x}-1}-\frac{1}{2\pi x}\right)\sin(2\pi nx)\,dx=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{e^{2\pi n}-1}-\frac{1}{2\pi n}\right)$.
For $n>0$, $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\left(\frac{1}{e^{2\pi x}-1}-\frac{1}{2\pi x}\right)e^{-2\pi nx}\,dx=\frac{1}{2\pi}(\log n-\psi(1+n))$, where $\psi$ denotes the digamma function.
If $n>0$, $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}(\psi(1+x)-\log x)\cos(2\pi nx)\,dx=\frac{1}{2}(\psi(1+n)-\log n)$, where $\psi$ denotes the digamma function.

Define $\varphi(x):=\psi(x)+\dfrac{1}{2x}-\log x$, where $\psi$ denotes the digamma function. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=1$, then $\sqrt{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{\gamma-\log(2\pi\alpha)}{2\alpha}+\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\varphi(n\alpha)\right)=\sqrt{\beta}\!\left(\dfrac{\gamma-\log(2\pi\beta)}{2\beta}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\varphi(n\beta)\right)=-\dfrac{1}{\pi^{3/2}}\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\left|\Xi\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2t}\right)\Gamma\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{4}+\dfrac{it}{4}\right)\right|^2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{t}{2}\log\alpha\right)}{1+t^2}\,dt$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant and $\Xi(t):=\xi\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}+it\right)$ with $\xi(s):=(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{s}{2}\right)\zeta(s)$.
For $w>0$, with $\varphi_w(t):=\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\cos(\pi tx)}{\cosh(\pi x)}e^{-\pi wx^2}\,dx$, $\varphi_w(t)=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{w}}e^{-\pi t^2/(4w)}\varphi_{1/w}(it/w)$.
If $n$ is any positive integer, then $\varphi_w(t)+(-1)^{n+1}\varphi_w(t+2ni)=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{w}}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(-1)^ke^{-\pi(t+(2k+1)i)^2/(4w)}$.
If $n$ is any positive integer, then $\psi_w(t)-\psi_w(t+2ni)=-\dfrac{i}{\sqrt{w}}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}e^{-\pi(t+(2k+1)i)^2/(4w)}$.

Let $m$ and $n$ denote any positive integers and set $s=t+2mw\pm2ni$. Then $\varphi_w(s)+(-1)^{(m+1)(n+1)}e^{-\pi m(s+t)/2}\varphi_w(t)=e^{-\pi s^2/(4w)}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}(-1)^ke^{\pi(s-(2k+1)w)^2/(4w)}+(-1)^{(m+1)(n+1)}\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{w}}e^{-\pi m(s+t)/2}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(-1)^ke^{-\pi(t\pm(2k+1)i)^2/(4w)}$.

Let $m$ and $n$ denote positive integers. If $s=2mw\pm2ni$, then $\dfrac{1}{2}-\psi_w(s)+(-1)^{mn+m+1}e^{-\pi m(s+t)/2}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}-\psi_w(t)\right)=e^{-\pi s^2/(4w)}\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m}(-1)^{j-1}e^{\pi(s-2jw)^2/(4w)}\pm(-1)^{mn+m+1}\dfrac{i}{\sqrt{w}}e^{-\pi m(s+t)/2}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}e^{-\pi(t\pm(2j+1)i)^2/(4w)}$.

Let $t=mw\pm ni$, where $m$ and $n$ are positive integers. If $m$ is odd and $n$ is odd, or if $m$ is even and $n$ is odd, or if $m$ is odd and $n$ is even, then $\varphi_w(t)=\dfrac{1}{2}e^{-\pi t^2/(4w)}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}(-1)^je^{\pi(t-(2j+1)w)^2/(4w)}+\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{w}}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}(-1)^je^{\pi(t\mp(2j+1)i)^2/(4w)}$.

Let $t=mw\pm ni$, where $m$ and $n$ are positive integers. If $m$ is odd and $n$ is odd, or if $m$ is even and $n$ is odd, or if $m$ is even and $n$ is even, then $\psi_w(t)=-\dfrac{1}{2}e^{-\pi t^2/(4w)}\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m}(-1)^{j-1}e^{\pi(t-2jw)^2/(4w)}\pm\dfrac{i}{2\sqrt{w}}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}e^{\pi(t\mp(2j+1)i)^2/(4w)}$.

If $n$ is any positive integer, then $F_w(t)-F_w(t+2ni)=-\dfrac{i}{\sqrt{w}}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{n}{}'e^{-\pi(t+2ji)^2/(4w)}$, where the prime on the summation indicates that the terms with $j=0$ and $j=n$ are multiplied by $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

If $n$ is a positive integer, then $F_w(t)-e^{\pi n(t+nw)}F_w(t+2nw)=-e^{-\pi t^2/(4w)}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{n}{}'e^{\pi(t+2jw)^2/(4w)}$, where the prime on the summation has the same meaning as in Entry $14.4.2$.

Let $s=t+2\eta_1mw+2\eta_2ni$, where $\eta_1^2=\eta_2^2=1$, and where $m$ and $n$ are positive integers. Then $F_w(s)+(-1)^{mn-1}e^{-\pi\eta_1m(s+t)/2}F_w(t)=\eta_1e^{-\pi s^2/(4w)}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{m}{}'e^{\pi(s-2j\eta_1w)^2/(4w)}+\eta_2(-1)^{mn}\dfrac{i}{\sqrt{w}}e^{-\pi\eta_1m(s+t)/2}\displaystyle\sum_{j=0}^{n}{}'e^{-\pi(t+2\eta_2ji)^2/(4w)}$, where the primes on the summation signs have the same meaning as in Entries $14.4.2$ and $14.4.3$.

Let $X_1(s)=\int_0^\infty x^{s-1}\chi_1(x)\,dx$ and $X_2(s)=\int_0^\infty x^{s-1}\chi_2(x)\,dx$. Ramanujan's main problem is to choose $\chi_1,\chi_2$ so that $X_1(s)X_2(1-s)=\lambda^2$ for a constant $\lambda$ independent of $s$.

Ramanujan claims that the integral equations $\int_0^\infty \varphi(x)\chi_1(nx)\,dx=\lambda\psi(n)$ and $\int_0^\infty \psi(x)\chi_2(nx)\,dx=\lambda\varphi(n)$ imply each other; equivalently, with $Z_1(s)=\int_0^\infty x^{s-1}\varphi(x)\,dx$ and $Z_2(s)=\int_0^\infty x^{s-1}\psi(x)\,dx$, $\dfrac{Z_1(s)}{Z_2(1-s)}=\dfrac{X_2(s)}{\lambda}=\dfrac{\lambda}{X_1(1-s)}$.

If $\chi_1(x)=\chi_2(x)=\cos x$ or $\chi_1(x)=\chi_2(x)=\sin x$, then $X_1(s)X_2(1-s)=\lambda^2=\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$. If $\chi_1(x)=\chi_2(x)=J_
u(x)/\sqrt{x}$ with $\Re
u>-1$, then $\lambda=1$. If $\chi_1(x)=\chi_2(x)=\dfrac{x^
u}{1+x^2}$ with $
u$ an integer, then $\lambda=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

Given $X_1(s)$ with $X_1(s)X_2(1-s)$ constant, Ramanujan inverts by $\chi_j(x)=\dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-i\infty}^{c+i\infty} x^{-s}X_j(s)\,ds$. For $X_1(s)=X_2(s)=\Gamma(s)\cos\!\dfrac{\pi s}{2}$ or $\Gamma(s)\sin\!\dfrac{\pi s}{2}$, $\chi_1(x)=\chi_2(x)=\cos x$ or $\sin x$, using $\dfrac{1}{2\pi i}\int_{c-i\infty}^{c+i\infty} x^{-s}\Gamma(s)\,ds=e^{-x}$.

Partitioning $0,1,\ldots,2g-1$ into $a_1,\ldots,a_g$ and $b_1,\ldots,b_g$, Ramanujan sets $X_1(s)=\Gamma(s)\prod_{r=1}^g \sin\!\dfrac{\pi(s+a_r)}{2g}$ and $X_2(s)=\Gamma(s)\prod_{r=1}^g \sin\!\dfrac{\pi(s-b_r+2g-1)}{2g}$, so that $X_1(s)X_2(1-s)=\dfrac{\pi}{2^{2g-1}}$.

Ramanujan counts solutions of class $g$ by $\omega_g=\sum_\delta \mu(\delta)\,\dfrac{(2g/\delta)!}{((g/\delta)!)^2}$, where $\delta$ runs over divisors of $g$ and $\mu$ is the Möbius function; for example $\omega_1=2$, $\omega_2=4$, $\omega_3=18$, $\omega_4=64$, $\omega_5=250$.

When $\chi_1(x)=\chi_2(x)=\chi(x)$ and $\varphi(x)=\psi(x)$ with $X(s)X(1-s)=\lambda^2$, Ramanujan deduces $\int_0^\infty \varphi(x)\chi(nx)\,dx=\lambda\varphi(n)$, so $\varphi$ is a self-reciprocal function with respect to the kernel $\chi$. For $g=2$ with $a_1=0$, $a_2=1$, he finds $\chi_1(x)=\chi_2(x)=e^{-x}-\cos x+\sin x$ with $\lambda=\sqrt{\pi}$.

Ramanujan defines $\varphi(\alpha,\beta)=\prod_{n=1}^\infty \left(1+\dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{n+\alpha}\right)^3$ and factors each factor as $\left(1+\dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{n+\alpha}\right)^3=\left(1+\dfrac{\alpha+2\beta}{n}\right)\!\left(1+\dfrac{2\alpha+\beta}{n}\right)\left(1+\dfrac{\alpha}{n}\right)^3\!\left(1+\dfrac{\beta}{n}\right)^3\left\{1-\left(\dfrac{(\alpha-\beta)+i(\alpha+\beta)/\sqrt{3}}{2n}\right)^2\right\}\left\{1-\left(\dfrac{(\alpha-\beta)-i(\alpha+\beta)/\sqrt{3}}{2n}\right)^2\right\}$.

Using Binet's formula for $\log\Gamma(\alpha)$, Ramanujan deduces $\tfrac12\log(2\pi\alpha)+\log\prod_{n=1}^\infty\left(1+\dfrac{\alpha}{n}\right)^3=\log\!\left(\dfrac{\cosh(\pi\alpha/\sqrt{3})-\cos\pi\alpha}{\pi\alpha}\right)-\dfrac{\pi\alpha}{\sqrt{3}}+2\int_0^\infty \dfrac{(\tan^{-1}(x/\alpha))^3}{e^{2\pi x}-1}\,dx$, so $\int_0^\infty \dfrac{(\tan^{-1} x)^3}{e^{2\pi\alpha x}-1}\,dx$ is finite for positive integers $\alpha$.

Ramanujan gives $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{(-1)^{n-1}n^2}{n^3+\alpha^3}=\dfrac13\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n+\alpha}+\dfrac43\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{(-1)^{n-1}(2n-\alpha)}{(2n-\alpha)^2+3\alpha^2}$, expressible in finite terms when $\alpha$ is an odd integer; for $\alpha=1$, $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{(-1)^{n-1}n^2}{n^3+1}=\dfrac13\left(1-\log 2+\pi\,\mathrm{sech}\!\dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$.

Ramanujan states $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{n}{n^4+4\alpha^4}=\dfrac{1}{4\alpha}\sum_{n=1}^{2\alpha}\dfrac{1}{(n-\alpha)^2+\alpha^2}$ when $2\alpha$ is a positive integer, and $\dfrac{1}{16\pi\alpha^4}+\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{n\coth(n\pi)}{n^4+4\alpha^4}=\dfrac{\pi}{8\alpha^2}\cdot\dfrac{\cosh(2\pi\alpha)+\cos(2\pi\alpha)}{\cosh(2\pi\alpha)-\cos(2\pi\alpha)}$.

For positive integer $\alpha$, Ramanujan gives $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{2n+1}{(2n+1)^4+4\alpha^4}=\dfrac{1}{4\alpha}\sum_{n=0}^{\alpha-1}\dfrac{1}{(2n+1-\alpha)^2+\alpha^2}$, and the example $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{1}{(n^2+(n+1)^2)(\cosh((2n+1)\pi)-\cosh\pi)}=\dfrac{1}{2\sinh\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\pi}+\coth\pi-\dfrac{\pi}{2}\tanh^2\!\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$.
Let $\Phi(x)=\int_0^x \dfrac{\tan^{-1}t}{t}\,dt$. For $x>0$, $\Phi(x)-\Phi(1/x)=\tfrac12\pi\log x$; for $-1\le x\le 1$, $\Phi(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{(-1)^n x^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)^2}$.

For $0<x<\tfrac12\pi$, Ramanujan gives $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{\sin((4n+2)x)}{(2n+1)^2}=\Phi(\tan x)-x\log(\tan x)$, $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{(-1)^n}{(4n+1)^2}=\Phi(\sqrt{2}-1)+\dfrac{\pi}{8}\log(1+\sqrt{2})+\dfrac{\pi^2}{16}$, and $\Phi(1)=\tfrac32\Phi(2-\sqrt{3})+\tfrac18\pi\log(2+\sqrt{3})$.

For $0<x<\tfrac12\pi$, Ramanujan has $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{(\tfrac12)_n}{n!}\,\dfrac{\cos^{2n+1}x+\sin^{2n+1}x}{(2n+1)^2}=\Phi(\tan x)+\tfrac12\pi\log(2\cos x)$, $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{\sin(nx)}{n^2\cos^n x}=\Phi(\tan x)+\tfrac12\pi\log(\cos x)-x\log(\sin x)$, and $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{\sin(nx)}{n^2}\!\left(1-\dfrac{1}{\alpha}\right)^n\!\cos^{-n}x+\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{\sin(n(x+\pi/2))}{n^2}(1-\alpha)^n\sin^n x=\Phi(\tan x)-\Phi(\alpha\tan x)+x\log\alpha$.

Ramanujan gives $\prod_{n=0}^\infty \left(1-\dfrac{x^2}{(2n+1)^2}\right)^{(-1)^n(2n+1)}=\dfrac{\pi}{8}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{4}{\pi}\Phi(1)\right)$, $\prod_{n=0}^\infty \left(1+\dfrac{x^2}{(2n+1)^2}\right)^{(-1)^n(2n+1)}=\exp\!\left(\dfrac{4}{\pi}\left\{\Phi(1)-\Phi(e^{-\pi x/2})-2x\tan^{-1}(e^{-\pi x/2})\right\}\right)$, and for $\alpha\beta=\pi^2$, $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{1}{(2n+1)^2(e^{(2n+1)\alpha}-1)}+\dfrac{\pi}{4\beta}\sum_{n=1}^\infty \dfrac{1}{n^2(e^{n\beta}+e^{-n\beta})}=\dfrac{\pi}{16}\!\left(\alpha^3+\beta^3\right)-\dfrac12\Phi(1)$.

From $\alpha=\beta=\pi$ in the residue formula, Ramanujan calculates $\Phi(1)\approx 0.9159655942$; for $0<x<\tfrac12\pi$, $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \dfrac{n!}{(\tfrac32)_n}\,\dfrac{\sin^{2n+1}x}{2n+1}=2\Phi(\tan\tfrac{x}{2})$.

Let $\chi_4(n)=(-1)^m$ if $n=2m+1$ and $\chi_4(n)=0$ if $n$ is even, and let $\chi_3(n)=\left(\dfrac{n}{3}\right)$ denote the Legendre symbol. Let $\zeta(s)$ denote the Riemann zeta function, and let $L(s,\chi_4)$ and $L(s,\chi_3)$ denote the Dirichlet $L$-functions associated with $\chi_4$ and $\chi_3$. Then $\dfrac{(1-2^{2/3})\zeta(1/3)}{(1-2^{1/3})\zeta(2/3)}=\dfrac{\pi^{1/3}}{\Gamma(1/3)}\dfrac{2^{1/3}+4^{1/3}}{\sqrt{3}}$, $\dfrac{L(1/3,\chi_4)}{L(2/3,\chi_4)}=\dfrac{\pi^{1/3}}{\Gamma(1/3)}\cdot 4^{1/3}$, $\dfrac{L(1/3,\chi_3)}{L(2/3,\chi_3)}=\dfrac{\pi^{1/3}}{\Gamma(1/3)}\cdot 2^{1/3}\cdot 3^{1/6}$, $\dfrac{(1-2^{3/4})\zeta(1/4)}{(1-2^{1/4})\zeta(3/4)}=\dfrac{\pi^{1/4}}{\Gamma(1/4)}\sqrt{3+2^{5/4}}$, $\dfrac{L(1/4,\chi_4)}{L(3/4,\chi_4)}=\dfrac{\pi^{1/4}}{\Gamma(1/4)}\sqrt{2+2\sqrt{2}}$, and $\dfrac{L(1/4,\chi_3)}{L(3/4,\chi_3)}=\dfrac{\pi^{1/4}}{\Gamma(1/4)}\sqrt{3+\sqrt{6}}$.

Let $\sigma_s(n)=\sum_{d\mid n}d^s$, and let $\zeta(s)$ denote the Riemann zeta function. Then $\Gamma\!\left(s+\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\left\{\dfrac{\zeta(1-s)}{s-\frac{1}{2}}x^{s-\frac{1}{2}}+\dfrac{\zeta(-s)}{2}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi s}{2}\right)x^{s+\frac{1}{2}}\right.+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sigma_s(n)}{2i}\left[(x-in)^{-s-\frac{1}{2}}-(x+in)^{-s-\frac{1}{2}}\right]\right\}=(2\pi)^s\left\{\dfrac{\zeta(1-s)}{2\sqrt{\pi x}}-\pi\sqrt{\pi x}\,\zeta(-s)\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi s}{2}\right)+\sqrt{\pi}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{\sigma_s(n)}{\sqrt{n}}e^{-2\pi\sqrt{2n}\,x}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+2\pi\sqrt{2n}\,x\right)\right\}$.

Let $\sigma_s(n)$ and $\zeta(s)$ be as in the preceding entry. If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive numbers such that $\alpha\beta=4\pi^2$, then $\alpha^{(s+1)/2}\left\{\dfrac{1}{\alpha}\zeta(1-s)+\dfrac{1}{2}\zeta(-s)\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi s}{2}\right)+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_s(n)\sin(n\alpha)\right\}=\beta^{(s+1)/2}\left\{\dfrac{1}{\beta}\zeta(1-s)+\dfrac{1}{2}\zeta(-s)\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi s}{2}\right)+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sigma_s(n)\sin(n\beta)\right\}$.

Let $n>0$ and let $t>0$. Then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{\sin(\pi t x)}{x\cosh(\pi x)}e^{-i\pi n x^2}\,dx=\dfrac{\pi}{2}-2\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^k e^{-(2k+1)\pi t/2+(2k+1)^2 i\pi n/4}}{2k+1}-\pi\sqrt{n}\,e^{-i\pi/4}\int_0^{\infty}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^k e^{(t+u+(2k+1)i)^2 i\pi/(4n)}\,du$.

For $x>0$, define $r>0$ and $0<\theta<\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ by $r\cos\theta=\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{e^{-xt}}{1+t^2}\,dt$ and $r\sin\theta=\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{te^{-xt}}{1+t^2}\,dt$. Then $r^2=\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{e^{-xt}}{t}\log(1+t^2)\,dt$.

For $x>0$, with $r$ and $\theta$ as in Entry 19.8.1, $\displaystyle\int_0^x\dfrac{\sin u}{u}\,du=\dfrac{\pi}{2}-r\cos(x-\theta)$ and $\displaystyle\int_0^x\dfrac{1-\cos u}{u}\,du=\gamma+\log x-r\sin(x-\theta)$, where $\gamma$ denotes Euler's constant.

Let $S(x):=\displaystyle\int_0^x\dfrac{\sin u}{u}\,du$ for $x>0$. Then $S(x)$ has local maxima at $x=(2n+1)\pi$, $n\ge 0$, with maximum values $S(2n+1)=\dfrac{\pi}{2}+\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{e^{-(2n+1)\pi t}}{1+t^2}\,dt$, while the local minima are at $x=2n\pi$, $n\ge 1$, with minimum values $S(2n)=\dfrac{\pi}{2}-\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}\dfrac{e^{-2n\pi t}}{1+t^2}\,dt$.

Let $0<k<1$, $K=K(k)$ the complete elliptic integral of the first kind, $q=\exp\!\left(-\pi K'/K\right)$ with $K'=K(k')$ and $k'=\sqrt{1-k^2}$, and for $0<\varphi\le\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ set $\theta=\dfrac{2K}{\pi}\displaystyle\int_0^{\varphi}\dfrac{dt}{\sqrt{1-k^2\sin^2 t}}$. Then $\log\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)+4\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^n q^{2n+1}\sin\{(2n+1)\theta\}}{(2n+1)(1-q^{2n+1})}=\log\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\dfrac{\varphi}{2}\right)$.

With the notation of Entry 19.10.1, also put $\theta'=\dfrac{2K}{\pi}\displaystyle\int_0^{\varphi}\dfrac{dt}{\sqrt{1-k'^2\sin^2 t}}$. Then $\theta'+2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^n\sinh(2n\theta')}{n(1+q^{2n})}=\log\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\dfrac{\varphi}{2}\right)$.

Suppose that $(x^6+ax)^5-(x^6+bx)^5=A(x^5+p)^5+B(x^5+q)^5+C(x^5+r)^5$. Furthermore, write $\dfrac{A}{1-pz}+\dfrac{B}{1-qz}+\dfrac{C}{1-rz}=\dfrac{\alpha+\beta z+\gamma z^2}{1+\delta z+\epsilon z^2+\varphi z^3}$. Then $\alpha=5(a-b)$, $\beta=-\dfrac{2a-b}{a+b}(4a^2+3ab+4b^2)$, $\gamma=2(a^3-b^3)$, $\delta=-\dfrac{2a^2+ab+b^2}{a+b}$, $\epsilon=a^2+ab+b^2$, and $\varphi=-\dfrac{a^4+6a^3b+6a^2b^2+6ab^3+b^4}{10(a+b)}$.

If $z=\dfrac{1}{N}$ and $N=\dfrac{1}{2}(a+b)+\dfrac{1}{2}(a-b)M$, and if $\delta$, $\epsilon$, and $\varphi$ are given in Entry 20.2.1, then $1+\delta z+\epsilon z^2+\varphi z^3=0$ is equivalent to $5(a+b)(M^3-M)=(a-b)(5M^2-1)$.

If $g^5=2$, then $\dfrac{\sqrt{g+3}+\sqrt{5g-5}}{\sqrt{g+3}-\sqrt{5g-5}}=g+g^2$ and $\dfrac{\sqrt{g^2+1+\sqrt{4g-3}}}{\sqrt{g^2+1-\sqrt{4g-3}}}=\dfrac{1}{5}\left(1+g^2+g^3+g^9\right)^2$. If $g^5=3$, then $\dfrac{\sqrt{g^2+1+\sqrt{5g-5}}}{\sqrt{g^2+1-\sqrt{5g-5}}}=\dfrac{1}{g+g+g^2+g^3}$. If $g^5=2$, then $\sqrt{1+g^2}=\dfrac{g^4+g^3+g-1}{\sqrt{5}}$ and $\sqrt{4g-3}=\dfrac{g^9+g^7-g^6-1}{\sqrt{5}}$. If $g^5=3$, then $\sqrt[3]{2-g^3}=\dfrac{1+g-g^2}{\sqrt[3]{5}}$. If $g^5=2$, then $\sqrt[5]{1+g+g^3}=\dfrac{\sqrt{1+g^2}}{\sqrt[10]{5}}$.

$\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{1}{3}+\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{5}{3}}}=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\sqrt[3]{5}-1}{2}-\dfrac{\sqrt[3]{5}}{\sqrt[3]{3}}}=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3+\sqrt[3]{5}}{\sqrt[3]{5}-1}}=\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\sqrt[3]{3}+\sqrt[3]{15}}{2}-\sqrt[3]{5}}$. If $a$, $b$, and $c$ are arbitrary numbers, then $\dfrac{\sqrt[3]{\sqrt[3]{(a+b)(a^2+b^2)-a}}}{\sqrt[3]{\sqrt[3]{(a+b)(a^2+b^2)-b}}}=\dfrac{\sqrt[3]{(a+b)^2-\sqrt[3]{a^2+b^2}}}{\sqrt[3]{(a+b)^2+\sqrt[3]{a^2+b^2}}}\,(a^2+ab+b^2)$, $\dfrac{(\sqrt{a^2+ab+b^2}-a)(\sqrt{a^2+ab+b^2}-b)}{a+b-\sqrt{a^2+ab+b^2}}=a+b$, and $\dfrac{-a+\sqrt[4]{(c+a)(a+b)}}{4}-\dfrac{b+\sqrt[4]{(a+b)(b+c)}}{4}-\dfrac{c+\sqrt[4]{(b+c)(c+a)}}{4}=\dfrac{2\sqrt{ab+bc+ca}}{\sqrt{a+b}+\sqrt{b+c}+\sqrt{c+a}}$.

Ramanujan claims $\sum_{k=1}^n d(k)=n(2\gamma-1+\log n)+O(\max d(n))$, and that $a_1+a_2+\cdots+a_n$ can be expressed in terms of $n$ with error $O(\max a_n-\min a_n)$, perhaps even with the maximum and minimum of the error term determined.

Ramanujan asserts that the sum of the first $n$ prime logarithms can be expressed in terms of $n$ with error $o(1)$ or even $O(n^{-1/2+\varepsilon})$, and defines the maximum order of $a_n$ as $\max_r(\text{average order of }a_r n)$; for example $\max d(n)=2^{\log n/\log\log n}(1+\varepsilon)$.

If $b_n$ is steadily increasing, $\max a_n>b_n$, and $\sum_{k=1}^\infty a_k e^{-kx}-\sum_{k=1}^\infty b_k e^{-kx}=O(1)$ as $x\to 0$, then Ramanujan claims $\sum_{k=1}^m a_k e^{-kx}-\sum_{k=1}^m b_k e^{-kx}=O(\max a_n-\min a_n)$, and if $a_n$ is also steadily increasing, then $a_n\sim b_n$.

Ramanujan states that if $a_n$ and $b_n$ are steadily increasing and $\sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n^{-s}$ and $\sum_{n=1}^\infty b_n^{-s}$ both converge for $s>k$ and both diverge for $s=k$ with difference $O(1)$ at $s=k$, then $a_n\sim b_n$; analogous results hold for $\sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n/n^s$.

Ramanujan claims that if $a_n$ and $b_n$ are steadily increasing and $\sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-a_n x}\sim\sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-b_n x}$ as $x\to 0$, then (conclusion missing in the manuscript) $a_n\sim b_n$.
The number of integers of the form $2^m3^n$ less than $x$ equals $\tfrac12\cdot\dfrac{\log(2x)}{\log 2}\cdot\dfrac{\log(3x)}{\log 3}+O(1)$.

The number of integers of the form $a^2+b^2$ less than $x$ equals $C\int^x \dfrac{dt}{\sqrt{\log t}}+O(x^{1/2+\varepsilon})$, where $C=\sqrt{2}\prod_{p\equiv 3\pmod 4}\!\left(1-\dfrac{1}{p^2}\right)^{-1/2}=\sqrt{2}\left(1+\dfrac{1}{7}\right)\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{11}\right)\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{19}\right)\cdots$.

The number of integers of the form $a^2b^3$ less than $x$ equals $\sqrt{4.723034}\,x^{1/2}-\sqrt[3]{3.10227}\,x^{1/3}+O(x^{1/6+\varepsilon})$ (Ramanujan's constants; the correct values are $\zeta(\tfrac32)\sqrt{x}+\zeta(\tfrac23)\sqrt[3]{x}+O(x^{1/5})$).

With $\phi(-q)$, $f_3(q)$, $\varphi_3(q)$, and $\psi_3(q)$ defined in (2.3.1), (2.1.1), (2.1.2), and (2.1.3), respectively, $2\varphi_3(-q)-f_3(q)=f_3(q)+4\psi_3(-q)=\dfrac{\phi^2(-q)}{(q;q)_\infty}$.
If $f_3(q)$ is defined by (2.1.1), $\phi(-q)$ by (2.3.1), and $\chi_3(q)$ by (2.1.4), then $\chi_3(q)=\dfrac{1}{4}f_3(q)+\dfrac{3}{4}\dfrac{\phi^2(-q^3)}{(q;q)_\infty}$.

If $f_3(q)$ is defined by (2.1.1) and $\phi(-q)$ by (2.3.1), then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{3n^2}}{(-q;q^3)_{n+1}(-q^2;q^3)_n}=\dfrac{1}{4}-\dfrac{1}{4}f_3(q^3)+\dfrac{1}{4}\dfrac{\phi^2(-q)}{(q^3;q^3)_\infty}$.

For $\omega_3(q)$ defined by (2.1.5) and $\psi(q)$ defined by (2.3.6), $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{6n^2}}{(q;q^6)_{n+1}(q^5;q^6)_n}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(1+q^2\omega_3(q^3)+\dfrac{\psi^2(q)}{(q^6;q^6)_\infty}\right)$.

If $f_3(q)$ is given by (2.1.1) and $\phi(-q)$ by (2.3.1), then $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^n(q;q)_{2n}q^{n^2}}{(q^6;q^6)_n}=\dfrac{3}{4}f_3(q^3)+\dfrac{1}{4}\dfrac{\phi^2(-q)}{(q^3;q^3)_\infty}$.
If $\nu_3(q)$ is given by (2.1.6), $\omega_3(q)$ by (2.1.5), and $\psi(q)$ by (2.3.6), then $\nu_3(-q)=q\,\omega_3(q^2)+\dfrac{\psi(q^2)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty}$.

Suppose that $a$ and $b$ are real numbers such that $a^2+b^2=4$, and let $f_a(q):=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{n^2}}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(1+aq^j+q^{2j})}$. Then $\dfrac{b-a+2}{4}f_a(-q)+\dfrac{b+a+2}{4}f_{-a}(-q)-\dfrac{b}{2}f_b(q)=\dfrac{(q^4;q^4)_\infty}{(-q;q^2)_\infty}\displaystyle\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{1-bq^n+q^{2n}}{1+(a^2b^2-2)q^{4n}+q^{8n}}$.

Let $a$ and $b$ be real numbers with $a^2+ab+b^2=3$, and let $f_a(q)$ be defined by (4.1.3). Then $(a+1)f_{-a}(q)+(b+1)f_{-b}(q)-(a+b-1)f_{a+b}(q)=\dfrac{3(q^3;q^3)_\infty^2}{(q;q)_\infty}\displaystyle\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{1+ab(a+b)q^{3n}+q^{6n}}$.

Let $f_a(q)$ and $\psi(q)$ be defined by (4.1.3) and (4.1.2), respectively. Then $\dfrac{1+\sqrt{3}}{2}f_{-1}(-q)+\dfrac{3+\sqrt{3}}{6}f_1(-q)=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{n^2}}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(1+\sqrt{3}q^j+q^{2j})}+\dfrac{2\sqrt{3}\,\psi(-q)(q^4;q^4)_\infty}{(q^6;q^6)_\infty}\displaystyle\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{1}{1+\sqrt{3}q^n+q^{2n}}$.

Let $\widetilde{\varphi}(q):=\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{n^2}}{(-q^2;q^2)_n}$ and let $\psi(q)$ be defined by (4.1.2). Then $\dfrac{1}{2}(1+e^{\pi i/4})\widetilde{\varphi}(iq)+\dfrac{1}{2}(1+e^{-\pi i/4})\widetilde{\varphi}(-iq)=f_{\sqrt{2}}(q)+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\dfrac{\psi(-q)(-q^2;q^4)_\infty}{\displaystyle\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}(1+\sqrt{2}q^n+q^{2n})}$.

Let $A(q):=\dfrac{G^2(q)(q^5;q^5)_\infty}{H(q)}$, where $G(q)$ and $H(q)$ are defined in (3.1.16) and (3.1.17), and let $\Phi(q):=-1+\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{5n^2}}{(q;q^5)_{n+1}(q^4;q^5)_n}$. Then $M_1(q):=\chi_0(q)-2-3\Phi(q)+A(q)=0$, $M_2(q):=F_0(q)-1-\Phi(q)+q\psi(q^5)H(q^2)=0$, $M_3(q):=\varphi_0(-q)+\Phi(q)-\dfrac{(q^5;q^5)_\infty G(q^2)H(q)}{H(q^2)}=0$, $M_4(q):=\psi_0(q)-\Phi(q^2)-qH(q)f(-q^9,-q)=0$, and $M_5(q):=f_0(q)+2\Phi(q^2)-\phi(-q^5)G(q)=0$.

If $\varphi_6(q)$ is defined by (7.1.1), then $\varphi_6(q)+2\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-q;q)_{2n-1}\,q^n}{(q;q^2)_n}=\dfrac{1}{(q;q)_\infty}\left(1+6\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(\dfrac{n}{3}\right)\dfrac{q^{2n}}{1-q^{2n}}\right)$, where $\left(\dfrac{n}{3}\right)$ denotes the Legendre symbol.

If $\varphi_{10}(q)$ and $\psi_{10}(q)$ are defined by (8.1.1) and (8.1.2), respectively, and $\omega$ is a primitive cube root of unity, then $q^{2/3}\varphi_{10}(q^3)-\dfrac{1}{\omega-\omega^2}\bigl(\psi_{10}(\omega q^{1/3})-\psi_{10}(\omega^2 q^{1/3})\bigr)=-\dfrac{q^{1/3}\,\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^n q^{n^2/3}\displaystyle\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^m q^{m(5m+3)/2}}}{(q;q^2)_\infty\,\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^n q^{n^2}}$.

Under the same hypotheses as Entry 8.4.1, $q^{-2/3}\psi_{10}(q^3)+\dfrac{1}{\omega-\omega^2}\bigl(\omega\varphi_{10}(\omega q^{1/3})-\omega^2\varphi_{10}(\omega^2 q^{1/3})\bigr)=\dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^n q^{n^2/3}\displaystyle\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^m q^{m(5m+1)/2}}}{(q;q^2)_\infty\,\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^n q^{n^2}}$.

If $X_{10}(q)$ and $\chi_{10}(q)$ are defined in (8.1.3) and (8.1.4), respectively, and $\omega$ is a primitive cube root of unity, then $X_{10}(q^3)-\dfrac{1}{\omega-\omega^2}\bigl(\omega\chi_{10}(\omega q^{1/3})-\omega^2\chi_{10}(\omega^2 q^{1/3})\bigr)=\dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}q^{n(n+1)/6}\displaystyle\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^m q^{m(5m+1)}}{(-q;q^2)_\infty\,\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}q^{n(n+1)}}$.

With the same hypotheses as Entry 8.4.3, $\chi_{10}(q^3)+\dfrac{q^{2/3}}{\omega-\omega^2}\bigl(X_{10}(\omega q^{1/3})-X_{10}(\omega^2 q^{1/3})\bigr)=-\dfrac{q\,\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}q^{n(n+1)/6}\displaystyle\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty}(-1)^m q^{m(5m+3)}}{(-q;q^2)_\infty\,\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}q^{n(n+1)/2}}$.

If $\varphi_{10}(q)$ and $\psi_{10}(q)$ are defined by (12.1.1) and $n>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-\pi n x^2}\cosh\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi x}{\sqrt{5}+1}+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{4}\right)\,dx+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\,e^{\pi/(5n)}\,\psi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})=\dfrac{\sqrt{5+\sqrt{5}}}{2}\,e^{-\pi n/5}\,\varphi_{10}(-e^{-\pi n})-\dfrac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2\sqrt{n}}\,e^{-\pi/(5n)}\,\varphi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})$.

If $\varphi_{10}(q)$ and $\psi_{10}(q)$ are defined by (12.1.1) and $n>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-\pi n x^2}\cosh\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi x}{\sqrt{5}+1}-\dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{4}\right)\,dx+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\,e^{\pi/(5n)}\,\psi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})=-\dfrac{\sqrt{5-\sqrt{5}}}{2}\,e^{\pi n/5}\,\psi_{10}(-e^{-\pi n})+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2\sqrt{n}}\,e^{-\pi/(5n)}\,\varphi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})$.

If $\varphi_{10}(q)$ and $\psi_{10}(q)$ are defined by (12.1.1) and $n>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-\pi n x^2}\cosh\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi x}{\sqrt{5}+1}+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{4}\right)\,dx+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\,e^{\pi/(5n)}\,\psi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})=\dfrac{\sqrt{5+\sqrt{5}}}{2}\,e^{-\pi n/5}\,\varphi_{10}(-e^{-\pi n})-\dfrac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2\sqrt{n}}\,e^{-\pi/(5n)}\,\varphi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})$.

If $\varphi_{10}(q)$ and $\psi_{10}(q)$ are defined by (12.1.1) and $n>0$, then $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty}e^{-\pi n x^2}\cosh\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi x}{\sqrt{5}+1}-\dfrac{\sqrt{5}}{4}\right)\,dx+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\,e^{\pi/(5n)}\,\psi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})=-\dfrac{\sqrt{5-\sqrt{5}}}{2}\,e^{\pi n/5}\,\psi_{10}(-e^{-\pi n})+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2\sqrt{n}}\,e^{-\pi/(5n)}\,\varphi_{10}(-e^{-\pi/n})$.

Ramanujan defines mock $\vartheta$-functions: unlike Rogers's false $\vartheta$-functions, mock $\vartheta$-functions enter mathematics as beautifully as ordinary $\vartheta$-functions, yet are not ordinary $\vartheta$-functions.
Ramanujan gives an Eulerian $\vartheta$-series of type (A): $1+\dfrac{q}{(1-q)^2}+\dfrac{q^4}{(1-q)^2(1-q^2)^2}+\dfrac{q^9}{(1-q)^2(1-q^2)^2(1-q^3)^2}+\cdots$.
Ramanujan gives an Eulerian $\vartheta$-series of type (B): $1+\dfrac{q}{1-q}+\dfrac{q^4}{(1-q)(1-q^2)}+\dfrac{q^9}{(1-q)(1-q^2)(1-q^3)}+\cdots$.
For series (A) with $q=e^{-t}$ and $t\to 0$, Ramanujan claims $(\mathrm{A})=\sqrt{\dfrac{t}{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\pi^2}{6t}-\dfrac{t}{24}\right)+o(1)$.
For series (B) with $q=e^{-t}$ and $t\to 0$, Ramanujan claims $(\mathrm{B})=\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\pi^2}{15t}-\dfrac{t}{60}\right)\sqrt{\dfrac{5-\sqrt{5}}{2}}+o(1)$.

Ramanujan states that near $q=1$ the function $\bigl\{(1-q)(1-q^2)(1-q^3)\cdots\bigr\}^{-120}$ is equivalent to the sum of five terms of the closed exponential form above, together with $O(1)$ instead of $o(1)$.

Ramanujan gives an unclosed Eulerian series (C): $1+\dfrac{q}{(1-q)^2}+\dfrac{q^3}{(1-q)^2(1-q^2)^2}+\dfrac{q^6}{(1-q)^2(1-q^2)^2(1-q^3)^2}+\cdots$. With $q=e^{-t}$ and $t\to 0$, $(\mathrm{C})=\sqrt{\dfrac{t}{2\sqrt{5}}}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\pi^2}{5t}+a_1 t+a_2 t^2+\cdots+O(a_k t^k)\right)$ where $a_1=\dfrac{1}{8\sqrt{5}}$.

Ramanujan defines a mock $\vartheta$-function: if a function in Eulerian form has exponential singularities at every $q=e^{2\pi i m/n}$ whose asymptotics close as neatly as in types (A) and (B), yet the function is not the sum of an ordinary $\vartheta$-function and a trivial $O(1)$ function at all such points, then it is a mock $\vartheta$-function.
Ramanujan defines the third-order mock theta function $f_3(q):=1+\dfrac{q}{(1+q)^2}+\dfrac{q^4}{(1+q)^2(1+q^2)^2}+\cdots$.

Ramanujan claims $f_3(q)+(1-q)(1-q^3)(1-q^5)\cdots(1-2q+2q^4-2q^9+\cdots)=O(1)$ at $q=-1,q^3=-1,q^5=-1,\ldots$, and $f_3(q)-(1-q)(1-q^3)(1-q^5)\cdots(1-2q+2q^4-\cdots)=O(1)$ at $q^2=-1,q^4=-1,\ldots$, while $f_3(q)=O(1)$ at $q=1,q^3=1,\ldots$; hence $f_3$ is a mock $\vartheta$-function.

Ramanujan claims the coefficient of $q^n$ in $f_3(q)$ equals $(-1)^{n-1}\exp\!\left(\pi\sqrt{\bigl(\dfrac{n}{6}-\dfrac{1}{144}\bigr)^2\bigl(n-\dfrac{1}{24}\bigr)}\right)+O\!\left(\exp\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\bigl(\dfrac{n}{6}-\dfrac{1}{144}\bigr)^2\bigl(n-\dfrac{1}{24}\bigr)\right)\right)$.

Ramanujan relates third-order mock functions by $2\varphi_3(-q)-f_3(q)=f_3(q)+4\psi_3(-q)=1-2q+2q^4-2q^9+\cdots$, $(1+q)(1+q^2)(1+q^3)\cdots$, and $4\chi_3(q)-f_3(q)=\dfrac{(1-2q^3+2q^{12}+\cdots)^2}{(1-q)(1-q^2)(1-q^3)\cdots}$.

Ramanujan gives fifth-order identities $\varphi_0(-q)+\chi_0(q)=2F_0(q)$, $f_0(-q)+2F_0(q^2)-2=\varphi_0(-q^2)+\psi_0(-q)=2\varphi_0(-q^2)-f_0(q)$=1-2q+2q^4-2q^9+\cdots$ over $(1-q)(1-q^4)(1-q^6)\cdots$, and $\psi_0(q)-F_0(q^2)+1=\dfrac{q}{1+q^2+q^6+q^{12}+\cdots}$ over $(1-q^8)(1-q^{12})(1-q^{28})\cdots$.
Ramanujan states that the fifth-order functions $f_1,\varphi_1,\psi_1,\chi_1,F_1$ have similar relations to those of $f_0,\varphi_0,\psi_0,\chi_0,F_0$.
Ramanujan states that the three seventh-order mock $\vartheta$-functions are not related to each other.

If $f(-q)=(q;q)_\infty$ as in (15.1.2) and $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n q^n=qf^3(-q)f^3(-q^7)$, then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n n^{-s}=\dfrac{1}{1+7^{1-s}}\prod_{p\equiv 3,5,6\,(7)}\dfrac{1}{1-p^{2(1-s)}}\prod_{q\equiv 1,2,4\,(7)}\dfrac{1}{1+2c_q q^{-s}+q^{2(1-s)}}$, where $c_q=2$ if $q=2$ and $c_q=7v^2-u^2$ if $q=u^2+7v^2$.

With $a_n$ defined by $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n q^n=qf^3(-q)f^3(-q^7)$, Ramanujan gives $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n n^{-s}=\dfrac{1}{1+7^{1-s}}\prod_{p\equiv 3,5,6\,(7)}\dfrac{1}{1-p^{2(1-s)}}\prod_{p\equiv 1,2,4\,(7)}\dfrac{1}{1+C_p p^{-s}+p^{2(1-s)}}$, where $C_p=2p-a^2$ with $4p=a^2+7b^2$.

If $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}q(n)q^n=qf^4(-q)f^4(-q^5)$, then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}q(n)n^{-s}=\dfrac{1}{1+5^{1-s}}\prod_{p\neq 5}\dfrac{1}{1-q(p)p^{-s}+p^{3-2s}}$. Ramanujan records $q(2)=-4$, $q(3)=2$, $q(5)=-5$, $q(7)=6$, and $q(p)^2<4p^3$ in general.

If $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\varphi(n)q^n=qf^4(-q^6)R(q^6)$ with $R(q)$ as in (15.1.5), then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\varphi(n)n^{-s}=\prod_{p>3}\dfrac{1}{1-\varphi(p)p^{-s}+p^{7-2s}}$, where $\varphi(p)=0$ if $p\equiv -1\,(6)$ and $\varphi(p)=(A+iB\sqrt{3})^7+(A-iB\sqrt{3})^7$ if $p\equiv 1\,(6)$ with $p=A^2+3B^2$.

Ramanujan conjectures analogous Euler products for $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n n^{-s}$ whenever $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n q^n$ is any of $q^5f^{10}(-q^{12})$, $q^7f^{14}(-q^{12})$, $q^5f^{20}(-q^6)$, or $q^{11}f^{22}(-q^{12})$.

With $F(q):=qf^{16}(-q^3)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}A_n q^n$ and $F_1(q):=qf^8(-q^3)Q(q^3)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_n q^n$, Ramanujan gives $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(a_n+6A_n\sqrt{n})n^{-s}=\dfrac{1-6\sqrt{10}\cdot 2^{-s}+2^{7-2s}}\dfrac{1+96\sqrt{10}\cdot 5^{-s}+5^{7-2s}}\dfrac{1-260\cdot 7^{-s}+7^{7-2s}}\dfrac{1+1920\sqrt{10}\cdot 11^{-s}+11^{7-2s}}\cdots$ and $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(a_n-6A_n\sqrt{n})n^{-s}=\dfrac{1+6\sqrt{10}\cdot 2^{-s}+2^{7-2s}}\dfrac{1-96\sqrt{10}\cdot 5^{-s}+5^{7-2s}}\dfrac{1-260\cdot 7^{-s}+7^{7-2s}}\dfrac{1-1920\sqrt{10}\cdot 11^{-s}+11^{7-2s}}\cdots$.

For primes $p=6k+1$ with $p=A^2+3B^2$ and $A\equiv 1\,(3)$, Ramanujan defines $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_0(n)q^{n/6}=q^{1/6}(q;q)_\infty^4$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_1(n)q^{n/6}=q^{1/6}(q;q)_\infty^4 P(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_2(n)q^{n/6}=q^{1/6}(q;q)_\infty^4 Q(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_3(n)q^{n/6}=q^{1/6}(q;q)_\infty^4 R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_4'(n)q^{n/6}=q^{1/6}(q;q)_\infty^4 Q^2(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_4''(n)q^{n/6}=q^{5/6}(q;q)_{20}^\infty$, $\Omega_4(n)=\Omega_4'(n)+288\omega\sqrt{70}\,\Omega_4''(n)$ with $\omega^2=1$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5(n)q^{n/6}=q^{1/6}(q;q)_\infty^4 Q(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7'(n)q^{n/6}=q^{1/6}(q;q)_\infty^4 Q^2(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7''(n)q^{n/6}=q^{5/6}(q;q)_{20}^\infty R(q)$, and $\Omega_7(n)=\Omega_7'(n)+10080\omega\sqrt{286}\,\Omega_7''(n)$ with $\omega^2=1$. In each case $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_\lambda(n)n^{-s}=\prod_{p>3}\dfrac{1}{1-\Omega_\lambda(p)p^{-s}+p^{2\lambda+1-2s}}$, with $\Omega_\lambda(p)=0$ for $p\equiv -1\,(6)$ and $\Omega_\lambda(p)=(A+iB\sqrt{3})^{2\lambda+1}+(A-iB\sqrt{3})^{2\lambda+1}$ for $p\equiv 1\,(6)$ when $\lambda=0,2,3,5$; also $\Omega_1(n)=n\Omega_0(n)$.

Ramanujan (List II) defines $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_0(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_\infty^8$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_1(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_\infty^8 P(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_2'(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_\infty^8 Q(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_2''(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_{16}^\infty$, $\Omega_2(n)=\Omega_2'(n)+6\omega\sqrt{10}\,\Omega_2''(n)$ with $\omega^2=1$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_3(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_\infty^8 R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_4'(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_\infty^8 Q^2(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_4''(n)q^{n/3}=q^{2/3}(q;q)_{16}^\infty Q(q)$, $\Omega_4(n)=\Omega_4'(n)+6\omega\sqrt{70}\,\Omega_4''(n)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5'(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_\infty^8 Q(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5''(n)q^{n/3}=q^{2/3}(q;q)_{16}^\infty R(q)$, $\Omega_5(n)=\Omega_5'(n)+12\omega\sqrt{55}\,\Omega_5''(n)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7'(n)q^{n/3}=q^{1/3}(q;q)_\infty^8 Q^2(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7''(n)q^{n/3}=q^{2/3}(q;q)_{16}^\infty Q(q)R(q)$, and $\Omega_7(n)=\Omega_7'(n)+12\omega\sqrt{910}\,\Omega_7''(n)$. Each satisfies $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_\lambda(n)n^{-s}=\prod_{p\neq 3}\dfrac{1}{1-\Omega_\lambda(p)p^{-s}+p^{2\lambda+3-2s}}$ with $\Omega_\lambda(p)=0$ for $p\equiv -1\,(3)$ and $\Omega_\lambda(p)=(A+iB\sqrt{3})^{2\lambda+3}+(A-iB\sqrt{3})^{2\lambda+3}$ for $p\equiv 1\,(3)$ when $\lambda=0$ or $3$; also $\Omega_1(n)=n\Omega_0(n)$.

For $p=4k+1$ with $p=A^2+4B^2$, Ramanujan (List III) defines $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_0(n)q^{n/4}=q^{1/4}(q;q)_\infty^6$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_1(n)q^{n/4}=q^{1/4}(q;q)_\infty^6 P(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_2(n)q^{n/4}=q^{1/4}(q;q)_\infty^6 Q(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_3'(n)q^{n/4}=q^{1/4}(q;q)_\infty^6 R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_3''(n)q^{n/4}=q^{3/4}(q;q)_{18}^\infty$, $\Omega_3(n)=\Omega_3'(n)+24\omega\sqrt{35}\,\Omega_3''(n)$ with $\omega^2=-1$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_4(n)q^{n/4}=q^{1/4}(q;q)_\infty^6 Q^2(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5'(n)q^{n/4}=q^{1/4}(q;q)_\infty^6 Q(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5''(n)q^{n/4}=q^{3/4}(q;q)_{18}^\infty Q(q)$, $\Omega_5(n)=\Omega_5'(n)+24\omega\sqrt{1155}\,\Omega_5''(n)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7'(n)q^{n/4}=q^{1/4}(q;q)_\infty^6 Q^2(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7''(n)q^{n/4}=q^{3/4}(q;q)_{18}^\infty Q^2(q)$, and $\Omega_7(n)=\Omega_7'(n)+120\omega\sqrt{3003}\,\Omega_7''(n)$ with $\omega^2=-1$. Each satisfies $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_\lambda(n)n^{-s}=\prod_{p\equiv 3\,(4)}\dfrac{1}{1-\Omega_\lambda(p)p^{-s}-p^{2\lambda+2-2s}}\prod_{p\equiv 1\,(4)}\dfrac{1}{1-\Omega_\lambda(p)p^{-s}+p^{2\lambda+2-2s}}$ with $\Omega_\lambda(p)=0$ for $p\equiv -1\,(4)$ and $\Omega_\lambda(p)=(A+2iB)^{2\lambda+2}+(A-2iB)^{2\lambda+2}$ for $p\equiv 1\,(4)$ when $\lambda=0,2,4$; also $\Omega_1(n)=n\Omega_0(n)$.

Ramanujan (List IV) defines $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_0(n)q^{n/12}=q^{1/12}(q;q)_\infty^2$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_1(n)q^{n/12}=q^{1/12}(q;q)_\infty^2 P(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_2'(n)q^{n/12}=q^{1/12}(q;q)_\infty^2 Q(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_2''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{5/12}(q;q)_{10}^\infty$, $\Omega_2(n)=\Omega_2'(n)+48\omega\,\Omega_2''(n)$ with $\omega^2=1$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_3'(n)q^{n/12}=q^{1/12}(q;q)_\infty^2 R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_3''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{7/12}(q;q)_{14}^\infty$, $\Omega_3(n)=\Omega_3'(n)+360\omega\sqrt{3}\,\Omega_3''(n)$ with $\omega^2=-1$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_4'(n)q^{n/12}=q^{1/12}(q;q)_\infty^2 Q^2(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_4''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{5/12}(q;q)_{10}^\infty Q(q)$, $\Omega_4(n)=\Omega_4'(n)+672\omega\,\Omega_4''(n)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5'(n)q^{n/12}=q^{1/12}(q;q)_\infty^2 Q(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{5/12}(q;q)_{10}^\infty R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5'''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{7/12}(q;q)_{14}^\infty Q(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_5''''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{11/12}(q;q)_{22}^\infty$, $\Omega_5(n)=\Omega_5'(n)+96\omega_1\sqrt{1045}\,\Omega_5''(n)+216\omega_2\sqrt{7315}\,\Omega_5'''(n)+103680\omega_1\omega_2\sqrt{7}\,\Omega_5''''(n)$ with $\omega_1^2=1$, $\omega_2^2=-1$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7'(n)q^{n/12}=q^{1/12}(q;q)_\infty^2 Q^2(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{5/12}(q;q)_{10}^\infty Q(q)R(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7'''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{7/12}(q;q)_{14}^\infty Q^2(q)$, $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\Omega_7''''(n)q^{n/12}=q^{11/12}(q;q)_{22}^\infty Q(q)$, and $\Omega_7(n)=\Omega_7'(n)+48\omega_1\sqrt{910\cdot 2911}\,\Omega_7''(n)+216\omega_2\sqrt{5005\cdot 2911}\,\Omega_7'''(n)-471744\omega_1\omega_2\sqrt{22}\,\Omega_7''''(n)$ with $\omega_1^2=1$, $\omega_2^2=-1$.

With $Q(q):=q\dfrac{f^5(-q^5)}{f(-q)}$ and $F(q)=R(q)$ the Rogers–Ramanujan continued fraction, Ramanujan gives $\dfrac{Q(q^{1/5})}{Q(q)}=\dfrac{F(q)+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}}{F(q)^5}\Big/\dfrac{F(q^{1/5})+\dfrac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}}{F(q^{1/5})^5}$.
If $x$ is a primitive $n$th root of unity with $n\equiv 2,3\,(5)$, then $\dfrac{1+x}{1+x^2}\dfrac{1+x^3}{1+x^4}\cdots\dfrac{1+x^{n-1}}{1+x^n}=0$.
If $x$ is a primitive $n$th root of unity with $n\equiv 1,4\,(5)$, then $\dfrac{1+x}{1+x^2}\dfrac{1+x^3}{1+x^4}\cdots\dfrac{1+x^{n-2}}{1+x^{n-1}}=0$.
If $x$ is a primitive $n$th root of unity with $n\equiv 0\,(5)$, then $\dfrac{1+x}{1+x^2}\cdots\dfrac{1+x^{n-2}}{1+x^{n-1}}=0$ and $\dfrac{1+x}{1+x^2}\cdots\dfrac{1+x^{n-1}}{1+x^n}=0$.

With $t:=R(e^{-\pi\sqrt{2}})$ and $t':=R(e^{-2\pi\sqrt{2}})$, Ramanujan gives $\dfrac{1}{t}-t=\dfrac{1+\xi}{\sqrt{5}}$ and $\dfrac{1}{t'}-t'=\dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{\xi}$, where $\dfrac{\xi^2}{1+\xi}\dfrac{1}{1-\xi}=\dfrac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}$.

Let $\zeta_5$ be a primitive fifth root of unity and $f_5(q):=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{n^2}}{(\zeta_5 q;q)_n(\zeta_5^{-1}q;q)_n}$. With $G(q):=\dfrac{1}{(q;q^5)_\infty(q^4;q^5)_\infty}$, $H(q):=\dfrac{1}{(q^2;q^5)_\infty(q^3;q^5)_\infty}$, $A(q):=(q^5;q^5)_\infty\dfrac{G^2(q)}{H(q)}$, $B(q):=(q^5;q^5)_\infty G(q)$, $C(q):=(q^5;q^5)_\infty H(q)$, $D(q):=(q^5;q^5)_\infty\dfrac{H^2(q)}{G(q)}$, $\Phi(q):=-1+\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{5n^2}}{(q;q^5)_{n+1}(q^4;q^5)_n}$, and $\dfrac{\Psi(q)}{q}:=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{q^{5n^2-1}}{(q^2;q^5)_{n+1}(q^3;q^5)_n}$, Ramanujan gives $f_5(q)=A(q^5)+(\zeta_5+\zeta_5^{-1}-2)\Phi(q^5)+qB(q^5)+(\zeta_5+\zeta_5^{-1})q^2 C(q^5)-(\zeta_5+\zeta_5^{-1})q^3\!\left(D(q^5)-(\zeta_5^2+\zeta_5^{-2}-2)\dfrac{\Psi(q^5)}{q^5}\right)$.

Let $F(x)=[x]$ if $x$ is not an integer and $F(x)=x-\tfrac{1}{2}$ if $x$ is an integer. If $0<\theta<1$ and $x>0$, then Ramanujan gives $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}F\!\left(\dfrac{x}{n}\right)\sin(2\pi n\theta)=\dfrac{\pi x}{\tfrac{1}{2}-\theta}-\dfrac{1}{4}\cot(\pi\theta)+\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left(\dfrac{J_1\!\bigl(4\pi\sqrt{m(n+\theta)x}\bigr)}{\sqrt{m(n+\theta)}}-\dfrac{J_1\!\bigl(4\pi\sqrt{m(n+1-\theta)x}\bigr)}{\sqrt{m(n+1-\theta)}}\right)$.

For $|q|<1$ and complex $a,b,\lambda$, define $G(a,b,\lambda):=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-\lambda/a;q)_n a^n q^{n(n+1)/2}}{(q;q)_n(-bq;q)_n}$. Ramanujan gives $\dfrac{G(aq,b,\lambda q)}{G(a,b,\lambda)}=\cfrac{1}{1+\dfrac{aq+\lambda q}{1+\dfrac{bq+\lambda q^2}{1+\dfrac{aq^2+\lambda q^3}{1+\dfrac{bq^2+\lambda q^4}{1+\cdots}}}}$.
